topic: Production-grade Tauri 2 packaging & distribution for Windows .exe (2026): bundling targets, code signing, updater, WebView2 distribution, reproducibility, enterprise installer UX — and what a harness scaffolds vs CI-gates

recommendations.practice: Default the Windows bundle target to NSIS only (bundle.targets: ["nsis"]) with bundle.windows.nsis.installMode: "perMachine"; treat MSI as an opt-in secondary target reserved for IT that requires GPO software-installation (which only supports MSI).
recommendations.rationale: Tauri's own docs recommend the NSIS installer as the newer default and say 99% of apps should ship one format, not both (dual formats caused duplicate-install bugs, fixed in tauri#12365). Tauri's MSI uses WiX v3, whose light.exe requires the VBSCRIPT optional Windows feature — VBScript is deprecated and may be disabled on Windows 11 24H2+ build machines (tauri#13752). Tauri MSI is also per-machine-only (perUser InstallScope is an open feature request, tauri#13792), while NSIS supports currentUser/perMachine/both plus installer hooks and cross-compilation. perMachine matters on managed university machines: AppLocker/WDAC policies commonly block executables in user-writable %LOCALAPPDATA%, where NSIS's default currentUser mode installs.
recommendations.tooling: tauri-cli 2.x (pin exact version in package.json), tauri.conf.json keys: bundle.targets, bundle.windows.nsis.installMode, bundle.windows.nsis.installerHooks (src-tauri/windows/hooks.nsh) for NSIS_HOOK_PREINSTALL/POSTINSTALL customization; WiX v3 only if MSI is required (tauri-bundler now also supports bundle.windows.wix.version pinning).
recommendations.enforcement: Harness scaffolds a tauri.conf.json contract test (ajv/vitest in packages/eval or apps/desktop) asserting targets === ["nsis"] and installMode === "perMachine"; a PreToolUse write-guard hook (pretool-write-guard.mjs pattern) rejects edits to tauri.conf.json that add "msi"/"all" targets or flip installMode without an explicit override marker file; CI gate lists target/release/bundle/ output and fails on unexpected artifact types.

recommendations.practice: Sign every binary and the installer with Azure Artifact Signing (formerly 'Trusted Signing', renamed at GA in Jan 2026) via Tauri's bundle.windows.signCommand — do not buy an EV certificate for SmartScreen purposes.
recommendations.rationale: Microsoft removed EV certificates' instant-SmartScreen-reputation behavior (confirmed in the May 2026 revision of the SmartScreen docs: 'EV certificates no longer bypass SmartScreen… paying a premium for EV solely to avoid SmartScreen warnings is no longer justified'). Artifact Signing is Microsoft's recommended non-Store signing path: ~$9.99/mo Basic (5,000 signatures), CI-friendly (no hardware token), daily-renewed 24-hour certificates, available to orgs in US/Canada/EU/UK. signCommand is the right hook because Tauri invokes it per file (%1), signing the app .exe before it is packed into the NSIS installer, then the installer itself — post-build signing scripts miss the inner exe. Reputation still accrues per publisher identity, so keep one consistent certificate/publisher name across releases.
recommendations.tooling: bundle.windows.signCommand: "trusted-signing-cli -e https://wus2.codesigning.azure.net -a <account> -c <profile> %1" (crate: trusted-signing-cli; Tauri docs now also reference it as artifact-signing-cli after the rename — pin the exact cargo-installed version); env AZURE_CLIENT_ID / AZURE_TENANT_ID / AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET (or GitHub OIDC federated credential); legacy certificateThumbprint/digestAlgorithm/timestampUrl keys only for local OV certs; relic + Azure Key Vault as the fallback path.
recommendations.enforcement: CI release gate on a windows runner runs `signtool verify /pa /all <artifact>` against every .exe/-setup.exe and fails on unsigned or wrong-subject output, and greps for a timestamp countersignature; the workflow marks signing secrets as required so unsigned 'release' builds cannot be produced; a repo-level grep lint forbids committing .pfx files or WINDOWS_CERTIFICATE base64 blobs (extend the harness hygiene credential patterns).

recommendations.practice: For SmartScreen inside the university, don't chase public reputation — deploy through managed channels and pre-clear the binary with IT.
recommendations.rationale: Per Microsoft's SmartScreen docs, even validly signed new files warn until hash/publisher reputation accumulates (weeks, hundreds of installs). But enterprise-managed environments differ: files distributed from Trusted Intranet locations aren't subject to SmartScreen review, IT can submit the file to the Microsoft Security Intelligence portal to accelerate trust, and Intune/SCCM-pushed per-machine installs sidestep the download-reputation flow entirely. Windows 11 Smart App Control additionally blocks unsigned executables regardless of download source, so signing remains mandatory even on-prem.
recommendations.tooling: Microsoft Security Intelligence file-submission portal (https://www.microsoft.com/wdsi/filesubmission); Intune Win32 app packaging (IntuneWinAppUtil, .intunewin wrapping the NSIS -setup.exe with /S install command) or SCCM; GPO only if MSI is produced.
recommendations.enforcement: Harness scaffolds a DEPLOYMENT.md runbook plus a release checklist encoded as a CI job: the release workflow publishes SHA-256SUMS and a signed artifact inventory that IT can allow-list/submit; CI asserts the publisher/subject name in the Authenticode signature matches a pinned expected value so reputation never resets due to an accidental certificate identity change.

recommendations.practice: Set bundle.windows.webviewInstallMode.type: "offlineInstaller" (embeds the ~127MB Evergreen Standalone Installer) as the scaffold default; document fixedRuntime (~180MB, xcopy-deployed, app-private) only as an escape hatch when IT blocks Edge Update endpoints and accepts the patching burden.
recommendations.rationale: Tauri's default downloadBootstrapper needs live access to Microsoft's CDN at install time — a silent failure mode inside a firewalled university network. Microsoft recommends Evergreen for security (auto-patched runtime, hard-linked with Edge, shared across apps) and explicitly positions the Standalone Installer for offline environments; Win11 ships the runtime preinstalled but Microsoft still advises bundling for edge cases. Fixed Version gives version pinning but 'can't be installed by using an installer', is never auto-updated (you re-ship Chromium security patches yourself), and adds ~180MB per app. If IT firewalls Edge Update, runtime updates can instead flow through WSUS/Microsoft Update per Microsoft's WebView2 enterprise-management docs — coordinate rather than pin.
recommendations.tooling: tauri.conf.json bundle.windows.webviewInstallMode ({"type":"offlineInstaller"}; fixedRuntime requires downloading the .cab from Microsoft's WebView2 page, extracting, and setting path); WebView2 Evergreen Standalone Installer; WSUS/Edge Update group policies for runtime servicing.
recommendations.enforcement: Config contract test fails if webviewInstallMode is absent or left at downloadBootstrapper (explicit choice required); CI records installer size and enforces a size budget (flags accidental fixedRuntime ~180MB jump or a silent fallback to 0MB bootstrapper); if fixedRuntime is chosen, a scheduled CI job diffs the pinned WebView2 version against Microsoft's latest-fixed-version feed and fails when the pin is older than N releases (mechanical patch-lag alarm).

recommendations.practice: Ship two build flavors: default 'managed' build with the updater plugin compiled out (IT deploys new versions via Intune/SCCM), and an opt-in 'self-updating' flavor pointed exclusively at an on-prem HTTPS endpoint serving a static latest.json.
recommendations.rationale: In managed environments auto-update fights the endpoint-management tooling (perMachine installs need elevation to update; updater 'quiet' mode requires admin) and violates change control. Where self-update is wanted, Tauri's updater is safe on-prem: it enforces minisign (Ed25519) signature verification that cannot be disabled, supports static-JSON endpoints on any internal server with {{target}}/{{arch}}/{{current_version}} templating, enforces TLS in production, and Windows update install modes (plugins.updater.windows.installMode: passive|basicUi|quiet) are configurable. Zero external cloud dependency: no GitHub, no CrabNebula needed.
recommendations.tooling: tauri-plugin-updater 2.x; `tauri signer generate -w` for the minisign keypair; bundle.createUpdaterArtifacts: true; TAURI_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY(_PASSWORD) env vars at build time; capabilities: updater:default only in the self-updating flavor; static latest.json {version, platforms: {"windows-x86_64": {url, signature}}} on the on-prem server.
recommendations.enforcement: Pre-write hook blocks committing minisign private keys (*.key, TAURI_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY literals) — extend harness hygiene patterns; CI release gate verifies each updater artifact's .sig against the pubkey embedded in tauri.conf.json before latest.json is published (minisign -V), so a key mismatch can never ship; grep lint hard-fails on dangerousInsecureTransportProtocol anywhere in the repo; a capabilities lint asserts updater permissions are absent from the managed flavor; CI validates latest.json against a JSON schema and that url hosts match the internal allowlist.

recommendations.practice: Encode enterprise installer UX as executable tests: silent install/uninstall round-trip on a clean runner using NSIS /S (and /D=dir), correct Program Files placement, Add/Remove Programs registration, and app launch.
recommendations.rationale: Silent, deterministic installs are the contract with university IT: NSIS supports /S and /D=path; MSI equivalents are msiexec /i app.msi /quiet /norestart (or /passive). perMachine NSIS requires elevation — the test must run elevated exactly as SCCM/Intune SYSTEM context would. Testing the uninstaller (Uninstall.exe /S) catches orphaned registry keys and files that make IT reject a package. This converts 'installer UX best practice' from documentation into a regression suite.
recommendations.tooling: GitHub Actions windows-latest (runs elevated by default) or a self-hosted Windows runner inside the network; PowerShell assertions (Test-Path 'C:/Program Files/<app>', registry HKLM:\...\Uninstall entry, exit codes); IntuneWinAppUtil packaging step if IT uses Intune.
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate 'installer-smoke' job: build → sign → run '<app>-setup.exe /S' → assert exit 0, binary present, uninstall key present → launch 'app.exe --version' → 'Uninstall.exe /S' → assert clean removal; job is required for merge to release branches; the harness Stop gate includes this job's local dry-run equivalent (config contract tests) in the validate chain so an agent cannot end a turn with a broken bundle config.

recommendations.practice: Make builds deterministic-by-pinning and provenance-attested rather than chasing bit-for-bit reproducibility of the signed artifact: pin rust-toolchain.toml, commit Cargo.lock and build with --locked, pin pnpm via packageManager + --frozen-lockfile, pin exact tauri-cli, and set SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH from the release commit.
recommendations.rationale: Authenticode signatures plus RFC3161 timestamps make the final signed installer non-bit-identical by design, so the achievable target is: identical pre-signature binaries plus cryptographic provenance for the signed one. NSIS ≥3.06.1 honors SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH for reproducible installer generation; Rust builds are reproducible when the toolchain, lockfile, and paths are pinned. GitHub Artifact Attestations bind the artifact digest to SLSA build provenance via Sigstore, letting IT verify the .exe came from your repo's release workflow (note: private repos require GitHub Enterprise Cloud; attest-build-provenance is now a wrapper over actions/attest — new workflows should use actions/attest).
recommendations.tooling: rust-toolchain.toml (pinned channel+components), cargo build --locked, pnpm packageManager field, exact-pinned tauri-cli/@tauri-apps/cli, SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH=$(git log -1 --format=%ct), actions/attest (or attest-build-provenance@v4) on the -setup.exe + updater artifacts, gh attestation verify, sha256sum manifest, cargo-auditable for embedded SBOM, Renovate for pin freshness.
recommendations.enforcement: CI runs a double-build determinism job (same SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH, two runners) and diffs the unsigned bundle hashes, failing on drift; all installs in CI use --locked/--frozen-lockfile so lockfile drift is a hard failure; a post-release job runs `gh attestation verify <artifact> -R <repo>` and blocks the release on failure; the harness pretool-bash-guard blocks `cargo update`/`pnpm up` in agent sessions without an explicit task marker.

recommendations.practice: Protect installer identity keys behind a change-control gate: bundle.identifier, publisher, NSIS installMode, the Authenticode subject, and the updater pubkey must not drift between releases.
recommendations.rationale: These values ARE the app's identity to Windows: changing identifier or install mode causes side-by-side duplicate installs (the class of bug fixed in tauri#12365); changing the signing certificate identity resets SmartScreen publisher reputation; changing the minisign pubkey orphans the installed fleet (Tauri warns: lose the key and 'you will NOT be able to publish new updates'). Agent-driven codebases are especially prone to well-meaning config 'cleanups' here.
recommendations.tooling: tauri.conf.json (identifier, bundle.publisher), a checked-in identity.lock.json snapshot of {identifier, publisher, installMode, updaterPubkey, expectedSigningSubject}; signtool for subject extraction.
recommendations.enforcement: PreToolUse write-guard hook diffs any tauri.conf.json edit against identity.lock.json and refuses writes that change locked fields unless an override file is present in the same change; CI re-derives the same comparison (defense in depth against non-agent edits) and additionally extracts the certificate subject from the signed artifact with signtool and compares to expectedSigningSubject; commitlint scope rule requires a 'release-identity' scoped commit to touch identity.lock.json.

recommendations.practice: Scaffold the full Windows release pipeline as one hardened GitHub Actions workflow: tag-triggered, least-privilege permissions, signing via OIDC-federated Azure credentials, artifacts + checksums + attestation + latest.json published atomically.
recommendations.rationale: tauri-action (tauri-apps/tauri-action) handles build+bundle+release upload, but signing steps, signature verification, installer smoke tests, and attestation are where regressions actually happen (e.g., tauri#13991: trusted-signing-cli started failing mid-bundle on a non-PE file after a version bump, breaking releases). A scaffolded, pinned, hardened workflow makes the release path reviewable and reproducible instead of tribal knowledge; on-prem constraint means the only external egress in the whole pipeline is Azure signing + (optionally) the WebView2 offline installer download, both of which can be mirrored.
recommendations.tooling: tauri-apps/tauri-action pinned by commit SHA; permissions: {contents: write, id-token: write, attestations: write} only; azure/login with federated OIDC (no long-lived client secrets); actions pinned by SHA (enforced by an actionlint/pinning check); concurrency group on the release workflow; runner: windows-latest or self-hosted inside the network.
recommendations.enforcement: CI gates in order, each required: (1) config contract tests, (2) build --locked, (3) sign, (4) signtool verify all artifacts, (5) installer silent-install smoke test, (6) determinism spot-check, (7) attest + checksums, (8) latest.json signature validation — release job publishes only if all pass; zizmor/actionlint job lints the workflows themselves (SHA-pinning, no pull_request_target foot-guns); branch protection requires the pipeline on release/* branches.

pitfalls: Tauri's own Windows signing docs still describe EV certificates as giving 'immediate reputation with Microsoft SmartScreen' — this is outdated: Microsoft removed the EV SmartScreen bypass (circa 2024; restated in the May 2026 SmartScreen doc revision). Budgeting for an EV token/HSM to fix SmartScreen is wasted money and blocks CI signing.

MSI builds silently depend on the VBSCRIPT optional Windows feature (WiX v3 light.exe). VBScript is deprecated and may be disabled by default on newer Windows 11 CI images — 'targets: all' or 'msi' then fails with cryptic light.exe errors (tauri#13752). If you must ship MSI, gate CI on a runner with VBScript explicitly enabled.

Install-scope mismatch: Tauri MSI is per-machine only (perUser is an open request, tauri#13792) while NSIS defaults to currentUser (%LOCALAPPDATA%). On AppLocker/WDAC-managed university machines, per-user installs into user-writable directories are frequently blocked from executing at all — an installer that 'works' in dev and is dead on arrival in the deployment environment.

The default webviewInstallMode (downloadBootstrapper) phones Microsoft's CDN at install time; on a firewalled network the install fails late and unclearly. Conversely, fixedRuntime freezes a Chromium engine you must re-ship for every security patch (~180MB, no auto-update, 'can't be installed by using an installer' — it's xcopy-deployed). Both extremes are traps; offlineInstaller + WSUS-managed Evergreen servicing is the sane middle.

Two independent key systems must both be protected: the Authenticode identity (Azure Artifact Signing account/profile) and the updater's minisign private key. Losing the minisign key permanently strands the installed fleet on old versions (no rotation path); leaking it lets an attacker who can also poison the update endpoint push updates. Treat TAURI_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY as a tier-0 secret and never let hooks/agents write it to disk.

signCommand is invoked for every file the bundler decides to sign — version bumps have changed that file set and broken releases (tauri#13991: bundling failed when trusted-signing-cli was handed a non-PE file). Pin tauri-cli/tauri-action exactly, and remember each release consumes multiple Artifact Signing quota signatures (exe + installer + updater artifacts), not one.

Expecting bit-for-bit reproducible signed installers: Authenticode + RFC3161 timestamping makes the shipped artifact non-deterministic by design. Compare unsigned intermediate hashes (SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH works for NSIS ≥3.06.1 and pinned Rust toolchains) and rely on provenance attestation for the signed artifact — also note GitHub attestations on private repos require Enterprise Cloud.

Shipping both NSIS and MSI 'to be safe': Tauri explicitly advises choosing one; dual formats create duplicate side-by-side installs and update-path bugs (the msi→nsis migration is supported because NSIS is the recommended direction, not to run both indefinitely). Similarly, don't flip nsis.installMode after first release — it changes install location and orphans existing installs.

topic: Tauri 2 security hardening — definitive 2026 checklist (capabilities ACL, IPC, CSP, isolation pattern, advisories, Rust hygiene, CI enforcement)

recommendations.practice: One capability file per window in src-tauri/capabilities/*.json, least-privilege permission identifiers only: no "windows": ["*"], no blanket plugin defaults (e.g. fs:default) where a narrower allow-* permission exists, filesystem/asset scopes limited to app-owned dirs ($APPDATA/**, $RESOURCE) with explicit deny entries for dotfiles — never $HOME/** or **. Keep assetProtocol.enable=false unless required, then scope it narrowly.
recommendations.rationale: Tauri 2 is default-deny: a command is only reachable from a webview if a capability grants it. Broad windows/*, wildcard scopes, or plugin :default sets silently re-open the attack surface. Three historical advisories (GHSA-6mv3-wm7j-h4w5 too-permissive glob, GHSA-q9wv-22m9-vhqh scope bypass, GHSA-wmff-grcw-jcfm dotfile regression) were all scope-breadth failures.
recommendations.tooling: Tauri >= 2.11.1; capability files with "$schema": "../gen/schemas/desktop-schema.json" (generated by tauri-build); jq 1.7 for policy asserts; Node script in packages/eval or scripts/ for the policy lint.
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: jq script fails if any capabilities/*.json contains windows==["*"], a scope entry matching "$HOME" or bare "**", or app.security.assetProtocol.enable==true without an accompanying scope allowlist; tauri-build itself hard-fails compilation on unknown permission identifiers, so `cargo check -p app` in CI is a second gate. Claude Code PreToolUse hook on Write|Edit blocks any diff to capabilities/** that introduces these banned patterns; lefthook pre-commit runs the same jq lint.

recommendations.practice: Ban remote-URL capabilities and remote content outright: no "remote" key in any capability file, no external origins loaded in app windows, and pin tauri >= 2.11.1 to get the is_local_url() origin-confusion fix.
recommendations.rationale: remote.urls grants IPC to external origins; the docs themselves warn that on Linux and Android Tauri cannot distinguish an embedded <iframe> from the window (GHSA-57fm-592m-34r7 iframe origin bypass). CVE-2026-42184 / GHSA-7gmj-67g7-phm9 (patched 2.11.1, May 2026) showed is_local_url() classified http://app.attacker.com as local on Windows/Android because it compared only the first subdomain label — remote pages could invoke 'local-only' IPC commands. Lesson: origin checks are fragile; the only robust posture for a fully on-prem app is zero remote capability grants.
recommendations.tooling: tauri crate >= 2.11.1 (@tauri-apps/cli ~2.11.4); jq; cargo-deny [bans] for version floors.
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: `jq -e 'has("remote")' capabilities/*.json` failing the build if any file has the key; cargo-deny [bans] entry denying tauri < 2.11.1 so the floor is mechanical, not advisory; Claude Code PreToolUse hook denies writes adding "remote": to capabilities or adding http(s) URLs to app.windows[].url in tauri.conf.json.

recommendations.practice: Treat every #[tauri::command] as an untrusted network endpoint: deserialize into strong types (serde with #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)], newtypes over raw String), canonicalize + prefix-check every path argument, and never let the IPC/capability layer be the authorization boundary — capabilities gate which WINDOW may call a command, not which USER may perform an action. In this stack all real authz lives in the Hono API session + Postgres FORCE RLS; Tauri commands must hold no privileged business authority of their own.
recommendations.rationale: The official security model states data crossing the webview→core trust boundary without access control lets attackers elevate privileges; the webview is compromised by any XSS or malicious npm dependency, after which it can call anything a capability exposes with attacker-chosen arguments. A command that trusts a client-supplied user ID or role is a confused deputy.
recommendations.tooling: serde 1.x deny_unknown_fields; std::fs::canonicalize / dunce crate for Windows path canonicalization; tauri::AppHandle path resolver for base dirs; Rust unit tests per command.
recommendations.enforcement: Test gate: cargo test in the Stop-gate validate chain runs per-command negative tests (path traversal ../, absolute paths, oversized payloads must return Err). Lint gate: grep/ast-grep CI rule failing any #[tauri::command] whose signature takes String or PathBuf for a filesystem target without going through the shared validated_path() helper module (ast-grep 0.3x pattern rule). Architecture rule: ESLint + code layout forbids the frontend from sending role/user-id fields to commands (no-restricted-syntax on invoke payload keys userId/role).

recommendations.practice: Single typed IPC surface: generate TypeScript bindings from Rust commands with tauri-specta v2 (#[specta::specta] + collect_commands!), commit the generated bindings.ts, and forbid raw invoke() calls anywhere except the generated module. Frontend uses only @tauri-apps/api and audited plugin packages; app.withGlobalTauri stays false and app.security.freezePrototype set true.
recommendations.rationale: tauri-specta makes the Rust signature the single source of truth so the frontend cannot drift or hand-roll stringly-typed invoke('cmd', {...}) calls that bypass review; withGlobalTauri=false removes the window.__TAURI__ global that injected/XSS code could reach without imports; freezePrototype=true blocks prototype-pollution hijacks of the IPC bridge.
recommendations.tooling: tauri-specta v2 + specta v2 (crates.io); @tauri-apps/api 2.x; ESLint 9 flat config: no-restricted-imports banning '@tauri-apps/api/core' outside src/ipc/**, no-restricted-globals for __TAURI__ and __TAURI_INTERNALS__.
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: regenerate bindings (cargo test export_bindings or build script) and `git diff --exit-code` on bindings.ts to fail on drift; ESLint 9 rules run in lefthook pre-commit and CI; jq assert in CI that .app.withGlobalTauri != true and .app.security.freezePrototype == true in tauri.conf.json.

recommendations.practice: Strict CSP in tauri.conf.json app.security.csp (and app.security.devCsp for dev): default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' (never 'unsafe-inline'/'unsafe-eval'; add 'wasm-unsafe-eval' only if shipping WASM); connect-src ipc: http://ipc.localhost plus only the on-prem API/llama.cpp origins; img-src 'self' asset: http://asset.localhost blob: data:; no CDN fonts/scripts (fully on-prem). Keep dangerousDisableAssetCspModification false so Tauri keeps auto-appending nonces/hashes to bundled assets.
recommendations.rationale: CSP is the main XSS blast-radius limiter inside the webview; Tauri appends its own nonces/hashes at compile time only to bundled code, so the developer-authored part must be strict. The default csp is null — unset means NO CSP ships. Official docs explicitly warn against remote CDN content as an attack vector.
recommendations.tooling: tauri.conf.json keys app.security.csp / devCsp / dangerousDisableAssetCspModification (schema https://schema.tauri.app/config/2); jq; Vite build (Tailwind v4 compiles to a bundled stylesheet so style-src 'self' generally suffices; shadcn/ui inline style attributes may need style-src 'unsafe-inline' — styles only, never scripts).
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: jq assertions — .app.security.csp != null; script-src (or default-src fallback) contains neither 'unsafe-inline' nor 'unsafe-eval'; connect-src values all match an allowlist regex (^ipc:$|^http://ipc.localhost$|^https://<on-prem-hosts>); .app.security.dangerousDisableAssetCspModification != true. Claude Code PreToolUse hook blocks edits that delete the csp key or add unsafe-* tokens to script-src.

recommendations.practice: Enable the isolation pattern: app.security.pattern = {"use": "isolation", "options": {"dir": "../dist-isolation"}} with an isolation hook that inspects every IPC payload before it reaches core (e.g., reject fs/command payloads whose paths fall outside expected roots), instead of the default brownfield pattern.
recommendations.rationale: The isolation app runs in a sandboxed iframe with per-launch keys; every frontend→core message is intercepted, verifiable, and re-encrypted, so malicious npm dependencies in the main frontend cannot forge IPC calls unseen. Official docs recommend it whenever untrusted/third-party JS runs in the frontend — which is any real React app with a dependency tree.
recommendations.tooling: Tauri 2 isolation pattern (v2.tauri.app/concept/inter-process-communication/isolation/); a small hand-written dist-isolation/index.html + hook.js kept dependency-free; Vitest unit tests for the hook logic.
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: jq assert .app.security.pattern.use == "isolation"; unit tests for the isolation hook run in the validate chain; Claude Code PreToolUse hook denies any edit switching pattern.use back to "brownfield"; dist-isolation/ is excluded from bundler config so it cannot silently gain npm dependencies (CI check: no package.json/node_modules inside it).

recommendations.practice: Schema-validate all Tauri JSON in CI: validate tauri.conf.json against the version-pinned config schema and every capabilities/*.json against the freshly regenerated gen/schemas/desktop-schema.json (regenerate first — never validate against stale vendored schemas). There is no first-party 'capability linter', so pair schema validation with the jq policy lints above.
recommendations.rationale: Schemas catch typos, unknown keys, and structurally invalid permissions before runtime (a misspelled permission otherwise only fails at cargo build; a misspelled config key may be silently ignored depending on tooling). gen/schemas is emitted by tauri-build per platform, so validating against it also proves the declared permissions exist for the exact plugin versions in the lockfile.
recommendations.tooling: ajv-cli 5.x (draft-07 support, fits pnpm monorepo) or check-jsonschema (Python) as fallback; schema sources: https://schema.tauri.app/config/2 (vendor a pinned copy) and src-tauri/gen/schemas/{desktop-schema.json,windows-schema.json}; tauri-build (runs in cargo check) regenerates gen/schemas.
recommendations.enforcement: CI job order: (1) cargo check -p app (regenerates gen/schemas + compiles ACL, failing on unknown permissions), (2) pnpm ajv validate -s src-tauri/gen/schemas/desktop-schema.json -d 'src-tauri/capabilities/*.json', (3) ajv validate tauri.conf.json against the vendored config schema, (4) jq policy lints. Same chain wired into lefthook pre-push and the Claude Code Stop-gate validate chain so an agent turn cannot end with invalid ACL files.

recommendations.practice: Rust host hygiene enforced via the manifest, not convention: [workspace.lints.rust] unsafe_code = "forbid"; [workspace.lints.clippy] unwrap_used = "deny", expect_used = "deny", panic = "deny", indexing_slicing = "warn", pedantic = "warn"; every crate opts in with [lints] workspace = true; CI runs cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings and cargo fmt --check.
recommendations.rationale: A panic in a #[tauri::command] is a denial-of-service reachable from the webview; unwrap/index panics on attacker-controlled input are the classic path. unsafe_code=forbid (not deny) cannot be overridden by an inner #[allow], making it a true mechanical guarantee for the host crate. Putting lints in Cargo.toml (RFC 3389) means the config travels with the code instead of living only in CI flags.
recommendations.tooling: rustc stable (2026), clippy shipped with toolchain, [lints]/[workspace.lints] tables (Cargo 1.74+, standard in 2026); rustfmt.
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: clippy -D warnings job required for merge via branch protection; lefthook pre-push runs the same command; CI grep gate fails if any Cargo.toml in src-tauri lacks [lints] workspace = true or if source contains #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)] without an adjacent // JUSTIFY: comment (grep -B1 check).

recommendations.practice: Supply-chain gates with version floors baked into policy: cargo-deny (advisories, licenses, bans, sources) + cargo-audit against the RustSec DB on every PR and nightly cron; [bans] deny tauri < 2.11.1 (CVE-2026-42184) and tauri-plugin-shell < 2.2.1 (CVE-2025-31477) — better, ban tauri-plugin-shell entirely and use tauri-plugin-opener for URL opening; every advisory ignore in deny.toml must carry a justification comment and expiry.
recommendations.rationale: Both 2025–2026 Tauri CVEs were fixed upstream quickly; the only real defense is mechanical version floors plus continuous advisory scanning. The shell-plugin advisory is instructive: the documented-safe default (open restricted to http/https/mailto) simply did not work due to improper validation — so prefer the purpose-built opener plugin and never expose shell execute to the webview. cargo-deny [sources] also pins registries, relevant for an on-prem/air-gapped mirror.
recommendations.tooling: cargo-deny 0.16+ with EmbarkStudios/cargo-deny-action@v2; cargo-audit 0.21+ with rustsec/audit-check action (or cargo audit --deny warnings — without the flag it exits 0 and CI passes green); tauri-plugin-opener 2.x; Renovate for automated bumps.
recommendations.enforcement: CI: cargo-deny-action@v2 and cargo audit --deny warnings as required checks + scheduled nightly workflow (new advisories break the cron, not just new PRs); deny.toml [bans] version floors make downgrades a build failure; CI grep gate fails if pnpm-lock.yaml or Cargo.lock contains tauri-plugin-shell; Renovate PRs auto-open for tauri-* patch releases.

recommendations.practice: Signing/updater key hygiene for the shipped Windows .exe: Authenticode signing only inside an environment-protected GitHub Actions release job; the Tauri updater minisign private key (TAURI_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY) lives only in CI secrets, is never given a VITE_ prefix or written to any .env consumed by Vite, and the updater pubkey + on-prem endpoint are pinned in tauri.conf.json.
recommendations.rationale: GHSA-2rcp-jvr4-r259 documented updater private keys leaking because Vite statically inlines env vars into the shipped frontend bundle — a build-system foot-gun, not a Tauri bug, and directly applicable to this Vite stack. A leaked updater key = silent malicious updates to every install; a leaked Authenticode key = trusted malware.
recommendations.tooling: tauri-cli signing (minisign) with createUpdaterArtifacts; Windows signing via azure-code-signing / SignTool step; gitleaks 8.x for secret scanning; GitHub environment-protected secrets with required reviewers.
recommendations.enforcement: CI grep gate: fail if any file matches VITE_[A-Z_]*(PRIVATE|SIGNING|KEY) or if TAURI_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY appears outside the release workflow YAML; gitleaks runs in lefthook pre-commit and as a required CI check; release workflow bound to a protected GitHub environment so the signing secret is unreadable from PR-triggered runs (no pull_request_target exposure); jq assert plugins.updater.pubkey is non-empty and endpoints match the on-prem host allowlist.

pitfalls: Confusing the capability ACL with user authorization: capabilities decide which WINDOW may invoke which command — they know nothing about which human is signed in. If a #[tauri::command] trusts client-supplied identity/role, any XSS or rogue npm dependency escalates instantly; authz must be re-checked server-side (Hono session + Postgres RLS).

Shipping with no CSP because the default is null: leaving app.security.csp unset means no CSP at all, and Tauri only auto-appends nonces/hashes when a CSP exists. Also, devCsp differs from prod, so a green dev run proves nothing about the shipped policy.

Trusting origin checks for 'local-only' commands: CVE-2026-42184 (fixed 2.11.1) showed is_local_url() matched only the first subdomain label, so http://app.attacker.com passed as local on Windows/Android; and on Linux/Android iframes are indistinguishable from the parent window. Any remote.urls capability or remote page load should be treated as granting IPC to the internet.

Believing plugin 'safe defaults' without testing them: CVE-2025-31477 — the shell plugin's documented default protocol restriction on open was non-functional, allowing file://, smb://, nfs://. Write a failing-input test for every security-relevant plugin behavior you rely on; don't cite docs as evidence.

Validating capabilities against stale schemas: gen/schemas/*.json is regenerated by tauri-build per platform and plugin set; CI must run cargo check first and validate against the fresh output, otherwise renamed/removed permissions pass schema validation and fail (or worse, silently no-op) later.

cargo audit without --deny warnings exits 0 on findings, so the CI job passes green while advisories pile up; similarly, advisory scans only on PR events miss advisories published between merges — a nightly scheduled run is required.

Vite env inlining leaks secrets into the shipped bundle: any VITE_-prefixed variable is baked into frontend JS (GHSA-2rcp-jvr4-r259 leaked updater private keys this way). Windows signing and updater keys must never transit the frontend build environment.

Wildcard erosion over time: "windows": ["*"], plugin :default permission sets, and $HOME/** scopes each look harmless in one PR; without a mechanical jq/policy lint plus a pre-write hook, agent-generated code will re-add them because they make errors go away.

topic: Maximum-rigor TypeScript for a pnpm monorepo (Vite React SPA + Hono Node 22 server + shared packages), verified against primary sources as of July 2026. Landscape: TypeScript 6.0 GA (Mar 23, 2026; final JS-codebase release, strict-by-default, deprecations become hard errors in 7.0) and TypeScript 7.0 GA (Jul 8, 2026; Go-native compiler, ~10x faster, `tsc` binary ships in typescript@7). typescript-eslint 8.63.x, Biome 2.5 (500+ rules, partial type-aware linting), knip 6.25, dependency-cruiser 18.0, Zod 4 (drizzle-zod >=0.8.3 for Zod-4 peers), Node 22.18+ runs erasable-syntax TypeScript natively via type stripping.

recommendations.practice: Pin TypeScript 6.0.x repo-wide as the checked compiler; run a non-blocking canary CI lane on typescript@7. TS 6.0 is deliberately the bridge release: everything it deprecates becomes a hard error in 7.0, so a deprecation-clean 6.0 config is the 7.0 migration.
recommendations.rationale: TS 7.0 (Go-native) GA'd Jul 8, 2026 — days old. Tooling that consumes the TypeScript JS API (typescript-eslint typed linting, ts-morph, api-extractor) is not guaranteed compatible with the native compiler yet; 6.0 gives identical type-checking semantics with a guaranteed upgrade path. TS 6.0 also flipped defaults the harness wants anyway: strict=true, noUncheckedSideEffectImports=true, types=[], module=esnext, target=es2025, and removed baseUrl-as-lookup-root, outFile, moduleResolution classic.
recommendations.tooling: typescript@~6.0.x as a single root devDependency; pnpm catalogs (pnpm-workspace.yaml `catalog:`) so every package resolves the same version; syncpack or `pnpm dedupe --check` as belt-and-braces; canary job uses `pnpm add -D typescript@7 --dir /tmp-overlay` style override.
recommendations.enforcement: CI: `pnpm ls typescript --depth Infinity --json` script fails if more than one version resolves; pnpm catalog makes version drift a lockfile diff that a lefthook pre-commit hook and a CI `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` catch. Canary lane: separate GitHub Actions job running `tsc -b` under typescript@7, `continue-on-error: true`, reported as annotation.

recommendations.practice: Single shared max-strict base tsconfig in packages/config (extended everywhere) with the full flag set: strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, exactOptionalPropertyTypes, noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature, noImplicitOverride, noImplicitReturns, noFallthroughCasesInSwitch, allowUnreachableCode:false, allowUnusedLabels:false, verbatimModuleSyntax, isolatedModules, skipLibCheck:true, declaration+declarationMap+sourceMap in packages. Two overlays: apps/desktop (Vite): module 'preserve' (implies moduleResolution 'bundler'), noEmit, types:[] plus explicit vite/client; apps/server (Node 22): module 'nodenext', erasableSyntaxOnly, rewriteRelativeImportExtensions, allowImportingTsExtensions, types:['node'].
recommendations.rationale: This is the maximal soundness set: noUncheckedIndexedAccess makes the 10k-node graph traversal code handle missing keys; exactOptionalPropertyTypes distinguishes absent vs undefined (matters for PATCH semantics and exactOptional Zod fields); verbatimModuleSyntax makes type-only imports explicit, which transpile-only pipelines (Vite/Oxc, Node type stripping) require. erasableSyntaxOnly on the server bans enums/namespaces/parameter properties so Node 22.18+ can execute server TS directly via type stripping — the official Node docs recommend exactly this tsconfig (noEmit, module nodenext, rewriteRelativeImportExtensions, erasableSyntaxOnly, verbatimModuleSyntax). TS 6.0's types:[] default means @types/node must now be explicit.
recommendations.tooling: typescript@6.0.x; packages/config/tsconfig.base.json + tsconfig.vite.json + tsconfig.node.json; Vite 8 (Oxc transpile, no type check — docs mandate isolatedModules:true); Node >=22.18 (type stripping on by default).
recommendations.enforcement: CI drift gate: script runs `tsc --showConfig -p <each tsconfig>` and asserts the required flag set with jq; fails on any weakening. Claude Code PreToolUse hook: block Write/Edit to any tsconfig*.json that removes/sets-false a protected flag (regex + jq diff against baseline), exit 2 to refuse the write. Typecheck gate itself: `tsc -b` at repo root in CI and in the Stop-gate validate chain.

recommendations.practice: Use TypeScript project references (composite packages, apps as leaf projects) as the type-check topology, with `tsc -b` as the one typecheck command; keep references mechanically synchronized with package.json workspace dependencies. Enable isolatedDeclarations only on leaf utility packages (packages/importer, packages/eval), NOT on the Zod/drizzle contracts package.
recommendations.rationale: References give per-package isolation (importing a workspace package absent from `references` is a compile error — free architecture enforcement), incremental builds, and emitted .d.ts that solves Hono RPC type-instantiation blowups (Hono's own docs recommend compiling types / project references for monorepos). The alternative 'live types via custom export conditions' (colinhacks) is better DX but loses the declared-graph enforcement; a rigor-first harness wants references. isolatedDeclarations enables parallel d.ts emit and forces explicit public APIs, but it fights schema-inferred exports (Zod/drizzle types can't be reasonably hand-annotated), so scope it.
recommendations.tooling: typescript@6.0.x `tsc -b`; @monorepo-utils/workspaces-to-typescript-project-references (has a --check mode) to sync references from pnpm-workspace deps; composite:true + declaration:true in packages; apps use noEmit and reference the packages.
recommendations.enforcement: CI: `workspaces-to-typescript-project-references --check` fails when references drift from package.json deps; `tsc -b --force` (or cached incremental with actions/cache on tsbuildinfo) is a required status check; lefthook pre-push runs `tsc -b`. The Stop gate includes `tsc -b` so an agent turn cannot end with a red typecheck.

recommendations.practice: typescript-eslint 8.x (currently 8.63.x) flat config with strictTypeChecked + stylisticTypeChecked, enabled via parserOptions.projectService:true + tsconfigRootDir — no per-package parser config needed in the pnpm monorepo. Scope ESLint to type-aware work only; keep no-floating-promises, no-misused-promises, the no-unsafe-* family, switch-exhaustiveness-check, restrict-template-expressions at error.
recommendations.rationale: projectService is the v8 stable path: it reuses the same tsconfigs as the editor, supports project references, and needs zero monorepo-specific setup (official monorepo docs). strictTypeChecked is the maximal preset; type-aware rules are exactly the class Biome cannot yet fully replicate (Biome's own numbers: its inference catches ~75-85% of floating promises vs typescript-eslint).
recommendations.tooling: typescript-eslint@^8.63, eslint@^9 flat config; projectService.allowDefaultProject for stray config files (eslint.config.ts, vite.config.ts) not covered by a tsconfig; @eslint-community/eslint-plugin-eslint-comments with require-description and no-restricted-disable so type-safety rules cannot be silently disabled.
recommendations.enforcement: CI + Stop gate: `eslint . --max-warnings 0` (warnings are failures). eslint-comments/no-restricted-disable configured to forbid disabling @typescript-eslint/no-unsafe-*, no-floating-promises, no-explicit-any — making 'agent turns off the rule' itself a lint error. Claude Code PreToolUse hook additionally regex-blocks writes containing `eslint-disable` without a trailing `-- reason` description, `@ts-ignore`, or `: any`.

recommendations.practice: Biome 2.4/2.5 is the sole formatter and the fast first-pass linter (correctness/suspicious/a11y/nursery-Vue-free set); typescript-eslint is the sole owner of type-aware rules. Maintain an explicit single-owner matrix: disable Biome's type-aware rules (noFloatingPromises, noMisusedPromises, useAwaitThenable, noUnnecessaryConditions) because ESLint owns them; disable typescript-eslint stylistic rules that Biome covers syntactically.
recommendations.rationale: Biome 2.5 passed 500 rules and its compiler-free type inference is genuinely useful but still partial (Pick/Omit/Record resolution only recently landed; ~75-85% floating-promise recall) — running both owners for one rule produces double or conflicting diagnostics and autofix fights. One formatter (Biome) eliminates Prettier/ESLint formatting conflicts entirely; Biome's a11y rules also support the WCAG 2.2 AA goal cheaply on every keystroke.
recommendations.tooling: @biomejs/biome@^2.5 (biome.json with `assist` + formatter + linter sections, per-rule offs documented inline); eslint-config-biome (or manual off-list) to silence ESLint rules Biome owns; lefthook for staged runs.
recommendations.enforcement: CI: `biome ci .` (checks formatting + lint, no writes) as a required check. lefthook pre-commit: `biome check --staged --write` then re-stage; pre-push: full `biome ci`. Stop gate runs `biome ci .` first because it is the fastest failure. Rule-ownership drift is caught by a repo test that parses biome.json and eslint config and fails if a denylisted rule pair is enabled in both.

recommendations.practice: knip 6.x in monorepo mode as the dead-code gate: workspaces map in knip.json mirroring pnpm-workspace.yaml, per-workspace entry/project globs, `--production --strict` profile for apps/desktop and apps/server, default profile for packages. Never let agents run `knip --fix`.
recommendations.rationale: knip has first-class pnpm workspace support and understands cross-workspace exports (a packages/schema export used only by apps/server is not 'unused'). Dead exports, unused deps, and unlisted deps are exactly the entropy AI-generated code accumulates; strict production mode also catches devDependencies leaking into runtime code — relevant to keeping server code out of the signed desktop bundle.
recommendations.tooling: knip@^6.25 with knip.json workspaces config; ignoreDependencies/entry overrides for dynamic references (Tauri command handlers invoked by string from Rust, GBNF grammar assets, lefthook scripts).
recommendations.enforcement: CI required check: `knip` and `knip --production --strict` (two jobs). Claude Code PreToolUse Bash-guard denies `knip --fix` (auto-deletion by an agent is how false positives become outages); instead the Stop gate surfaces knip failures and the agent must edit code or the reviewed knip.json (tsconfig-style protected file, hook-guarded) to resolve.

recommendations.practice: dependency-cruiser 18 as the architecture law: forbidden rules (1) apps/desktop may import only packages/schema + its own tree — never apps/server, drizzle-orm, pg, or any DAL path; (2) packages/schema imports nothing from apps/* and is the only workspace allowed to import drizzle-zod; (3) no-circular, no-orphans repo-wide; (4) server-only modules (env, db, auth) reachable only from apps/server. Layer a second, bundle-level backstop in the SPA.
recommendations.rationale: Type-level boundaries (project references) don't stop value imports that bundle server secrets/DAL into the shipped Windows .exe. dependency-cruiser validates the real import graph and fails CI with named rule violations; it is framework-agnostic and supports allowed/forbidden/required rule types. The bundle grep backstop catches anything that sneaks past static analysis via re-exports.
recommendations.tooling: dependency-cruiser@^18 (.dependency-cruiser.cjs, doNotFollow node_modules, tsPreCompilationDeps:true so type-only imports are distinguished from runtime ones); eslint no-restricted-imports zones in apps/desktop as a same-editor early warning; a post-build script scanning Vite output for markers ('drizzle-orm', 'pg', 'process.env') that must never appear client-side.
recommendations.enforcement: CI required check: `depcruise apps packages --config .dependency-cruiser.cjs --output-type err` (non-zero exit on violation); lefthook pre-push mirrors it. Post-build CI step greps dist/ for forbidden markers and fails. Rule file is hook-protected like tsconfig (PreToolUse blocks weakening edits).

recommendations.practice: One contracts package (packages/schema) as the single source of truth: Drizzle table definitions -> drizzle-zod (>=0.8.3, Zod-4 peers) createSelectSchema/createInsertSchema with .extend/.refine for API shapes -> Hono routes built as a chained app with @hono/zod-validator zValidator -> `export type AppType = typeof routes` -> desktop consumes a pre-compiled hcWithType wrapper (hc<AppType> instantiated once in the server package, exported through emitted .d.ts). Optionally import zod/mini in the SPA for bundle size.
recommendations.rationale: The column type then flows DB row -> insert type -> request validator -> handler return -> RPC client with zero hand-written duplication; the compiler fails when any layer drifts. Hono's RPC docs explicitly recommend compiling types (hcWithType + project references) and identical hono versions on both sides to avoid monorepo IDE/type-instantiation blowups. drizzle-zod before 0.8.3 pins Zod 3 — a silent version-skew trap. Zod 4's native z.toJSONSchema also feeds the llama.cpp constrained-decoding grammar from the same schema.
recommendations.tooling: zod@^4, drizzle-zod@>=0.8.3, drizzle-orm, hono + @hono/zod-validator (same hono version pinned via pnpm catalog), hc/hcWithType from hono/client; z.toJSONSchema for GBNF/JSON-schema generation.
recommendations.enforcement: pnpm catalog pins hono and zod to one version (lockfile-gated). dependency-cruiser rule: only packages/schema imports drizzle-zod. `tsc -b` across the reference boundary is the contract test — client/server drift is a build failure. Vitest contract suite: golden request/response fixtures must safeParse against the exported schemas; a snapshot test asserts z.toJSONSchema output matches the checked-in grammar so LLM constrained decoding cannot drift from the parser.

recommendations.practice: Parse-don't-validate at every trust boundary, enforced as bans: all ingress (HTTP body/query/params via zValidator, env via a single env.ts Zod parse, LLM output via constrained decoding + safeParse, file imports in packages/importer, Tauri IPC payloads) is parsed into typed values exactly once; interior code never re-checks and never asserts.
recommendations.rationale: With FORCE RLS and human-in-the-loop QA, the server must treat every payload — including the local LLM's output — as untrusted text until a Zod parse succeeds. Type assertions and unchecked JSON.parse are the two escape hatches that let 'validated' lies into typed code; strictTypeChecked's no-unsafe-* family plus assertion bans close them mechanically.
recommendations.tooling: @typescript-eslint/consistent-type-assertions { assertionStyle: 'never' } (carve-out for `as const` via allowConstAssertions patterns), no-non-null-assertion (in strict preset), no-restricted-syntax banning `JSON.parse` and `as unknown as` outside packages/schema/src/parsers/**, eslint-plugin-n n/no-process-env so raw process.env only exists in env.ts, no-restricted-imports blocking raw `fetch`/axios in apps/desktop in favor of the typed hc client.
recommendations.enforcement: All of the above run under `eslint --max-warnings 0` in CI and the Stop gate. Claude Code PreToolUse write-guard regexes reject new occurrences of ` as any`, `@ts-ignore`, `JSON.parse(` outside the parsers directory, and `process.env.` outside env.ts before the file ever hits disk. Vitest boundary tests feed malformed fixtures (truncated LLM JSON, over-long strings, wrong enum members) and assert 4xx/typed-error, never a throw.

recommendations.practice: Wire the whole chain into a three-layer mechanical gate: (1) Claude Code hooks — PreToolUse write-guards for protected files/patterns, a Bash-guard denylist (knip --fix, eslint --fix on protected rules, editing lockfile by hand), and a Stop hook that runs `pnpm validate` (biome ci -> eslint --max-warnings 0 -> tsc -b -> depcruise -> knip -> vitest run) and exits 2 to refuse ending the turn until green; (2) lefthook pre-commit (biome staged) and pre-push (full chain) with commitlint; (3) GitHub Actions running the identical `pnpm validate` as required status checks, actions pinned by commit SHA, `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`.
recommendations.rationale: Each layer catches what the previous can be bypassed on: hooks discipline the agent in-session, lefthook disciplines humans locally (bypassable with --no-verify), CI is the unbypassable arbiter. Ordering the validate chain fastest-first (Biome ~ms, then eslint typed, then tsc -b) gives agents the cheapest possible red/green loop, which matters when a Stop gate forces iteration until green.
recommendations.tooling: Claude Code hooks (PreToolUse matcher on Write|Edit and Bash; Stop hook returning exit code 2 with stderr instructions); lefthook@^1 + @commitlint/config-conventional; GitHub Actions with pnpm/action-setup, actions pinned to SHAs, concurrency-cancelled PR workflow; turbo or pnpm -r --workspace-concurrency for parallel package runs with caching.
recommendations.enforcement: This item IS the enforcement fabric: Stop hook blocks turn completion (exit 2 + reason), PreToolUse blocks bad writes before they exist, branch protection requires the CI jobs, and the hook settings file itself (.claude/settings.json) plus lefthook.yml are in the protected-file list so the agent cannot edit its own guardrails.

pitfalls: Jumping straight to TypeScript 7.0 (GA Jul 8, 2026): the Go-native compiler does not expose the legacy JS compiler API the typed-lint ecosystem consumes — typescript-eslint typed linting, ts-morph, api-extractor and similar may break or silently degrade. Pin 6.0.x for checking/linting; treat 7.0 as a canary lane until typescript-eslint announces support.

TS 6.0's changed defaults break implicit setups: types now defaults to [] (so @types/node must be listed explicitly or process/Buffer go missing), baseUrl is deprecated as a module lookup root (path-alias configs relying on it must move to package.json imports/exports), and moduleResolution 'node10'/'classic' are gone. These surface as sudden 'cannot find name/module' errors mid-upgrade.

exactOptionalPropertyTypes is the flag most likely to fight third-party types (some React/radix/shadcn prop types assign `undefined` explicitly). The failure mode to guard against is an agent 'fixing' it by weakening the flag repo-wide; the correct fixes are local (spread-conditional object construction, satisfies, or a typed wrapper). Protect tsconfig with a pre-write hook.

Dual-linter rule overlap: Biome 2.5 now ships type-aware rules (noFloatingPromises etc.) with ~75-85% recall; enabling them alongside typescript-eslint's versions yields duplicate/conflicting diagnostics and autofix fights. Every rule needs exactly one owner, and the ownership matrix needs a test, or it drifts.

Hono RPC hc<AppType> in a monorepo without pre-compiled types causes massive type-instantiation cost — IDE lag and slow tsc — and mismatched hono versions between server and desktop silently degrade inference (client calls devolve toward any/never). Mitigations are documented by Hono itself: emit d.ts via project references, export an hcWithType wrapper, pin one hono version via pnpm catalog.

isolatedDeclarations on the contracts package is a trap: Zod/drizzle-inferred exported types can't be reasonably hand-annotated, so the flag forces either giant explicit annotations or abandoning inference (the schema single-source-of-truth). Scope it to leaf utility packages only.

verbatimModuleSyntax + module nodenext makes ESM/CJS explicit: any workspace package without "type": "module" gets its files parsed as CJS and ESM import/export syntax becomes an error; likewise erasableSyntaxOnly bans enums/namespaces/parameter properties that agents habitually generate. Decide 'ESM-only, type:module everywhere' up front and lint-guard package.json.

knip and projectService edge cases in agent hands: knip false-positives on dynamically referenced code (Tauri commands invoked by string from Rust, GBNF assets, script entries) tempt agents to delete live code or add blanket ignores — deny `knip --fix` and hook-protect knip.json; typescript-eslint projectService errors on files outside any tsconfig (vite.config.ts, eslint.config.ts) unless allowDefaultProject lists them, which agents often 'fix' by disabling typed linting.

topic: World-class testing stack for a Tauri 2 + React 19 + Hono + Postgres 16 pnpm monorepo (verified July 2026)

recommendations.practice: Standardize on Vitest 4.x (not 3.x) with inline `test.projects` in a single root vitest.config.ts defining one project per concern: unit-node (apps/server, packages/schema, packages/importer, packages/eval), unit-dom (jsdom), browser (real Chromium), integration (testcontainers), property (fast-check).
recommendations.rationale: Vitest 4.0 shipped 2025-10-22 (current: 4.1.10, 2026-07-06). The separate vitest.workspace.ts file and top-level `workspace` option are REMOVED in v4 — `projects` inside the root config is the only supported monorepo shape. Named projects also give you selective execution (`vitest run --project unit-node`) which the Stop gate and CI lanes need.
recommendations.tooling: vitest 4.1.10, @vitest/coverage-v8 4.1.10; projects as glob (`./packages/*`) plus inline objects with per-project environment/setupFiles
recommendations.enforcement: Stop gate (stop-validate-gate.mjs) runs `pnpm vitest run --project unit-node --project unit-dom` in the validate chain; pre-write hook (pretool-write-guard.mjs) rejects creation of any `vitest.workspace.*` file or `defineWorkspace` import (agents trained on v3 docs will recreate them); CI required check runs all projects; Renovate pins the vitest package group.

recommendations.practice: Two-tier React component testing: @testing-library/react on jsdom for logic-heavy fast tests on every write, and Vitest Browser Mode (stable since v4) with vitest-browser-react + @vitest/browser-playwright for interaction, focus, CSS, and visual-regression tests (toMatchScreenshot) — the coverage-matrix visualization <1s render claims belong in the browser project.
recommendations.rationale: Browser Mode lost its experimental tag in Vitest 4 and is the 2026-recommended path (RTL's own author endorses migrating; vitest-browser-react 2.2.0 mirrors RTL's API with built-in retry-ability). jsdom cannot test computed CSS, ResizeObserver-driven layouts, canvas, or real focus behavior — all of which the coverage-matrix viz depends on. Browser tests run 2-4x slower, hence the split lanes.
recommendations.tooling: vitest-browser-react 2.2.0, @vitest/browser-playwright 4.1.10 (provider is now a separate package in v4; imports moved from @vitest/browser/context to vitest/browser)
recommendations.enforcement: Two Vitest projects with distinct include globs (`*.test.tsx` = jsdom, `*.browser.test.tsx` = browser); Stop gate runs jsdom lane always, browser lane when files under apps/desktop/src changed; CI runs both as required checks; eslint-plugin-testing-library + a no-restricted-imports rule banning `@vitest/browser` (removed package) keep test code on the v4 API.

recommendations.practice: Tauri 2 E2E primary lane: @wdio/tauri-service with the EMBEDDED provider (tauri-plugin-wdio-webdriver crate compiled into debug builds) driving the real .exe on windows-latest runners; do NOT hand-wire raw tauri-driver + WebdriverIO.
recommendations.rationale: This is the officially recommended approach in the Tauri v2 docs as of 2026. The embedded provider runs a W3C WebDriver server inside the app, eliminating the msedgedriver/WebView2 version-matching problem that plagued raw tauri-driver setups (documented community failures required downgrading to WDIO v7). It also exposes `browser.tauri.execute()` for real IPC assertions and forwards Rust + frontend logs to the test reporter. tauri-driver itself remains Windows/Linux only with no macOS support.
recommendations.tooling: @wdio/tauri-service 1.2.0 (2026-06-25), tauri-plugin-wdio-webdriver = "1" in Cargo.toml behind a `#[cfg(debug_assertions)]` guard, optional @wdio/tauri-plugin for in-webview integration
recommendations.enforcement: CI job on windows-latest builds the debug .exe and runs the wdio suite as a required status check on PRs touching apps/desktop; a Rust unit test (or CI grep gate) asserts the wdio-webdriver plugin is not registered in release builds; full suite nightly, tagged smoke subset on PR to keep wall-time bounded.

recommendations.practice: Fast E2E lane: Playwright 1.61 against the Vite dev server with Tauri IPC mocked via `mockIPC`/`mockWindows` from @tauri-apps/api/mocks, injected only when an env flag (e.g. VITE_E2E_MOCK_IPC=1) is set. Runs headless on cheap ubuntu-latest runners on every PR; the WDIO Windows lane remains the fidelity check.
recommendations.rationale: This is the established pragmatic pattern in the Tauri community (tauri-playwright and similar): all UI flows, routing, and rendering are testable without compiling the Rust binary, cutting PR feedback from ~20 min to ~3 min. The risk is mock drift, so the mock layer must be derived from the same command/type definitions the Rust side uses (e.g. tauri-specta-generated types) and every mocked command needs one real-IPC WDIO counterpart.
recommendations.tooling: @playwright/test 1.61.1, @tauri-apps/api/mocks (mockIPC, clearMocks), playwright.config.ts webServer block starting `vite --mode e2e`
recommendations.enforcement: Required PR status check; pre-write hook blocks imports of `@tauri-apps/api/mocks` outside e2e/ and src/testing/ paths; a CI script diffs the list of mocked command names against the Rust `#[tauri::command]` inventory (grep or specta export) and fails on unmocked/orphaned commands.

recommendations.practice: Rust host: `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings` and `cargo fmt --check` in CI, with lint policy declared in `[workspace.lints.clippy]` in the root Cargo.toml (not `#![deny(warnings)]` in source), and cargo-nextest as the test runner.
recommendations.rationale: Denying warnings at the CI command line keeps builds reproducible for contributors on newer toolchains (in-source deny(warnings) is a documented Rust anti-pattern). The workspace lints table makes lint levels reviewable and inherited by every crate. cargo-nextest runs each test in its own process — better isolation and up to ~3x faster, with native JUnit output for CI annotation.
recommendations.tooling: clippy on a pinned toolchain (rust-toolchain.toml), cargo-nextest (JUnit via --message-format), [workspace.lints] table, taiki-e/install-action for CI install
recommendations.enforcement: Required CI job: fmt --check, clippy -D warnings, `cargo nextest run --workspace`; lefthook pre-push runs clippy on the touched crate only; the Stop-gate validate chain includes `cargo check` (fast) whenever files under src-tauri changed so the agent cannot end a turn with a broken Rust build.

recommendations.practice: Mutation testing as the real test-quality metric: StrykerJS 9.6.x with the official Vitest runner in --incremental mode on PRs, scoped via `mutate` globs to high-value logic (packages/importer, packages/schema, server route handlers); cargo-mutants with --in-diff for the Rust host.
recommendations.rationale: Coverage says code ran; mutation score says tests would notice a bug. StrykerJS 9.4+ supports Vitest 4 (9.6.1, 2026-04-10, fixed mutant hit-count for Vitest 4.1) and incremental mode diffs code+tests against a stored report so PR runs only re-test changed mutants. cargo-mutants (Thoughtworks Radar-listed) supports --in-diff for PR-scoped runs and shard flags for nightly full runs.
recommendations.tooling: @stryker-mutator/core 9.6.1 + @stryker-mutator/vitest-runner, reports/stryker-incremental.json cached via actions/cache keyed on branch; cargo-mutants via install-action
recommendations.enforcement: stryker.config.json `thresholds.break` (start ~60, ratchet up) makes the CI job a hard failure; incremental report cache restore/save steps in the workflow; nightly full-run workflow publishes the HTML report; cargo-mutants PR job feeds `git diff origin/main` to --in-diff and fails on missed mutants in changed lines.

recommendations.practice: Property-based testing with fast-check 4.9 for the .xlsx importer: model-based round-trip properties (generate a random workbook model → write real .xlsx bytes → run the importer → assert model equivalence) plus adversarial arbitraries for merged cells, date/1900-leap-year encodings, formula strings, unicode, and empty/ragged rows; same technique for GBNF/JSON-schema LLM-output parsers in packages/eval.
recommendations.rationale: Parsers/importers are the canonical PBT win: example-based tests encode the fixtures you thought of, properties find the row-offset and type-coercion bugs you didn't. fast-check 4.x is actively developed (4.9.0 published 2026-07-08) with reversible fc.json() and stringMatching improvements useful for constrained-decoding validation. Failing seeds are reported and replayable, which fits agent-driven repair loops perfectly.
recommendations.tooling: fast-check 4.9.0, @fast-check/vitest for `test.prop` integration, exceljs (or actively-maintained xlsx-kit) as the write-side oracle; fc.configureGlobal({ numRuns: 100 local / 1000 nightly })
recommendations.enforcement: Dedicated `property` Vitest project included in the CI required check; failing seed printed and uploaded as a CI artifact, and the harness convention writes reproducing seeds into a committed regressions file (fc.sample with { seed }); a validate-chain meta-check fails if packages/importer contains a parser module with no corresponding *.prop.test.ts.

recommendations.practice: Accessibility automation in two layers: axe-core 4.12 run directly inside Vitest Browser Mode per component (do NOT use the abandoned vitest-axe wrapper), and @axe-core/playwright with AxeBuilder.withTags(['wcag2a','wcag2aa','wcag21a','wcag21aa','wcag22a','wcag22aa']) per route in E2E; keyboard-first behavior enforced with scripted Tab/Shift-Tab/Escape sequences and Playwright's order-sensitive `toMatchAriaSnapshot` YAML snapshots of the accessibility tree.
recommendations.rationale: axe automates roughly 30-50% of WCAG findings; the rest of your keyboard-first WCAG 2.2 AA bar (focus order 2.4.3, no keyboard traps 2.1.2, focus-not-obscured 2.4.11) needs explicit interaction tests. Aria snapshots are order-sensitive and resilient to CSS churn — ideal for pinning the traversal order of a 10k-node coverage matrix without brittle screenshots.
recommendations.tooling: axe-core 4.12.1, @axe-core/playwright 4.12.1, page.keyboard.press loops asserting document.activeElement, expect(page).toMatchAriaSnapshot()
recommendations.enforcement: Test helper asserts `violations` array is empty (hard failure, no warn mode); a route-manifest module is iterated by the a11y spec and a meta-test fails when a router route is missing from the manifest, so new pages cannot ship unscanned; aria-snapshot diffs require explicit `--update-snapshots` commits reviewed like code; CI a11y job is a required status check.

recommendations.practice: Coverage that means something: @vitest/coverage-v8 4.x (AST-based remapping is now the default), explicit `coverage.include` per project, per-package glob-key thresholds instead of one global number, and a ratchet policy — thresholds only ever go up, with high floors on packages/importer and packages/schema and modest floors on UI glue.
recommendations.rationale: Vitest 4 REMOVED coverage.all — files never loaded by tests silently disappear from reports unless coverage.include is set, which turns an unset include into invisible coverage loss. Per-glob thresholds (supported natively in coverage.thresholds) put the pressure where correctness lives; the mutation-score gate (rec 6) prevents assertion-free tests from gaming the line numbers.
recommendations.tooling: @vitest/coverage-v8 4.1.10; coverage.thresholds with glob keys (e.g. 'packages/importer/**': { lines: 90, branches: 85 }), thresholds.autoUpdate for the ratchet
recommendations.enforcement: vitest exits non-zero on threshold breach (CI required check); a lefthook/CI diff guard on vitest.config.ts rejects any commit that lowers a threshold number; thresholds.autoUpdate runs on main after merge so the ratchet is committed mechanically, not by hand.

recommendations.practice: Postgres integration tests with @testcontainers/postgresql 12.x running the pgvector/pgvector:pg16 image: apply Drizzle migrations once in beforeAll, use the module's snapshot()/restoreSnapshot() for per-test isolation, and make RLS tests first-class — every policy gets a positive control (owner role sees rows) and a negative control (non-owner API role, non-superuser so FORCE RLS applies, sees none).
recommendations.rationale: Mocked repositories cannot exercise FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, pgvector operators, or Drizzle-generated SQL; testcontainers runs the exact image you ship on-prem. Snapshot/restore (Postgres module, testcontainers >=10.23; current line is 12.0.4, 2026-06-29) gives clean-slate isolation orders of magnitude faster than container-per-test. Seeded positive controls prevent the classic vacuous-green RLS test where the query returns nothing for the wrong reason.
recommendations.tooling: @testcontainers/postgresql 12.0.4, pgvector/pgvector:pg16 image mirrored to the on-prem registry, drizzle-kit migrate against the container, connections as a dedicated non-superuser test role
recommendations.enforcement: Separate `integration` Vitest project run on ubuntu-latest only (Docker unavailable/flaky on Windows runners) as a required check; GitHub Actions path filter forces the integration job whenever packages/schema or migration files change; a meta-test enumerates tables with RLS enabled (pg_catalog query) and fails if any lacks a matching policy-test file — mirroring the sibling harness's rls_verify seeded-control pattern.

pitfalls: Vitest 3 muscle memory: agents and older docs will scaffold vitest.workspace.ts / defineWorkspace, which Vitest 4 removed outright — without a pre-write guard the config silently stops applying to sub-packages and tests still 'pass' because only the root project runs.

vitest-axe is abandoned (v0.1.0, published Oct 2022) — scaffolding it against Vitest 4 wastes an agent-repair loop; call axe-core 4.12 directly in Browser Mode or use @axe-core/playwright in E2E instead.

Raw tauri-driver + latest WebdriverIO has documented incompatibilities (community repros ended up pinning WDIO v7), tauri-driver has no macOS support, and Windows runs die on WebView2-vs-msedgedriver version mismatch with cryptic errors — use @wdio/tauri-service's embedded provider, and add a CI preflight step that prints WebView2 and driver versions so mismatches fail loudly.

The mocked-IPC Playwright lane drifts silently from the real Rust command surface: a renamed #[tauri::command] keeps mock tests green while the shipped .exe breaks — the mock inventory must be mechanically diffed against the Rust command list in CI, and mocked-lane green must never be the only E2E gate.

Coverage theater, v4 edition: with coverage.all removed, an unset coverage.include means never-imported files vanish from the report entirely, so a package can drop to 0% real coverage while the dashboard shows 95% — explicit include globs plus mutation score are the countermeasures.

Full StrykerJS runs on a monorepo this size take hours; without --incremental plus a correctly cached reports/stryker-incremental.json (actions/cache keyed to branch with a main fallback) every PR re-tests all mutants and the team disables the gate within a month.

An all-green axe run is not WCAG 2.2 AA: axe automates roughly 30-50% of criteria and cannot detect keyboard traps, focus order, or focus-obscured regressions — those need scripted keyboard tests and order-sensitive aria snapshots, and any compliance claim in docs should cite both layers.

Vitest Browser Mode tests are 2-4x slower than jsdom and embedded-WDIO .exe builds are slower still — putting every lane in the agent's Stop-gate validate chain makes turns take 20+ minutes and agents start gaming the gate; the chain should run fast lanes (typecheck, lint, unit, changed-package integration) and defer browser/WDIO/mutation lanes to PR CI and nightly.
