topic: Hardened GitHub Actions CI/CD for a Tauri 2 Windows desktop app + Node 22/pnpm monorepo (July 2026): build strategy, Windows runner economics, in-CI code signing, attestation/SBOM, supply-chain pinning, scanner coverage across npm+crates, and release automation — with mechanical enforcement for each.

recommendations.practice: Hand-roll the Windows release build job (checkout → pnpm install → `pnpm tauri build`) instead of delegating to tauri-apps/tauri-action; use tauri-action at most as a reference workflow.
recommendations.rationale: tauri-action v1 (v1.x line, Tauri-2-only since v1.0.0) is a large third-party JS action that wraps build+release-upload in one step, which blocks interleaving hardened steps (sign → verify signature → attest → SBOM → verify attestation → upload) and forces you to hand it repo-scoped tokens and signing env vars. Real-world breakage exists at the tauri-action/signing seam (tauri-apps/tauri#13991, trusted-signing-cli failures closed 'not planned'). A hand-rolled job is ~30 lines, fully SHA-pinnable, and each artifact-mutating step becomes an auditable, gateable stage. Keep dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable for the Rust toolchain.
recommendations.tooling: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable (SHA-pinned), pnpm/action-setup@v4 (>=4.3.0), actions/setup-node@v6 (node-version: 22), `pnpm tauri build` (tauri-cli 2.x), softprops/action-gh-release or `gh release upload` for asset publishing.
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: a `verify-release-artifacts` job (needs: build) runs a script asserting the exact expected artifact set exists (versioned NSIS `.exe`, `.exe.sig` updater signature, `latest.json`) and fails the workflow otherwise — no silent partial releases. zizmor+actionlint (required checks) lint the workflow itself; harness Stop-gate hook refuses to end a turn if the workflow YAML fails those linters locally.

recommendations.practice: Treat Windows minutes as 2x-billed scarce resource: run lint/typecheck/unit tests on ubuntu-latest for every PR; run the Windows bundle job only on release tags, `workflow_dispatch`, and a nightly schedule that keeps caches warm. Cache Rust with Swatinem/rust-cache@v2 (or mozilla-actions/sccache-action with SCCACHE_GHA_ENABLED=true, RUSTC_WRAPPER=sccache) and cache the pnpm store explicitly.
recommendations.rationale: Since GitHub's 2026-01-01 pricing change, Windows 2-core runners bill $0.010/min and drain included minutes at 2x (Linux 1x, macOS 10x). A cold Tauri 2 Windows build is 15–25 wall-clock minutes (30–50 billed); with a warm rust-cache it drops to ~5–10. GitHub Actions caches are evicted after 7 days and capped at 10 GB per repo, so a scheduled nightly Windows build is what keeps the cache hit rate real. rust-cache keys on Cargo.lock and prunes target/; sccache is finer-grained but shares the same 10 GB pool — pick one, not both, or they thrash the quota.
recommendations.tooling: Swatinem/rust-cache@v2 OR mozilla-actions/sccache-action@v0.0.x; pnpm/action-setup@v4 with `cache: true` (v4.3.0+ built-in store caching) or actions/cache@v4 keyed on hashFiles('pnpm-lock.yaml') over `pnpm store path`; workflow `on: push: tags: ['v*']` + `schedule` for the Windows job.
recommendations.enforcement: Lint script in CI (and as a Claude Code pre-write hook on .github/workflows/*) that fails if: any job lacks `timeout-minutes`, any `windows-*` job is triggered by plain `pull_request`, or any job lacks a `concurrency` group with cancel-in-progress. actionlint validates runner labels; monthly billing is watched via the Actions usage report, but the mechanical control is trigger topology enforced by the linter.

recommendations.practice: Sign the installer and inner exe in CI via Azure Trusted Signing — renamed 'Artifact Signing' in January 2026 — wired through Tauri's `bundle.windows.signCommand` so Tauri signs every binary it bundles (exe inside NSIS + the installer itself), not via a post-hoc action step.
recommendations.rationale: `signCommand` (e.g. `trusted-signing-cli -e https://weu.codesigning.azure.net -a <acct> -c <profile> %1`, or the newer `artifact-signing-cli`) is invoked by Tauri per file during bundling, so nothing ships unsigned inside the installer. The Azure action (azure/trusted-signing-action, successor azure/artifact-signing-action) only runs on Windows runners and signs files after the fact — acceptable as a fallback, worse as primary. Only a 'Public Trust' certificate profile avoids SmartScreen warnings. Authenticate the service principal with Entra ID workload-identity federation (OIDC) instead of a long-lived AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET where possible; scope AZURE_* and TAURI_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY(_PASSWORD) secrets to a protected `release` GitHub environment so PR builds can never reach them. Note this is the one unavoidable external cloud dependency in an otherwise on-prem stack; the alternative is an EV cert on an on-prem HSM with signtool via the same signCommand hook.
recommendations.tooling: trusted-signing-cli (cargo install) or dotnet `sign` CLI; azure/artifact-signing-action (rename of azure/trusted-signing-action, Jan 2026); Entra role 'Trusted Signing Certificate Profile Signer'; GitHub Environments with required reviewers; Tauri updater keypair via `tauri signer generate` for the separate updater signature (.sig).
recommendations.enforcement: Hard CI gate after bundling: PowerShell step runs `Get-AuthenticodeSignature` / `signtool verify /pa /all` on every produced .exe and fails on any unsigned or invalidly-signed file — signing can never silently no-op. Environment protection rules mechanically deny secret access to non-tag workflows; zizmor flags secrets exposure in PR-triggered workflows. Losing TAURI_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY bricks the update channel, so a CI preflight asserts the secret decrypts before the 20-minute build starts.

recommendations.practice: Attest build provenance for every release artifact with actions/attest-build-provenance@v4 (a wrapper over actions/attest — use actions/attest directly for new code), and verify the attestation in a separate required job before the release is published.
recommendations.rationale: Attestation binds the signed .exe digest to the exact repo+workflow+commit via Sigstore, giving SLSA v1.0 Build Level 2 out of the box; Build Level 3 additionally requires the build steps to live in an org-level reusable workflow (vetted build definition outside the repo) — achievable for this harness by shipping the Windows build as a reusable workflow in the harness org repo that template instances call. Caveat for an on-prem medical-school org: attestations on private repos require GitHub Enterprise Cloud; public template repo gets it free. GitHub's Jan 2026 changelog extends this with code-to-cloud artifact traceability.
recommendations.tooling: actions/attest-build-provenance@v4 (v4.1.1, Jun 2026) or actions/attest; permissions: `id-token: write`, `attestations: write`, `contents: read`; verification via `gh attestation verify <file> --repo <org>/<repo>`; optional org reusable workflow for SLSA L3.
recommendations.enforcement: The publish job `needs:` a verify job that downloads the artifact and runs `gh attestation verify` — release upload is unreachable if verification fails. Repo ruleset marks the verify job a required status check on the release branch. Document (and script) the same `gh attestation verify` command for IT staff installing the .exe on hospital-network machines.

recommendations.practice: Generate CycloneDX SBOMs for BOTH ecosystems at release time — cdxgen (or @cyclonedx/cyclonedx-npm) for the pnpm workspace and cargo-cyclonedx for the Rust host — publish them as release assets and bind them to the installer with actions/attest-sbom.
recommendations.rationale: A medical-school procurement/security review will ask for an SBOM. CycloneDX 1.7 is the current spec line (July 2026). cdxgen (@cyclonedx/cdxgen v12.x, official OWASP generator) natively understands pnpm-lock.yaml and monorepos, which plain cyclonedx-npm handles less well; cargo-cyclonedx covers Cargo.lock including the Tauri host and its build-script dependency tree. Attesting the SBOM (attest-sbom uses the same Sigstore machinery) makes the SBOM tamper-evident and queryable via gh CLI, closing the loop with the provenance attestation.
recommendations.tooling: @cyclonedx/cdxgen v12.x (or @cyclonedx/cyclonedx-npm) for pnpm; cargo-cyclonedx (CycloneDX/cyclonedx-rust-cargo) for crates; actions/attest-sbom@v3; CycloneDX 1.7 JSON output.
recommendations.enforcement: Release workflow fails if either SBOM file is missing or empty (same artifact-set gate as recommendation 1 — SBOMs are in the expected-asset manifest). A scheduled job diffs SBOM component counts against lockfile package counts and fails on gross mismatch (catches generator silently skipping a workspace). attest-sbom failure fails the release.

recommendations.practice: SHA-pin every `uses:` to a full 40-char commit SHA with a `# vX.Y.Z` trailing comment; let Renovate (config:best-practices, which includes helpers:pinGitHubActionDigests) maintain the pins with a minimumReleaseAge cooldown; enable the GitHub org Actions policy that requires full-SHA pinning and prefer actions publishing immutable releases.
recommendations.rationale: The tj-actions/changed-files compromise made tag-pinning indefensible; SHA-pinning is 2026 baseline. Renovate's digest pinning keeps pins fresh without manual toil, and `minimumReleaseAge` (e.g. 5–7 days) is the mechanical defense against a freshly-poisoned release being auto-adopted. GitHub shipped org/repo-level policy enforcement for SHA pinning + action blocking (Aug 2025) and immutable releases went GA (Oct 2025); GitHub's 2026 Actions security roadmap adds dependency locking and egress controls on top. The trailing version comment is load-bearing: Renovate and humans correlate the SHA to a semver.
recommendations.tooling: Renovate (renovate.json: extends ["config:best-practices"], minimumReleaseAge "5 days") — the sibling harness repo already does exactly this; GitHub org setting 'Require actions to be pinned to a full-length commit SHA'; immutable releases on the template repo itself.
recommendations.enforcement: Three independent mechanical layers: (1) zizmor's `unpinned-uses` audit fails the required lint check on any non-SHA ref; (2) the GitHub org policy rejects unpinned actions at run submission time; (3) a Claude Code PreToolUse write-guard hook on `.github/workflows/**` rejects edits introducing `uses: x/y@v` tag refs before they ever reach CI.

recommendations.practice: Lint workflows themselves with actionlint + zizmor as required PR checks, running zizmor with the `pedantic` persona and SARIF upload; stage template-embedded workflows (stored dotless, e.g. `template/**/github/workflows/*.yml`) into a fixture `.github/workflows/` dir so they get linted without being executable.
recommendations.rationale: actionlint (v1.7.12, Mar 2026) catches schema/shellcheck/expression errors including new runner labels and YAML anchors; zizmor (Trail of Bits-hardened in a May 2026 audit against 41k real workflows) catches security-class issues: template injection, cache poisoning, unpinned uses, excessive permissions, `persist-credentials`, secrets-in-PR-context. For a harness repo that SHIPS workflows as template content, the dotless-storage + stage-for-lint pattern (already used in the sibling repo's lint.yml) is essential: template workflows must never run in the harness repo but must still be linted to the same bar.
recommendations.tooling: rhysd/actionlint v1.7.12 via raven-actions/actionlint@v2 (SHA-pinned); zizmorcore/zizmor-action@v0.5.x with `persona: pedantic` and GH Advanced Security SARIF upload; lefthook pre-commit hook running local `actionlint` + `zizmor --persona pedantic` binaries.
recommendations.enforcement: Both jobs are required status checks in the branch ruleset. lefthook pre-commit blocks the commit locally; the Claude Code Stop gate includes `actionlint && zizmor .github/ template/` in the validate chain so the agent cannot end a turn with a failing workflow lint.

recommendations.practice: Put step-security/harden-runner@v2 as the first step of every job: `egress-policy: block` with an explicit `allowed-endpoints` list on Linux jobs; `egress-policy: audit` on Windows jobs (block mode not yet available there).
recommendations.rationale: Harden-Runner shipped Windows + macOS support on 2026-02-25 — same action, same syntax — but Windows launched audit-only: network egress monitoring works (Community tier, free for public repos), while block mode, file-integrity monitoring, and process events (Enterprise) are 'coming soon'/paid. Egress blocking on Linux jobs is the practical defense against exfiltration if any pinned action is later compromised; on Windows, audit mode still gives you the forensic baseline of expected endpoints (crates.io, static.crates.io, registry.npmjs.org, *.codesigning.azure.net, login.microsoftonline.com, github.com) so anomalies are visible.
recommendations.tooling: step-security/harden-runner@v2.x (SHA-pinned; sibling repo pins v2.20.0); Community tier for the public template repo.
recommendations.enforcement: A workflow-lint script (run in the same required lint job, and mirrored as a harness pre-write hook) parses every workflow and fails if any job's first step is not harden-runner, if a Linux job uses `audit` without a documented waiver comment, or if a Windows job claims `block` (currently unsupported — would silently downgrade).

recommendations.practice: Cover BOTH ecosystems with layered scanners, each a required check: cargo-deny (advisories + license allowlist + banned crates + duplicate versions + registry sources) for crates; osv-scanner reusable workflows scanning pnpm-lock.yaml AND Cargo.lock in PR-diff mode (blocks only newly introduced vulns) plus a weekly full scan; CodeQL for javascript-typescript, rust (GA since Oct 2025, buildless), and actions; gitleaks for secrets.
recommendations.rationale: cargo-audit alone misses policy (licenses/bans); cargo-deny 0.19.x enforces it and RustSec advisories feed OSV, so osv-scanner v2.3.x gives one cross-ecosystem net over npm + crates while cargo-deny adds Rust-specific policy depth. osv-scanner's PR-diff reusable workflow is the right merge gate (no legacy-debt blockage); the scheduled full scan catches newly disclosed CVEs in existing deps. CodeQL Rust went GA in CodeQL 2.23.3 (Oct 2025) with buildless scanning, so the Tauri host gets SAST without a Windows build. gitleaks-action@v2 requires a GITLEAKS_LICENSE key for org-owned repos and its Node 20 runtime dies when GitHub removes Node 20 from runners on 2026-09-16 — run the MIT-licensed gitleaks binary directly instead.
recommendations.tooling: EmbarkStudios/cargo-deny-action@v2 (cargo-deny 0.19.5) with committed deny.toml; google/osv-scanner-action reusable workflows @v2.3.8 (osv-scanner-reusable-pr.yml + osv-scanner-reusable.yml); github/codeql-action@v4 with languages: javascript-typescript, rust, actions; gitleaks binary (MIT) via `gitleaks git --redact` in CI + `gitleaks protect --staged` in lefthook.
recommendations.enforcement: All four are required status checks in branch rulesets; osv PR workflow needs `security-events: write` and blocks merge via protected-branch requirement; deny.toml license allowlist (e.g. MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC only) makes license policy a hard failure, not a review comment; CodeQL alerts gate via code-scanning merge protection (fail on high+); lefthook runs gitleaks pre-commit so secrets rarely reach CI at all.

recommendations.practice: Automate releases with release-please (googleapis/release-please-action@v4) driven by the conventional commits that commitlint already enforces: merge of the release PR creates the tag; the tag triggers the hardened Windows build/sign/attest/publish workflow; mark published releases immutable. Reserve changesets for the case where packages/schema|importer|eval are published to an internal npm registry with independent versions.
recommendations.rationale: For a template/installer repo the release unit is 'one versioned signed installer + template snapshot', not N independently-versioned npm packages — release-please's conventional-commit-driven release PR fits exactly, supports Rust + Node + generic files in one manifest (so tauri.conf.json / Cargo.toml / package.json versions stay in lockstep via extra-files), and adds zero contributor ceremony beyond commit style the harness already gates via commitlint+lefthook. changesets is superior only for independently-versioned JS package publishing (its monorepo versioning is the most mature), which is optional here. Splitting 'cut release' (release-please, minimal perms) from 'build+sign' (tag-triggered, environment-gated) keeps signing secrets out of the version-bump workflow entirely.
recommendations.tooling: googleapis/release-please-action@v4 with manifest config (release-type: node root + extra-files for Cargo.toml/tauri.conf.json); commitlint + lefthook commit-msg hook; GitHub immutable releases (GA Oct 2025) on the repo; optional changesets/action for internal npm publishing.
recommendations.enforcement: commitlint runs in lefthook commit-msg AND as a CI job on PR titles/commits (squash-merge safe) — malformed commits can't merge, so versioning can't drift. The tag-triggered release workflow is the ONLY workflow with access to the `release` environment (signing secrets); a ruleset restricts tag creation matching `v*` to the release-please bot + admins, so nobody can trigger a signed build from an unreviewed ref. Immutable releases prevent post-publish asset swaps of the installer.

pitfalls: tauri-action convenience trap: it bundles build+upload into one opaque third-party JS action, requires handing it signing env vars and a release-writing token, and has known breakage at the signing seam (tauri-apps/tauri#13991 closed 'not planned') — you lose the ability to insert verify-signature/attest/SBOM gates between build and publish.

Windows minutes burn 2x: under the Jan 2026 pricing, a cold 20-minute Tauri Windows build costs 40 billed minutes; running the Windows bundle job on every PR of an active repo can exhaust a free/team quota in days. Gate PRs on Linux, build Windows on tags + nightly only — and remember GitHub Actions caches evict after 7 days, so without the nightly the 'cached' release build is actually cold.

pnpm caching that silently never hits: setup-node's `cache: pnpm` without `cache-dependency-path` pointing at the root pnpm-lock.yaml, or an unpinned pnpm store-dir (which differs on Windows), yields 0% cache hits with green checkmarks. Use pnpm/action-setup@v4.3.0+ `cache: true` or cache `pnpm store path` explicitly, and assert cache-hit in a debug step.

harden-runner on Windows is audit-only as of mid-2026 (block mode, file monitoring, process events pending/Enterprise) — writing `egress-policy: block` on a windows job does not give Linux-parity protection; treat Windows jobs as unblocked-egress and keep signing/publish steps there minimal.

gitleaks-action@v2 has two time bombs: org-owned repos need a GITLEAKS_LICENSE key (free for exactly 1 repo), and its Node 20 runtime stops working when GitHub removes Node 20 from hosted runners on 2026-09-16. Invoke the MIT-licensed gitleaks binary directly in CI instead of the action.

Attestation over-claiming: actions/attest-build-provenance gives SLSA v1.0 Build L2 by default — L3 requires the build definition to live in an org-level reusable workflow; and attestations on private/internal repos require GitHub Enterprise Cloud (public repos: all plans). Don't write 'SLSA 3' in the README unless the reusable-workflow architecture is actually in place.

Azure Trusted Signing was renamed 'Artifact Signing' in January 2026: azure/trusted-signing-action is being superseded by azure/artifact-signing-action, endpoints/roles are being renamed, and the action runs only on Windows runners. Pin the exact action SHA, budget for a rename migration, and note only 'Public Trust' certificate profiles suppress SmartScreen — an internal-trust profile will still scare end users.

Cache-stack thrash: running Swatinem/rust-cache AND sccache-with-GHA-backend together competes for the same 10 GB per-repo cache quota and can evict each other plus the pnpm store cache; pick one Rust caching strategy per job and monitor cache sizes, or the 'optimized' pipeline degrades to cold builds nondeterministically.

topic: World-class self-hosted Postgres 16 + RLS + Drizzle quality patterns (plain on-prem Postgres, no Supabase), July 2026 — doctrine, testing, migration discipline, pgvector, property-graph patterns, and mechanical enforcement

recommendations.practice: ENABLE + FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY on every table in the app schema, with per-operation policies (separate FOR SELECT / INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE policies, each with explicit USING and, for writes, WITH CHECK) — never one permissive FOR ALL policy.
recommendations.rationale: ENABLE alone lets the table owner bypass policies; if migrations run as the owner role that also serves traffic, RLS is decorative. FORCE applies policies even to the owner (superusers and BYPASSRLS roles still bypass — see role rec). Per-operation policies are required because USING and WITH CHECK have different semantics: a single FOR ALL policy typically leaves writes governed only by the read predicate, letting a user INSERT rows they could never read back or UPDATE rows into another tenant.
recommendations.tooling: Postgres 16 (16.x supported until Nov 2028); Drizzle ORM 0.45.x or 1.0.0-beta (pgPolicy supports as/to/for/using/withCheck); generated SQL contains ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY — the harness must append FORCE (Drizzle's .enableRLS() does not emit FORCE; add it via a custom migration statement).
recommendations.enforcement: CI job `rls-catalog-gate` runs against the migrated test database: (1) `SELECT relname FROM pg_class c JOIN pg_namespace n ON ... WHERE n.nspname='app' AND relkind='r' AND (NOT relrowsecurity OR NOT relforcerowsecurity)` must return zero rows; (2) coverage query over pg_policies asserting every RLS table has policies whose polcmd set covers r/a/w/d (or explicit allowlist file, diffed in review). Claude Code pre-write hook rejects any new migration .sql containing CREATE TABLE without a matching ENABLE+FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY statement in the same file.

recommendations.practice: Two-tier database role architecture with fail-closed request identity: `migrator` (table owner, LOGIN, used only by the migration runner) and `app_api` (LOGIN, NOSUPERUSER, NOBYPASSRLS, NOCREATEROLE, never owns tables) used by the Hono server. Policies key off `current_setting('app.user_sub', true)` (Entra ID `oid`/`sub` claim) and optionally `app.user_roles`, set per request. Explicit GRANTs + ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES (newer Postgres images drop implicit public grants — already bitten in this repo's history), REVOKE CREATE ON SCHEMA public.
recommendations.rationale: With no Supabase auth layer, the DB cannot see the JWT; the API server validates the Entra ID token (issuer/audience/signature) and projects the immutable subject claim into a transaction-local GUC. Using `current_setting(name, missing_ok=>true)` returns NULL when context is unset, so every policy predicate (`user_sub = current_setting('app.user_sub', true)`) evaluates false → zero rows: fail-closed by construction, no COALESCE defaults.
recommendations.tooling: openid-client or jose (Node 22) for Entra ID OIDC validation in Hono middleware; roles declared in Drizzle via pgRole()/.existing() so they appear in migrations; pg 8.x (node-postgres) Pool connecting as app_api only.
recommendations.enforcement: CI SQL assertions after migrate: `SELECT rolsuper OR rolbypassrls OR rolcreaterole FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname='app_api'` must be false; `SELECT count(*) FROM pg_tables WHERE schemaname='app' AND tableowner='app_api'` must be 0. Hygiene script (extend existing scripts/hygiene.mjs) fails the build if server code contains the migrator DSN or `postgres://` with the migrator user outside the migration runner. A pgTAP/integration test runs a query as app_api with NO context set and asserts zero rows (fail-closed test).

recommendations.practice: All tenant-scoped queries go through one DAL wrapper `withUser(sub, roles, fn)` that opens db.transaction(), executes parameterized `SELECT set_config('app.user_sub', $1, true)` (transaction-local, equivalent to SET LOCAL), then runs the Drizzle queries inside that same transaction. Never session-level SET, never string-interpolated `SET app.x = '...'`, never raw pool.query for tenant data.
recommendations.rationale: set_config(..., true)/SET LOCAL resets at transaction end, so context can never leak across pooled connections; session-level SET on a recycled pool connection serves the next request with the previous user's identity. SET LOCAL outside a transaction is a silent no-op (warning only). set_config is an ordinary function so it takes bind parameters — SET does not, which pushes people into SQL-injectable string interpolation of the subject claim. Drizzle itself never switches roles or context per query; its own RLS docs use exactly this transaction+set_config pattern.
recommendations.tooling: drizzle-orm 0.45.x db.transaction() over node-postgres Pool (direct to Postgres — on-prem, no PgBouncer needed at this scale; if a transaction-mode pooler is ever added, SET LOCAL-only discipline already survives it).
recommendations.enforcement: ESLint 9 flat-config: no-restricted-imports bans importing the raw `db`/Pool outside packages/schema/src/dal/**; no-restricted-syntax bans `sql.raw` combined with `set_config`/`SET` and any `set_config(..., false)`. Claude Code PreToolUse write-guard greps staged content for `SET app.` without LOCAL and for `set_config` with third arg false. Leak test in CI: pool max=1, two sequential withUser calls as different subjects, assert second sees only its own rows and `current_setting('app.user_sub', true)` is NULL between transactions.

recommendations.practice: Declare RLS artifacts in Drizzle schema code — pgPolicy() attached to each pgTable, pgRole() for roles — so policies are TypeScript-visible, code-reviewed, and emitted into generated migrations; add a schema-introspection unit test that walks the exported Drizzle schema and fails on any tenant-schema table without an attached policy set.
recommendations.rationale: Drizzle (since drizzle-kit 0.27 / orm 0.36, current 0.45.x and v1 beta) is one of the few TS ORMs where policies are first-class schema objects, which keeps SQL policy text next to the table definition an agent is editing — the harness can lint it statically instead of only post-migration. Limits to respect: Drizzle only defines policies; it does not evaluate or respect them client-side — enforcement happens entirely at the connection role level, so schema declaration and the catalog gate are complementary, not redundant.
recommendations.tooling: drizzle-orm ≥0.45 / drizzle-kit ≥0.31 (pgPolicy, pgRole, .link(), .enableRLS()); vitest unit test importing packages/schema and reflecting over table configs.
recommendations.enforcement: Vitest test `schema-rls-manifest.test.ts` diffs the set of exported pgTable objects against tables carrying pgPolicy definitions plus an explicit `rls-exempt.json` allowlist; any un-listed, un-policied table fails. `drizzle-kit check` runs in CI to catch snapshot/journal corruption or race collisions in generated migrations.

recommendations.practice: Migration discipline: drizzle-kit generate → committed SQL applied by drizzle-kit migrate (or programmatic migrate()) everywhere; drizzle-kit push is banned outside a throwaway local loop. Treat migrations as forward-only (drizzle-kit still has no down migrations — issue #4005 open — and no official squash; squashing is a manual regenerate documented as a runbook, not a habit). Every migration file starts with `SET lock_timeout = '5s'; SET statement_timeout = '60s';` preamble and is linted by Squawk.
recommendations.rationale: push introspects and mutates the live DB with no history and can silently drop columns; generate produces reviewable SQL — the only form an agent harness can gate. Because rollback tooling doesn't exist in drizzle-kit, 'rollback tests' must mean: prove a fresh database reaches HEAD from zero, prove HEAD-1→HEAD applies cleanly, and rely on backups/PITR for reversal — encode that honestly rather than pretending down-scripts exist. Squawk catches lock-hazard DDL (non-concurrent index creation, NOT NULL without default backfill, etc.) that would stall the on-prem single instance.
recommendations.tooling: drizzle-kit ≥0.31 generate/migrate/check; Squawk ≥2.x via sbdchd/squawk-action (posts PR comments); optional eugene (kaaveland/eugene) as a lock-trace second opinion.
recommendations.enforcement: CI: (1) squawk-action on all changed template migration .sql files, failing on error-level rules; (2) `migration-apply` job boots a blank postgres:16 container, runs migrate() to HEAD, then runs `drizzle-kit check` and the rls-catalog-gate; (3) grep gate + lefthook pre-commit rejecting `drizzle-kit push` in package.json scripts, CI YAML, or docs' runnable blocks; (4) migrations directory is append-only — CI diff fails if an already-committed migration file was edited (compare against merge-base).

recommendations.practice: RLS test harness against a real Postgres 16 container with paired negative + seeded positive controls, executed as the actual app role: for every RLS table, (a) user A sees exactly N seeded own rows (positive control — green is never vacuous), (b) user A sees 0 of user B's rows, (c) INSERT/UPDATE crossing the tenant boundary raises SQLSTATE 42501 ('new row violates row-level security policy'), (d) with no context set, all reads return zero rows.
recommendations.rationale: RLS regressions are silent — no error, just wrong row sets — so cross-tenant tests are the only reliable guard; and an empty seed or broken context makes 'sees 0 foreign rows' pass vacuously, which is why every negative assertion must be paired with a positive count against known seeds (this repo's rls_verify already adopted that doctrine — carry it over). Tests must connect as app_api, not postgres, because superuser/BYPASSRLS makes every test pass.
recommendations.tooling: Either pgTAP 1.3.3 + pg_prove 3.36 (pure-SQL, runs inside the container) or TypeScript integration tests with @testcontainers/postgresql (testcontainers-node ≥10) — for this pnpm/vitest monorepo the TS route reuses the withUser() wrapper, testing the real code path; keep one pgTAP smoke file for DBA-level assertions (policies_are(), table_privs_are()). In GitHub Actions use a postgres:16 service container or testcontainers with Ryuk disabled.
recommendations.enforcement: Required status check `rls-verify` in branch protection; the vitest suite auto-derives its table list from the Drizzle schema manifest so a new table without tests fails the manifest diff, not a human's memory; Claude Code Stop-gate includes `pnpm test:rls` in the validate chain so an agent turn cannot end with red isolation tests.

recommendations.practice: Run performance and plan assertions as the app role, never as superuser, and index every column referenced in a policy qual; wrap current_setting() in policies as `(SELECT current_setting('app.user_sub', true))` so the planner evaluates it once per statement (InitPlan) instead of per row.
recommendations.rationale: Query plans differ under RLS: a superuser EXPLAIN bypasses policies entirely, so latency measured as postgres is fiction. Policy quals execute per-row unless the planner can treat them as stable initplans; un-indexed tenant/user columns turn every policy check into a scan — the classic 'RLS is slow' failure is actually a missing index plus per-row function calls.
recommendations.tooling: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, FORMAT JSON) parsed in vitest; pg_stat_statements enabled in the dev/CI container for budget tracking.
recommendations.enforcement: CI perf job seeds the 10k-node fixture, connects as app_api, runs the top N product queries, and asserts (1) plan JSON contains no Seq Scan on policy-filtered tables above a row threshold and (2) execution time under a stated CI budget (generous, e.g. 500ms, to absorb runner noise — catches order-of-magnitude regressions, not 10%). A policy-lint script reads pg_policies.qual text and fails any policy calling current_setting without the (SELECT ...) wrapper.

recommendations.practice: Pin pgvector ≥ 0.8.2 (0.8.2, Feb 2026, fixes CVE-2026-3172 — buffer overflow in parallel HNSW builds that can leak data from other relations or crash the server), use HNSW with explicit m/ef_construction, enable `hnsw.iterative_scan = relaxed_order` for policy/metadata-filtered similarity queries, and make the typed `vector(N)` column the embedding-dimension contract, verified end-to-end against the llama.cpp endpoint.
recommendations.rationale: 0.8.0 introduced iterative index scans precisely for the filtered-query over-filtering problem this app will hit (RLS + metadata filters shrink candidate sets below k); defaults m=16/ef_construction=64 are the right start. vector(N) rejects wrong-length inserts at the database, but nothing checks that the model behind the OpenAI-compatible llama.cpp endpoint still emits N dims after a model swap — that contract needs a live test. HNSW on `vector` caps at 2000 dims; halfvec (up to 4000) is the documented escape hatch.
recommendations.tooling: pgvector 0.8.2+ compiled into the pinned postgres:16 image (verify by digest); drizzle-orm vector column type with explicit dimensions; vitest contract test hitting llama.cpp /v1/embeddings.
recommendations.enforcement: CI: `SELECT extversion FROM pg_extension WHERE extname='vector'` asserted ≥ 0.8.2 (fails loud on image drift; Renovate pins the image digest); dimension contract test embeds a fixture string and asserts response length === schema constant imported from packages/schema (single source of truth); catalog check that every vector column has an HNSW index and its typmod ≤ 2000 (or is halfvec); EXPLAIN assertion that the similarity query uses the HNSW index.

recommendations.practice: Property graph at 10k nodes: one `edges` table (src, dst, edge_type, properties jsonb) with composite btree indexes on (src, dst) and (dst, src); traversals via recursive CTE with a mandatory depth cap and cycle guard (Postgres 14+ SEARCH/CYCLE clause or explicit visited-path array); materialize the coverage-matrix rollup (node × outcome reachability counts) as a materialized view or maintained closure table refreshed with REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY on write, rather than recomputing per page load.
recommendations.rationale: At 10k nodes / bounded depth, recursive CTEs are comfortably sub-second if indexed — the horror stories start at hundreds of thousands of nodes because Postgres's recursive executor keeps no visited set and re-derives duplicate paths exponentially. The coverage matrix is the one query shape that aggregates many traversals at once; materializing it is what guarantees the <1s render budget regardless of graph shape drift. Skip Apache AGE/ltree — plain adjacency + CTE + materialization is simpler, RLS-compatible, and sufficient at this scale.
recommendations.tooling: Postgres 16 recursive CTE with CYCLE ... SET ... USING clause; matview + trigger- or job-based refresh (pg_cron optional but plain app-triggered refresh is fine on-prem).
recommendations.enforcement: CI perf gate (same job as the app-role plan tests): seeded 10k-node/edges fixture, coverage query asserted under budget and traversal queries asserted to use the composite indexes. SQL review hook (Claude pre-write) rejects `WITH RECURSIVE` in app code lacking both a depth predicate and a CYCLE/visited guard. Freshness test asserts the matview refresh path runs after graph mutations (write → refresh → read shows new edge).

recommendations.practice: Close the view/materialized-view RLS bypass: every plain view in the app schema must be created WITH (security_invoker = true) (PG15+; otherwise views execute with the owner's privileges and silently bypass RLS), and materialized views — which cannot have RLS at all — must never be directly SELECT-able by app_api unless on an explicit reviewed allowlist (they either contain only non-tenant aggregate data or are filtered through a wrapper view/API check).
recommendations.rationale: This is the most common way FORCE-RLS shops still leak data: a convenience view or the coverage-matrix matview created by the migrator role reads as owner, and every tenant sees everything. Making invoker semantics and matview grants a catalog-audited invariant removes the trap from human/agent memory.
recommendations.tooling: Postgres 16 security_invoker view option; the coverage matview from the graph rec is the first entry on the allowlist with a documented justification (aggregate counts only, or tenant column + wrapper view filter).
recommendations.enforcement: rls-catalog-gate extended: (1) fail if any view in app schema lacks security_invoker=true (check pg_class.reloptions); (2) fail if app_api has SELECT on any matview (information_schema.role_table_grants ∩ pg_matviews) not present in `matview-allowlist.json`; plus an integration test querying the matview path as two different users asserting the tenant-filtered wrapper behaves like an RLS table.

pitfalls: ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY without FORCE: the table owner bypasses all policies, so if the app or tests connect as the owning role, RLS is silently inert. Superusers and BYPASSRLS roles bypass even FORCE — any RLS test suite that connects as `postgres` passes vacuously.

Session-level SET (or set_config(..., false)) for tenant context on pooled connections leaks the previous request's identity to the next request sharing the backend; and SET LOCAL outside an explicit transaction is a no-op that emits only a warning — RLS then sees NULL context and returns zero rows, which reads as 'no data' rather than 'bug'.

Vacuous green in RLS tests: with an empty or wrong seed, 'tenant A sees none of tenant B's rows' passes even when RLS is completely broken. Every negative isolation assertion needs a paired positive control ('A sees exactly N of its own seeded rows') or the whole suite proves nothing.

Assuming drizzle-kit has rollback or squash: there are no down migrations (issue #4005 open as of 2026) and no official squash command — a harness that promises 'apply+rollback tests' must define rollback as fresh-DB reproducibility plus backup/PITR, and editing an already-applied migration file corrupts the journal/snapshot chain.

drizzle-kit push against any shared environment: no history, no review artifact, and it can silently drop columns whose schema entries were removed — it must be mechanically banned (grep/CI), not just discouraged in docs.

Views and materialized views around RLS: plain views default to owner ('security definer') semantics and bypass RLS unless created WITH (security_invoker=true); matviews cannot carry RLS policies at all. A coverage-matrix matview granted to the app role is a full cross-tenant leak.

pgvector before 0.8.2 carries CVE-2026-3172 (parallel HNSW build buffer overflow, data leak/crash); separately, filtered similarity queries under-return rows unless iterative scans (hnsw.iterative_scan, 0.8.0+) are enabled, and HNSW on `vector` refuses >2000 dims — teams discover this at the first embedding-model swap unless a dimension contract test exists.

Recursive CTEs on a property graph (not a tree) without a cycle guard recurse until the working set explodes — Postgres keeps no visited set and re-derives duplicate paths exponentially; and plans/latency measured as superuser are fiction because RLS quals are absent from the superuser's plan — all graph perf budgets must be measured as the app role.

topic: World-class Hono + Node 22 API server engineering (2026) for an on-prem deployment: layered app structure with a server-only DAL, typed contracts, SSE for LLM streaming, Entra ID OIDC for a Tauri desktop client, pino/OTel/Langfuse observability, graceful shutdown, systemd packaging, and on-prem secrets — with mechanical enforcement for an agent harness

recommendations.practice: Structure the Hono app as modular routers mounted via app.route() (no 'controller' classes), with every handler written inline/chained for RPC type inference, and ALL data access + authorization living in a single server-only DAL directory (e.g. apps/server/src/dal/**). Routes may only call DAL functions; DAL is the only code allowed to import drizzle-orm or the db client.
recommendations.rationale: Hono's official Best Practices doc explicitly says 'don't make Rails-like controllers' because separating handlers from route definitions breaks path/type inference for the hc RPC client. Centralizing authz in the DAL means an agent editing a route file physically cannot bypass permission checks — the query surface isn't importable there. This mirrors the sibling repo's Next.js DAL doctrine and makes the boundary auditable in one directory.
recommendations.tooling: hono 4.12.x (latest 4.12.28, July 2026); @hono/node-server pinned >=1.13 (earlier versions fall back to a slow Web-Streams shim, ~12% throughput loss on Node 22); eslint-plugin-boundaries v5 (element types: routes, dal, lib) on ESLint 9 flat config; dependency-cruiser v16+ as a second independent checker.
recommendations.enforcement: (1) ESLint: boundaries/element-types rule forbidding routes/** → drizzle-orm, ../db, pg imports; no-restricted-imports with paths ['drizzle-orm','@repo/schema/tables'] scoped to everything except src/dal/**. (2) CI gate: depcruise --validate .dependency-cruiser.cjs with a forbidden rule 'routes-must-not-touch-db' — fails the build independently of ESLint. (3) Claude Code PreToolUse hook (pre-write guard): deny Write/Edit that introduces /\b(db\.|drizzle|\$inferSelect)/ into apps/server/src/routes/**. (4) Stop gate runs pnpm validate which includes both linters.

recommendations.practice: Use @hono/zod-openapi (OpenAPIHono + createRoute) as the single contract layer: every endpoint declares Zod v4 request/response schemas, the app exports AppType for the hc typed RPC client consumed by the Tauri SPA, and the generated openapi.json is committed and treated as a build artifact.
recommendations.rationale: This gives three things from one definition: runtime validation, an end-to-end typed client (RPC mode works through OpenAPIHono), and an OpenAPI spec for docs/codegen. As of 2026, @hono/zod-openapi 1.x supports Zod v4 only (v3 stuck on 0.x), so the whole monorepo must standardize on Zod 4. Hono docs note RPC type inference requires strict:true in BOTH server and client tsconfig.
recommendations.tooling: @hono/zod-openapi ^1.4 (Zod v4-only); zod ^4; hono/client (hc) in apps/desktop; @scalar/hono-api-reference for self-hosted API docs; tsc --noEmit in every package.
recommendations.enforcement: (1) CI contract-drift gate: a script renders the OpenAPI doc to openapi.json, then git diff --exit-code openapi.json — any route change that doesn't re-commit the spec fails CI. (2) ESLint no-restricted-syntax banning raw app.get/post/put/delete string-path calls inside routes/** (only createRoute-registered handlers allowed; the composition root is the lone exception via overrides). (3) A typecheck smoke file in apps/desktop instantiating hc<AppType> and calling representative endpoints — compile failure = broken contract. (4) tsconfig strict:true asserted by a CI script that parses both tsconfigs and fails if weakened.

recommendations.practice: DAL functions return DTOs, never raw Drizzle rows: each DAL module defines a Zod v4 DTO schema, declares its return type as z.infer<typeof XDto>, and actually runs XDto.strict().parse() (or .pick of allowed columns) on query results at the DAL exit — parse, don't cast.
recommendations.rationale: TypeScript is structural: a Drizzle row with extra columns (password hash, internal flags, embedding vectors) silently satisfies a narrower DTO type, so a type annotation alone leaks data. Runtime .strict() parsing strips/rejects excess properties, guaranteeing the wire shape equals the declared contract. It also decouples client types from packages/schema migrations.
recommendations.tooling: zod ^4 (.strict() / z.object with catchall never); drizzle-orm .select({...}) explicit column projection; expect-type or vitest's expectTypeOf for type-level tests.
recommendations.enforcement: (1) Type-level CI test: expectTypeOf<typeof users.$inferSelect>().not.toMatchTypeOf<UserDto>() — fails if someone widens the DTO to the row shape. (2) ESLint no-restricted-syntax in dal/** banning 'as' assertions on return statements (forces parse over cast). (3) Unit test per DAL function: seed a row with a canary extra column value, assert canary absent from the returned DTO. (4) Claude Code PreToolUse hook denying edits that add 'returning()' or '$inferSelect' as a return type in dal/** without an adjacent Dto.parse call (regex heuristic; the CI tests are the authoritative check).

recommendations.practice: SSE for LLM token streaming with hono/streaming's streamSSE: register stream.onAbort() in every SSE handler, propagate the client's AbortSignal (c.req.raw.signal, or an AbortController fired from onAbort) into the upstream fetch to llama.cpp, await every writeSSE call (backpressure via Web Streams desiredSize under @hono/node-server >=1.13), send a named heartbeat/ping event every ~15s, and end with an explicit done event carrying finish_reason.
recommendations.rationale: Without abort propagation, a user closing the coverage-matrix view leaves llama.cpp generating tokens on-prem GPU/CPU for minutes — the single most expensive resource in this stack. Hono issue #2068 documents handlers hanging when awaiting an already-aborted stream, so abort must be raced, not assumed. Awaited writes are what actually exert backpressure on Node; fire-and-forget writes buffer unboundedly on slow clients.
recommendations.tooling: hono 4.12.x streamSSE; native fetch with AbortSignal to llama.cpp's OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions (stream:true); undici keep-alive agent tuned for the local inference host; eventsource-parser on the Tauri client if not using hc.
recommendations.enforcement: (1) Integration test (vitest + real @hono/node-server on an ephemeral port + a mock llama.cpp server): open SSE, read 2 events, destroy the socket, assert the mock recorded an aborted request within 500ms and the process has no unhandled rejections. (2) ESLint no-restricted-syntax (custom selector) flagging streamSSE callbacks whose body contains no onAbort reference. (3) A proxy-config test asserting SSE routes set Content-Type: text/event-stream, Cache-Control: no-cache, X-Accel-Buffering: no and are excluded from hono compress() middleware (assert on actual response headers). (4) Load smoke in CI: 50 concurrent SSE clients, assert RSS stays bounded (heap snapshot delta threshold).

recommendations.practice: Desktop auth: authorization-code + PKCE (S256) through the SYSTEM browser per RFC 8252 — Tauri opens the Entra authorize URL externally; redirect lands on either a loopback listener (tauri-plugin-oauth spawning http://127.0.0.1:{port}, which Entra supports for public clients) or a custom-scheme deep link via tauri-plugin-deep-link. Use openid-client v6's functional API (discovery(), buildAuthorizationUrl(), authorizationCodeGrant()) and keep device-authorization grant (pollDeviceAuthorizationGrant) as a fallback for locked-down clinical workstations. Register the app as an Entra public client; never embed a client secret in the .exe.
recommendations.rationale: RFC 8252 mandates external user-agents for native apps: the system browser gives phishing-resistant UX, existing SSO cookies, and WebAuthn/Windows Hello support that an embedded webview cannot. PKCE removes the need for a client secret (unextractable from a shipped .exe anyway). openid-client v6 is panva's ground-up rewrite with first-class PKCE and device-flow helpers on native fetch — it runs identically in Node and can be vendored for token refresh in the Rust host via a sidecar if needed.
recommendations.tooling: openid-client v6.x; tauri-plugin-deep-link 2.x + tauri-plugin-oauth (loopback); Windows Credential Manager via keyring crate (or tauri-plugin-stronghold) for refresh-token storage on the Rust side — NOT localStorage in the webview; Entra ID app registration: public client, redirect URIs http://127.0.0.1 + the custom scheme.
recommendations.enforcement: (1) ESLint no-restricted-imports banning @azure/msal-node/msal-browser inside the webview bundle (auth code must not run in the SPA); ban localStorage/sessionStorage token writes via no-restricted-syntax matching /token/i assignments. (2) Unit tests for the PKCE flow with a mocked Entra issuer asserting code_verifier round-trip and state/nonce checks. (3) A CI grep gate over the built frontend bundle asserting no client_secret string and no refresh-token handling code shipped to the webview. (4) gitleaks in lefthook pre-commit + CI for Entra client IDs/secrets in config.

recommendations.practice: Server-side token validation and session: validate Entra v2.0 access tokens with jose (createRemoteJWKSet against https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant}/discovery/v2.0/keys), enforcing iss, aud = your own API's Application ID URI (api://…, never a Graph-audience token), alg RS256, exp/nbf, and tid pinning; then run ALL authorization inside the DAL — every DAL function takes a mandatory first parameter ctx: AuthContext (subject, roles from Entra groups/app roles), and the DAL's transaction wrapper does SET LOCAL ROLE app_user + set_config('app.user_id', ctx.sub, true) so Postgres FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is the backstop for every query.
recommendations.rationale: Decode-without-verify and wrong-audience acceptance are the two classic Entra API bugs — a token minted for Microsoft Graph must be rejected by your API. Making AuthContext a required positional parameter means an agent cannot write a compilable DAL function that skips authz; RLS with FORCE means even a bug in DAL logic cannot read rows outside the session's scope. jose is the maintained primary library (jsonwebtoken is in maintenance and lacks native JWKS rotation).
recommendations.tooling: jose ^6 (jwtVerify + createRemoteJWKSet with built-in JWKS caching/cooldown); Microsoft Entra access-token claims reference for v2.0 validation rules; drizzle-orm transaction wrapper; postgres 16 FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY.
recommendations.enforcement: (1) Unit tests with jose.generateKeyPair minting tokens: assert rejection on wrong aud, wrong iss, alg:none, expired, wrong tid — a required test file per auth middleware. (2) Custom ESLint rule (or boundaries + no-restricted-syntax) requiring every exported function in dal/** to have first param typed AuthContext; CI fails otherwise. (3) RLS verification suite in CI against ephemeral Postgres (testcontainers): positive control (authorized read succeeds) AND negative control (cross-tenant read returns zero rows) so a disabled policy can't pass vacuously — same pattern as the sibling repo's rls_verify. (4) ESLint no-restricted-imports banning jsonwebtoken repo-wide.

recommendations.practice: Observability: pino v9 as the only logger (child loggers per request with reqId, redact: ['req.headers.authorization','req.headers.cookie','*.token','*.password'] configured centrally), @opentelemetry/sdk-node + @opentelemetry/instrumentation-pino so every log line carries trace_id/span_id, OTLP export to a self-hosted OpenTelemetry Collector on the VM (which fans out to your backend), and Langfuse v3 self-hosted for LLM traces — either via the Langfuse SDK or by pointing OTLP traces at Langfuse's /api/public/otel endpoint so extraction-pipeline spans and HTTP spans correlate.
recommendations.rationale: For a medical-school on-prem deployment, logs must never leak bearer tokens or PII into journald; pino's redact paths do this mechanically at serialization time. The Collector decouples the app from the backend (batching, retry, air-gapped export). Langfuse v3 is the de-facto self-hosted LLM tracing stack (langfuse-web, worker, ClickHouse, Redis, Postgres, MinIO via docker compose) and natively ingests OTLP — note all its stores must run UTC or queries silently return wrong/empty results.
recommendations.tooling: pino ^9; pino-pretty dev-only; @opentelemetry/sdk-node + auto-instrumentations-node + @opentelemetry/instrumentation-pino (contrib); otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib container; Langfuse v3 self-hosted docker compose; stdout → journald (no log files to rotate).
recommendations.enforcement: (1) ESLint no-console: error in apps/server (pino only), plus no-restricted-imports allowing 'pino' only from src/lib/logger.ts so redaction config can't be bypassed. (2) Redaction unit test: log an object containing a fake Entra token and Set-Cookie header, capture the stream, assert '[Redacted]'. (3) CI boot test: start the server with OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT pointing at a collector stub, hit one route, assert a span and a correlated log record (matching trace_id) arrive. (4) Claude Code PreToolUse hook denying console.log insertions into apps/server/src/**.

recommendations.practice: Graceful shutdown + health as first-class code paths: /livez (process alive, no dependencies) and /readyz (Postgres SELECT 1 + llama.cpp /health reachable) endpoints; on SIGTERM — flip readiness to 503, sd_notify STOPPING=1, server.close() to stop new connections, send a terminal SSE event and end open streams within a drain window (e.g. 10s), close the pg pool, await OTel flush, exit 0; hard-kill timer (e.g. 30s) as backstop. Signal READY=1 to systemd only after the DB ping succeeds.
recommendations.rationale: systemd Type=notify makes 'ready' mean actually-serving, so Restart=on-failure and ordered dependencies work correctly, and the watchdog catches event-loop hangs that a liveness HTTP probe on the same process can't. Draining SSE explicitly matters here: LLM streams are long-lived, and killing mid-token corrupts the human-in-the-loop QA UX. Separating live vs ready prevents restart loops when only a dependency (llama.cpp) is down.
recommendations.tooling: sd-notify npm package (or writing to $NOTIFY_SOCKET directly, zero-dep) for READY=1/STOPPING=1/WATCHDOG=1; @hono/node-server's returned http.Server for close(); pg Pool.end(); NodeSDK.shutdown() for OTel flush.
recommendations.enforcement: (1) Integration test: start server, open an SSE stream + one slow request, send SIGTERM, assert in-flight request completes, SSE receives the terminal event, process exits 0 under the deadline; a second test asserts the hard-kill path exits non-zero if a handle leaks. (2) CI hits /readyz and asserts 503 before DB is up and 200 after (testcontainers ordering). (3) systemd-analyze verify on the committed unit file in a Linux CI job. (4) Stop-gate validate chain includes these tests so an agent can't end a turn with a broken shutdown path.

recommendations.practice: Package as a hardened systemd unit with secrets via systemd credentials, not environment variables: Type=notify, WatchdogSec=30, dedicated non-login user, DynamicUser or static user + StateDirectory, hardening block (NoNewPrivileges=yes, ProtectSystem=strict, ProtectHome=yes, PrivateTmp=yes, RestrictAddressFamilies=AF_INET AF_INET6 AF_UNIX, CapabilityBoundingSet=, SystemCallFilter=@system-service — but NOT MemoryDenyWriteExecute, which kills V8's JIT). Secrets (DB password, Entra client config, session-cookie key, Langfuse keys) are provisioned with systemd-creds encrypt (TPM2-sealed where available) and injected via LoadCredentialEncrypted=; the app reads files from $CREDENTIALS_DIRECTORY at boot and refuses env-var secrets in production.
recommendations.rationale: This is the correct on-prem substitute for a cloud KMS: credentials are encrypted at rest (host key and/or TPM2), decrypted only into a per-service ramfs visible solely to that service, never inheritable by child processes, and invisible in /proc/PID/environ, journal dumps, and systemctl show — all of which leak env vars. LoadCredential has been stable since systemd 247; encrypted creds since 250, so Ubuntu 24.04 LTS VMs are fully covered.
recommendations.tooling: systemd 255+ (Ubuntu 24.04); systemd-creds encrypt --name=db-password; LoadCredentialEncrypted= in the unit; a small config loader that resolves secret('db-password') → readFileSync(join(process.env.CREDENTIALS_DIRECTORY, 'db-password')); Node 22 --env-file for dev only.
recommendations.enforcement: (1) Boot-time assertion in code: if NODE_ENV=production and CREDENTIALS_DIRECTORY is unset or any secret arrived via process.env, log fatal and exit 1 (fail-loud, matching the sibling repo's doctrine). (2) CI Linux job runs systemd-analyze verify api.service and a systemd-analyze security api.service score gate (fail if exposure > threshold). (3) ESLint no-restricted-syntax banning process.env.DB_PASSWORD-style secret reads outside src/config/secrets.ts. (4) gitleaks in lefthook pre-commit + CI with custom rules for the repo's credential patterns; the unit file and creds workflow live in infra/ and are covered by the same depcruise no-orphan checks.

recommendations.practice: Runtime and dependency currency discipline: pin Node 22.x everywhere (engines + engine-strict, .node-version, CI matrix) knowing Node 22 entered Maintenance LTS in October 2025 with EOL April 30, 2027 — schedule the Node 24 (Active LTS) migration in the harness's roadmap by Q1 2027; pin @hono/node-server >=1.13; force a single Zod v4 instance monorepo-wide via pnpm.overrides (drizzle-zod and other deps must not drag in Zod v3 — dual instances break instanceof and @hono/zod-openapi 1.x); run Renovate plus osv-scanner/pnpm audit as CI gates; pin GitHub Actions to commit SHAs with least-privilege permissions blocks.
recommendations.rationale: The harness's promises are only as good as its pins: an unpinned @hono/node-server silently costs ~12% throughput on Node 22; a stray Zod v3 transitive dep breaks OpenAPI generation with cryptic type errors; and an on-prem 'zero external cloud' posture makes supply-chain review in CI the only checkpoint before code reaches an air-gapped VM. Node 22's maintenance status is exactly the kind of currency fact a 2026 harness must encode, not assume.
recommendations.tooling: pnpm 10.x with engine-strict=true in .npmrc and pnpm.overrides { "zod": "^4" }; Renovate self-hosted (on-prem friendly); osv-scanner v2 CI step; actionlint + zizmor for workflow linting/hardening; volta or mise for local pinning.
recommendations.enforcement: (1) CI job asserts node --version matches .node-version and fails on drift; engine-strict makes pnpm install fail on wrong Node. (2) A dedupe gate: pnpm why zod must resolve to exactly one version (script fails otherwise). (3) osv-scanner + pnpm audit --prod as blocking CI steps with a documented allowlist file (exceptions expire via a dated comment the CI parses). (4) actionlint + zizmor in CI; a lefthook pre-push hook runs the same so agents see failures before CI does. (5) A scheduled CI workflow fails after 2027-01-31 with 'migrate to Node 24' — a mechanical deadline, not a TODO.

pitfalls: Returning Drizzle rows (or .returning() results) straight from route handlers: TypeScript's structural typing lets a row with extra columns satisfy a narrower DTO annotation, silently leaking password hashes, internal flags, or pgvector embeddings over the wire. Only runtime Zod .strict() parsing at the DAL exit actually strips excess properties — a type cast enforces nothing.

SSE handlers without abort propagation: if stream.onAbort()/c.req.raw.signal never reaches the upstream llama.cpp fetch, closed clients leave the on-prem GPU generating tokens for the full completion; separately, Hono issue #2068 shows a handler can hang forever awaiting an already-aborted stream — abort must be raced with reads, and each writeSSE must be awaited or backpressure is fiction.

Compression and proxy buffering silently break SSE: hono compress() middleware, or nginx/Traefik default buffering in front of the on-prem VM, will hold the token stream until buffer flush — the UI sees nothing, then everything. SSE routes need exclusion from compression, Cache-Control: no-cache, and X-Accel-Buffering: no, verified by a response-header test, not by convention.

Validating the wrong Entra token: accepting a token whose aud is Microsoft Graph (Graph tokens are not third-party-verifiable and are not FOR your API), skipping signature verification (decode-only), or forgetting tid/issuer pinning in a single-tenant app. Your API must register its own Application ID URI, request that scope from the desktop client, and reject everything else in tests.

Relying on session cookies inside the Tauri webview: cookie stores differ per-platform (WebView2 vs WKWebView), tauri:// / custom-scheme origins interact badly with SameSite, and tokens in webview localStorage are readable by any XSS in the SPA. Keep refresh tokens in the Rust host (Windows Credential Manager via keyring) and send short-lived bearer tokens; treat any cookie-based session as server-to-browser only.

Secrets in environment variables or .env files on the prod VM: env vars leak via /proc/PID/environ, systemctl show -p Environment, journal crash dumps, and are inherited by every child process (including spawned llama.cpp sidecars). systemd LoadCredentialEncrypted + $CREDENTIALS_DIRECTORY exists precisely for on-prem, no-KMS deployments — and the app should refuse to boot in production if secrets arrive any other way.

Zod version schism: @hono/zod-openapi 1.x is Zod v4-only, while some transitive deps (older drizzle-zod, community middlewares) still pull Zod v3 — two Zod instances break instanceof checks and produce incomprehensible type errors in createRoute. Force one version with pnpm.overrides and gate on 'pnpm why zod' resolving to a single version.

systemd Type=notify misuse and over-hardening: signaling READY=1 before the DB pool is actually usable causes dependency-ordered services to race and restart-loop; conversely, applying MemoryDenyWriteExecute=yes from generic hardening templates breaks V8's JIT and Node won't start (or degrades to jitless). Also watch Hono RPC compile-time blowup in large monorepos — hundreds of chained routes can multiply tsc times; keep AppType composed from per-router types and require strict:true on both sides or hc types silently degrade to any.

topic: Desktop UX excellence & rendering performance for a data-dense React 19 SPA in a Tauri 2 / WebView2 shell (2026): WCAG 2.2 AA mechanics, 10k-cell matrix rendering <1s, large-graph state management, OKLCH/Tailwind v4 token enforcement, React 19 performance idioms, and CI-encodable performance budgets

recommendations.practice: Render the 10k-node coverage matrix on Canvas 2D (glide-data-grid or a custom canvas layer), never SVG/DOM cells; reserve WebGL for >100k elements
recommendations.rationale: Published 2025-26 benchmarks show SVG drops below 30fps past ~10k elements while canvas draws pixels directly, bypassing layout entirely; glide-data-grid (MIT, React 16-19 tested, canvas-based) handles millions of rows at 60fps with lazy cell rendering, comfortably inside the <1s budget for 10k cells. WebGL wins only at 100k+ where instanced rendering matters, and costs a shader toolchain plus a harder accessibility story.
recommendations.tooling: @glideapps/glide-data-grid (canvas grid, current on npm through 2026); @tanstack/react-virtual v3 (useVirtualizer) for surrounding DOM lists/trees; TanStack Table v8 + react-virtual combo for conventional tabular views
recommendations.enforcement: Architecture lint: ESLint no-restricted-imports/boundaries rule in apps/desktop forbidding SVG-chart libraries (recharts, visx, d3-selection) inside the matrix feature directory; plus the Playwright render-budget test (rec 2) that fails CI if the matrix paints >1s — an SVG implementation cannot pass it at 10k cells, making the choice self-enforcing

recommendations.practice: Instrument user-timing marks (performance.mark/measure) around matrix first-paint and key interactions, and assert budgets in Playwright against the real WebView2 runtime over CDP
recommendations.rationale: Lighthouse is navigation/server-oriented and useless for a desktop SPA; the portable substitute is app-emitted User Timing spans asserted in E2E tests. On Windows, WebView2 is Chromium-based and accepts CDP, so Playwright can drive the actual Tauri shell (launch with WEBVIEW2_ADDITIONAL_BROWSER_ARGUMENTS="--remote-debugging-port=9222", then chromium.connectOverCDP) — numbers come from the shipped engine, not Playwright's bundled Chromium. Run N iterations and assert on the median to tame CI noise.
recommendations.tooling: @playwright/test (1.5x line, 2026) with chromium.connectOverCDP against Edge WebView2; Haprog/tauri-cdp template pattern; app-side performance.mark('matrix:start')/measure('matrix:render'); web-vitals INP polyfilled in-app for interaction latency; optional tauri-plugin-playwright for cross-platform webview control
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: a dedicated perf-budget Playwright project on a Windows runner asserting expect(medianOf(measures)).toBeLessThan(1000) for matrix render and <200ms for hover/filter interactions; wired into the harness Stop-gate validate chain so an agent cannot end a turn with a red perf test; budgets stored as a versioned JSON manifest so loosening a budget is a reviewable diff

recommendations.practice: Encode WCAG 2.2 AA's nine new success criteria as concrete test obligations: 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (AA), 2.5.7 Dragging Movements (AA), 2.5.8 Target Size ≥24px (AA), 3.2.6 Consistent Help (A), 3.3.7 Redundant Entry (A), 3.3.8 Accessible Authentication (AA); 4.1.1 Parsing is removed
recommendations.rationale: WCAG 2.2 (the current W3C Recommendation) added exactly these criteria over 2.1; for a keyboard-first data-dense app the operative ones are focus never hidden behind sticky toolbars (2.4.11), every drag gesture (matrix pan, column reorder) having a single-pointer/keyboard alternative (2.5.7), and 24×24px minimum targets on matrix cell affordances and toolbar icons (2.5.8). Entra ID OIDC login satisfies 3.3.8 without extra work since no cognitive test is imposed.
recommendations.tooling: @axe-core/playwright with tags ['wcag2a','wcag2aa','wcag21aa','wcag22aa']; axe-core 4.x (target-size rule shipped; Deque states most other 2.2 SCs stay manual); eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y (flat-config compatible) for static JSX checks
recommendations.enforcement: Three mechanical layers: (1) ESLint jsx-a11y in the pre-write hook and CI; (2) @axe-core/playwright scan of every route failing CI on any violation at the wcag22aa tag set; (3) targeted Playwright specs — bounding-box assertion that focused elements are never covered by position:sticky overlays (2.4.11), and a getBBox ≥24px assertion over all interactive controls (2.5.8) — because axe covers only ~57% of WCAG issues and almost none of 2.2's new SCs automatically

recommendations.practice: Centralize all keyboard shortcuts in one typed registry that structurally forbids unmodified single-character global shortcuts (WCAG 2.1.4) and provides remap/disable UI; follow the ARIA grid pattern for matrix navigation
recommendations.rationale: SC 2.1.4 Character Key Shortcuts requires single-key shortcuts be disableable, remappable, or active only on focus — otherwise speech-input and screen-reader users trigger them accidentally (NVDA/JAWS consume many single keys in browse mode). A registry whose TypeScript type requires either a modifier (Ctrl/Alt) or a focus scope makes violations unrepresentable; roving tabindex + arrow-key navigation per the WAI-ARIA grid pattern keeps 10k cells reachable without 10k tab stops.
recommendations.tooling: In-house packages/ui shortcut registry (typed union: { modifier: 'ctrl'|'alt'|'meta' } | { scope: FocusScope }); react-aria/Radix primitives underlying shadcn/ui for focus trap + restore; Playwright keyboard-only test suite
recommendations.enforcement: ESLint no-restricted-syntax banning addEventListener('keydown') and useHotkeys outside the registry package (pre-write hook + CI); a unit test iterating the registry asserting no entry is a bare printable character with global scope; Playwright specs asserting focus returns to the trigger element after every dialog/popover closes and that a full keyboard-only traversal of each route matches a committed tab-order snapshot

recommendations.practice: Treat canvas-grid accessibility and the WebView2/Tauri screen-reader stack as a tested surface, not an assumption: rely on glide-data-grid's hidden DOM accessibility tree, keep window decorations enabled, and pin a manual NVDA smoke gate
recommendations.rationale: glide-data-grid mirrors visible cells into a hidden ARIA DOM so screen readers can navigate a canvas grid, but its maintainers explicitly invite a11y bug reports; separately, WebView2 has a history of total screen-reader inaccessibility bugs (MicrosoftEdge/WebView2Feedback#2330) and Tauri 2.3.x introduced an NVDA regression specifically with decorations:false frameless windows (tauri#12901). Chromium-based CI a11y scans cannot see these host-level failures.
recommendations.tooling: glide-data-grid a11y tree; Playwright aria snapshot assertions (expect(locator).toMatchAriaSnapshot) to lock the accessibility tree shape of the grid; NVDA (free) on the Windows test box; Tauri config with decorations:true
recommendations.enforcement: CI JSON-schema check on tauri.conf.json rejecting decorations:false (simple jq/ajv step); ARIA-snapshot Playwright tests versioned in-repo so accessibility-tree regressions show as diffs; a required PR-template checklist item 'NVDA smoke pass on packaged .exe' enforced by a Danger/GitHub Action that blocks merge on release-labelled PRs when the checkbox is absent — the honest boundary of mechanical enforcement for real-AT behavior

recommendations.practice: Adopt React Compiler 1.0 (exact-pinned) with eslint-plugin-react-hooks v6 'recommended-latest', and use useTransition/useDeferredValue for matrix filtering plus React 19.2 <Activity> for pre-rendering hidden views
recommendations.rationale: React Compiler v1.0 (babel-plugin-react-compiler, stable Oct 2025) auto-memoizes and removes most manual useMemo/useCallback, cutting re-render CPU in exactly the many-component data-dense case; its diagnostics moved into eslint-plugin-react-hooks v6 (flat-config default; compiler-powered rules like set-state-in-render via recommended-latest), replacing the deprecated eslint-plugin-react-compiler. useDeferredValue keeps typing responsive while the 10k-cell recompute lags one frame behind; <Activity hidden> (19.2) keeps QA-queue and matrix tabs warm with state preserved; React Performance Tracks appear in Chrome DevTools profiles for diagnosis with zero app changes.
recommendations.tooling: babel-plugin-react-compiler@1.x pinned exact (React team: avoid ^ ranges — memoization strategy shifts can change behavior); @vitejs/plugin-react babel.plugins entry; eslint-plugin-react-hooks@6 flat['recommended-latest']; react@19.2
recommendations.enforcement: ESLint gate (lefthook pre-commit + CI) with recommended-latest treated as errors, so compiler-bailout patterns are build-blocking; a CI script (or npmPackageJsonLintrc rule) asserting babel-plugin-react-compiler has no semver range prefix in package.json; PostToolUse hook running eslint --fix on written .tsx so agents see rule violations immediately

recommendations.practice: Keep the 10k-node graph out of React component state: TanStack Query v5 as the single server-state store, memoized select subscriptions per widget, per-entity query keys instead of a client normalizer, and typed-array cell buffers for the matrix
recommendations.rationale: TanStack Query's structural sharing keeps references stable (saving 50-70% of re-renders on 1000+ item lists) and select lets each component subscribe to a slice, but its own docs warn structural sharing becomes a CPU bottleneck on very large datasets — so the matrix payload should be a Float32Array/Uint8Array buffer with structuralSharing:false and whole-buffer replacement. Full normalization layers (@normy/react-query) exist but per-entity keys (['node',id]) plus targeted setQueryData cover the HITL-QA edit flow with less machinery; TanStack DB is emerging for live normalized collections if requirements grow.
recommendations.tooling: @tanstack/react-query v5; select wrapped in useCallback/stable refs; structuralSharing:false on the matrix query; packages/schema exporting the CellBuffer type; optional @normy/react-query if entity-update fan-out grows
recommendations.enforcement: ESLint boundaries rule forbidding useState/useReducer/createContext holding types from packages/schema graph namespaces in apps/desktop feature code (custom no-restricted-syntax with type-aware matcher, or a GritQL Biome plugin); vitest bench on the aggregation selectors with committed baseline JSON compared in CI at a ±20% threshold; TypeScript max-strict already blocks any-typed cache escapes

recommendations.practice: Define every color exactly once as OKLCH tokens in a Tailwind v4 @theme block, and lint all three escape hatches shut: arbitrary-value classes, raw CSS literals, and inline style props
recommendations.rationale: Tailwind v4 is CSS-first: @theme variables both generate utilities and expose native CSS custom properties, and v4's palette is OKLCH for perceptually uniform scales — ideal for the coverage-heatmap sequential ramp (equal lightness steps read as equal coverage steps). Enforcement must cover class strings (bg-[#ff5733]), stylesheet literals, and style={{color:...}} or the palette erodes; a machine-readable token manifest also lets a unit test verify contrast pairs.
recommendations.tooling: tailwindcss@4 @theme in packages/ui/tokens.css; eslint-plugin-better-tailwindcss (no-unregistered-classes with the v4 CSS entryPoint configured so real tokens pass, no-conflicting-classes, no-duplicate-classes, restricted-class patterns banning [#…]/rgb(/oklch( inside class strings); Biome 2.x useSortedClasses for class ordering; Biome GritQL CSS plugin (or stylelint declaration-property-value-allowed-list) banning hex/rgb()/hsl() literals in .css outside tokens.css; APCA/WCAG contrast unit test over the token manifest
recommendations.enforcement: All rules run in the pre-write PostToolUse hook and as a CI lint gate at error severity; the GritQL/stylelint CSS ban plus an ESLint react/forbid-dom-props-style-color rule close the non-class routes; the contrast unit test (vitest) fails if any documented foreground/background token pair drops below 4.5:1 (or APCA Lc 60), making palette edits self-checking

recommendations.practice: Put hard bundle and startup budgets on the desktop SPA even though it ships in an .exe
recommendations.rationale: WebView2 still parses/executes JS at web speeds; a bloated vendor chunk directly delays first matrix paint and window-open time. Budgets-as-code keep agent-generated dependency creep visible: any PR that grows a chunk past budget fails, forcing an explicit budget bump commit.
recommendations.tooling: size-limit (with @size-limit/file preset) or a Vite build --reportCompressedSize + jq assertion; rollup-plugin-visualizer for diagnosis; manualChunks splitting glide-data-grid/visualization code from the shell
recommendations.enforcement: CI job running size-limit against dist/ with per-chunk byte budgets committed in package.json; fails the required check on exceedance; a second Playwright assertion on window-open→first-interactive user-timing measure (<1.5s on the reference runner) catches startup-cost regressions that size alone misses

recommendations.practice: Track compute-kernel performance with vitest bench baselines compared in CI on a pinned self-hosted runner (CodSpeed-style discipline without the cloud)
recommendations.rationale: The graph-adjacency build, coverage aggregation, and extraction post-processing are pure functions whose regressions predict UI-budget failures earlier and more cheaply than E2E tests. CodSpeed solves CI noise with instrumented runners but is a cloud service — excluded by the on-prem constraint; the self-hosted equivalent is vitest bench's JSON output diffed against a committed baseline on a dedicated runner, with medians and a generous threshold to absorb residual noise.
recommendations.tooling: vitest bench (tinybench-based, vitest 3.x) with --outputJson; a small compare script (assert median ops/sec within 20% of baseline); benchmarks run in a separate process to avoid global-state contamination; a labeled self-hosted GitHub Actions runner reserved for perf jobs
recommendations.enforcement: CI perf-bench job on the pinned runner: run bench → compare to benchmarks/baseline.json → exit non-zero on regression beyond threshold; baseline updates only via explicit commit (reviewable); the harness Stop gate includes the bench suite in the full validate chain for perf-tagged tasks

pitfalls: Rendering matrix cells as SVG/DOM nodes: benchmarks show SVG collapses below 30fps past ~10k elements, so a 10k-cell SVG heatmap structurally cannot meet the <1s/60fps budget — this is the single most likely agent-generated mistake since D3/SVG examples dominate training data.

Treating a green axe-core CI run as WCAG 2.2 AA conformance: axe finds ~57% of WCAG issues overall and deliberately automates almost none of the new 2.2 criteria (Deque expects target-size to be the only 2.2 rule) — focus obscuration, dragging alternatives, and consistent help all need scripted Playwright checks or manual passes.

Validating accessibility and performance only in Playwright's bundled Chromium: the shipped app runs Edge WebView2, which has documented screen-reader failures (WebView2Feedback#2330) and different perf characteristics; connect Playwright over CDP to the real WebView2/Tauri shell for the gating runs.

Tauri decorations:false (frameless windows) triggered an NVDA read-aloud regression from tauri 2.3.x (tauri#12901) — a pure config choice can silently break screen readers with zero test failures in web-level CI.

Single-run perf thresholds on shared CI runners produce flaky gates that teams then delete; assert on medians of N iterations with headroom, or pin a dedicated runner — a deleted perf gate is worse than none because it signals budgets are negotiable.

Installing babel-plugin-react-compiler with a ^ range: the React team explicitly warns memoization-strategy changes across compiler versions can alter app behavior; latent Rules-of-React violations that were harmless uncompiled become real bugs under compilation.

TanStack Query structural sharing (a re-render optimization) inverts into a CPU bottleneck when the payload is the 10k-node graph — the deep-compare walk on every refetch can itself blow the frame budget; disable it for the matrix query and replace typed-array buffers wholesale.

Linting only Tailwind class strings for off-palette colors while leaving raw CSS files and style={{}} props unlinted — agents route around the class-level rule via inline styles or a new .css file, so all three channels need rules or the OKLCH token system erodes within weeks.
