topic: Engineering-quality patterns for local-LLM pipelines and eval harnesses (llama.cpp serving, constrained decoding, HITL) — what the agent harness should scaffold and mechanically enforce, July 2026

recommendations.practice: Pin the llama.cpp server build and use response_format json_schema, but pre-validate every schema's GBNF conversion offline as a CI gate
recommendations.rationale: llama-server's OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions supports response_format {type:'json_schema', schema:...} (and raw 'grammar'/'json_schema' params) via grammar-constrained sampling, but the schema-to-GBNF converter has documented gaps: numeric min/max only for integers, string patterns must be ^...$-anchored, nested $refs broken, additionalProperties defaults to false (non-spec), and unsupported features (uniqueItems, if/then/else, patternProperties, prefixItems) are SILENTLY skipped. There are also historical bugs in response_format handling on the chat endpoint (issues #11847, #11988), and specifying both grammar and json_schema errors. So the exact server build matters.
recommendations.tooling: llama.cpp llama-server pinned by build tag (b-number) or ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server image digest in docker-compose; examples/json_schema_to_grammar.py for offline conversion with warnings visible; llama-gbnf-validator to check produced grammars; Renovate to propose build bumps
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate script scripts/gates/schema-grammar-check.mjs runs json_schema_to_grammar.py over every extraction schema in packages/schema and fails on any converter warning or silently-skipped feature (diff supported-feature allowlist); llama-gbnf-validator run on the emitted grammar; a stack smoke test hits /props and asserts the pinned build number and GGUF model hash; docker image pinned by digest so upgrades are explicit PRs

recommendations.practice: Treat constrained decoding as formatting only — a deterministic validator stage after every LLM call is the trust boundary (schema conformance, closed-set membership, referential integrity)
recommendations.rationale: Grammar constraints guarantee token-level shape, not semantic validity: they cannot express closed-set membership against DB state (valid node types, curriculum-framework codes), referential integrity (cited source spans actually exist in the document; edge endpoints exist in the graph), or cross-field invariants. 2026 practice (Microsoft ISE, schema-guided extraction papers) converges on a three-stage deterministic post-check — syntactic, semantic, textual-fidelity — with LLM output never trusted past that boundary. The DB (FORCE RLS, FKs, CHECKs) is the last net, not the first.
recommendations.tooling: Zod v4 (or ajv 8 for full JSON Schema parity with what the grammar could not enforce) in packages/schema, exported to both pipeline and eval; branded type Validated<T> so raw LLM output is a distinct nominal type; Postgres FKs + CHECK constraints + enum types in Drizzle schema
recommendations.enforcement: Compile-time: pipeline stage signatures accept only Validated<T> (branded), so unvalidated output cannot cross a package boundary — tsc max-strict fails otherwise; ESLint no-restricted-imports so only the validator module may import the raw-response types; unit tests with malformed fixtures assert rejection (including a seeded positive control that MUST fail, preventing vacuous green); DB constraints make invalid inserts impossible even if TS is bypassed

recommendations.practice: Define swappable InferenceProvider / EmbeddingProvider ports in a dedicated package, with adapters as the only code allowed to touch the LLM endpoint
recommendations.rationale: Ports-and-adapters lets the eval harness swap models (llama.cpp model A vs B, or a ReplayProvider serving recorded fixtures) without touching pipeline code. The port should carry structured-output intent (schema, seed, temperature, promptRef) and return raw + parsed + usage + provenance so provenance capture is impossible to forget. Aligning the adapter layer with Vercel AI SDK's LanguageModelV3 spec (@ai-sdk/provider, AI SDK v6+) buys the middleware/mock ecosystem, but keep your own narrower domain port on top so pipeline code never depends on the SDK directly.
recommendations.tooling: packages/llm-port (interfaces + branded types); packages/llm-adapters with LlamaCppProvider (OpenAI-compatible, /v1/chat/completions + /v1/embeddings), ReplayProvider (fixtures), FakeProvider (tests); optionally @ai-sdk/provider LanguageModelV3 + @ai-sdk/openai-compatible under the adapter; EmbeddingProvider declares model id + dimensions and the pgvector column dimension is derived from it
recommendations.enforcement: dependency-cruiser (or eslint-plugin-boundaries) rule: only packages/llm-adapters may import the HTTP client or reference LLM_BASE_URL; Claude Code PreToolUse write-guard hook greps staged writes for inference-endpoint literals (localhost:8080, /v1/chat/completions) outside the adapters package and blocks the write; a shared vitest contract-test suite that every adapter must pass (run in CI against Fake + Replay always, against live llama.cpp only in nightly)

recommendations.practice: Eval harness with a stratified holdout, per-axis precision/recall/F1, and checked-in metric baselines keyed by prompt_version_id
recommendations.rationale: A single aggregate score hides regressions: stratify the gold set by document type/discipline/node type and report P/R/F1 separately per extraction axis (entities, edges, framework mappings). Baselines must be versioned artifacts in the repo so a metric drop is a reviewable diff, not a dashboard memory. 2026 consensus: eval-gated promotion — a prompt/model change must beat or match baseline on the labeled set before merge.
recommendations.tooling: evalite v1 (TypeScript-native, vitest-based, .eval.ts files, custom scorers, trace UI) or promptfoo (declarative YAML asserts, CI action) in packages/eval; gold data as hashed JSONL fixtures under packages/eval/fixtures; baselines under packages/eval/baselines/<axis>.json keyed by prompt_version_id + model build
recommendations.enforcement: CI job pnpm eval:offline computes per-axis metrics from stored outputs and fails if any axis drops more than the threshold in eval.config.ts below the checked-in baseline; baseline regeneration only via a dedicated script that also stamps prompt_version_id + model build, so a baseline change without a prompt/model change fails a consistency lint; a coverage check asserts minimum case counts per stratum so pruning fixtures can't fake a pass

recommendations.practice: No live model calls in PR CI — replay pinned fixtures; live evals run only on a scheduled on-prem GPU runner
recommendations.rationale: Live calls in CI are slow, flaky, nondeterministic, and violate the on-prem/hermetic constraint. Record request/response pairs keyed on (prompt hash, model build, params) and replay them; metric computation over stored outputs is fully deterministic even when generation is not. Live generation belongs in a nightly job on a self-hosted runner next to the llama.cpp box, which refreshes recorded outputs and posts metric diffs.
recommendations.tooling: ReplayProvider reading fixtures from packages/eval/recordings (content-addressed by request hash); undici MockAgent with net-connect disabled in vitest setup; GitHub Actions: PR workflow (hermetic) + schedule workflow pinned to runs-on: [self-hosted, gpu]; note promptfoo's disk cache expires after 14 days — do not rely on it as your fixture store
recommendations.enforcement: vitest setupFiles installs MockAgent().disableNetConnect() for eval/unit projects so any real fetch to an inference endpoint fails the test; CI exports EVAL_MODE=replay and the LlamaCppProvider constructor throws in that mode; recording refresh is a separate workflow whose output is a reviewable PR updating recordings + baselines together

recommendations.practice: Prompts are content-addressed, versioned artifacts with frontmatter metadata — enforced by a prompt-version lint
recommendations.rationale: Mature 2026 practice versions the whole prompt artifact (text + model + params + schema ref), immutable per version, with a content hash as prompt_version_id logged on every call. This makes 'which prompt produced this row' answerable forever, makes eval baselines addressable, and turns prompt edits into reviewable, gated changes instead of drive-by string edits.
recommendations.tooling: packages/schema/prompts/*.prompt.md with frontmatter {id, version, model, params, schemaRef, changelog}; scripts/gates/prompt-version-lint.mjs recomputes sha256 of body+frontmatter and compares to a committed lockfile (prompts.lock.json); the port's generate() accepts only a PromptRef type resolved from the registry — no raw strings
recommendations.enforcement: lefthook pre-commit + Claude Code PreToolUse hook run prompt-version-lint: any content change without a version bump + changelog entry blocks the write/commit; CI re-runs the lint and additionally fails PRs touching prompts/** that do not also touch baselines/ or recordings/ (path-filter gate); ESLint no-restricted-syntax bans string literals passed to generate(), forcing PromptRef usage

recommendations.practice: Mandatory per-output provenance: model build + GGUF hash, prompt_version_id, sampling params, confidence, review state — aligned to OTel GenAI attribute names but pinned to a semconv version
recommendations.rationale: Every extracted node/edge must be traceable to (model, model build, prompt version, params, seed, timestamp, confidence, requires_human_review, reviewer). OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions (gen_ai.request.model, gen_ai.usage.*, gen_ai.output.messages) are the emerging standard but are still in Development status as of mid-2026 (opt-in via OTEL_SEMCONV_STABILITY_OPT_IN=gen_ai_latest_experimental), so adopt the attribute vocabulary and pin the semconv version in one constants module rather than chasing it. Confidence must be a bounded float and treated as uncalibrated until proven otherwise.
recommendations.tooling: Drizzle schema: provenance columns NOT NULL with CHECK (confidence >= 0 AND confidence <= 1); a Provenance TS type required by the port's return value and by the insert DAL; OTel JS SDK spans with gen_ai.* attributes for the pipeline (local collector, on-prem); llama-server /props for build + model identity at startup
recommendations.enforcement: DB: NOT NULL + CHECK constraints make provenance-less inserts impossible; TS: the DAL insert function's parameter type requires the provenance object (tsc gate); an rls_verify-style CI script asserts the provenance columns and constraints exist in the migrated schema; a unit test inserting without provenance must fail (positive control)

recommendations.practice: HITL QA as a DB-enforced state machine; the pipeline role can create but never approve; accepted corrections feed a pgvector exemplar store for dynamic few-shot retrieval (no fine-tuning)
recommendations.rationale: Statuses extracted → validated → in_review → accepted/corrected/rejected, with only human-approved rows entering the live graph, is the trust model — and it must live in the database, not in application code, given FORCE RLS is already in the stack. Corrections harvested from review become retrieval exemplars: k-NN over pgvector selects input-similar accepted examples injected as few-shot context. 2025–2026 biomedical NER studies show retrieval-based dynamic prompting improves F1 by ~5–7% over static few-shot, giving a fine-tuning-free improvement loop that fits fully on-prem.
recommendations.tooling: Postgres enum for review_state + trigger constraining legal transitions; RLS policies: pipeline role may INSERT only with review_state='extracted' and may never UPDATE to accepted; exemplar table (input embedding vector, corrected output, provenance) in pgvector with HNSW index; retrieval inside packages/importer using the EmbeddingProvider port
recommendations.enforcement: pgTAP or SQL-based CI tests: connect as the pipeline role and assert INSERT with accepted state and UPDATE to accepted both fail (RLS + trigger); transition-matrix unit test against the trigger; a disjointness gate script asserts exemplar-store IDs ∩ eval-holdout IDs = ∅ (prevents feedback-loop contamination of the gold set); nightly eval includes an axis comparing with/without exemplars so a poisoned exemplar store shows up as a metric regression

recommendations.practice: Determinism discipline for eval generation: seed pinned, temperature 0, cache_prompt disabled (or dedicated slot), --parallel 1 eval profile — but compute metrics from stored outputs, never from re-generation
recommendations.rationale: llama-server exposes seed, temperature, cache_prompt (default ON), and id_slot; the docs explicitly warn KV-cache reuse can yield nondeterministic results due to backend batch-processing differences, and multi-slot batching adds more variance. So best-effort determinism for generation (seed, temp 0, cache_prompt:false, single slot), and hard determinism where it matters: raw outputs are stored artifacts and all scoring is a pure function over them, re-runnable byte-identically in CI.
recommendations.tooling: A dedicated eval llama-server profile in docker-compose (--parallel 1, pinned build); eval runner config in packages/eval asserts {seed, temperature:0, cache_prompt:false} on every request via the port; recordings stored with the full request object for auditability
recommendations.enforcement: The ReplayProvider/recorder rejects requests missing seed or with temperature>0 when EVAL_MODE is set (runtime assert covered by a test); CI recomputes metrics from recordings and diffs against baselines — a scoring-code change that alters metrics is caught as a baseline diff requiring review; the compose eval profile is linted for --parallel 1 by a config test

recommendations.practice: Scaffold the whole loop in the harness: packages/eval + packages/llm-port + gate scripts wired into the Stop-gate validate chain and mirrored in CI
recommendations.rationale: None of the above sticks unless the harness ships it as skeleton + mechanical gates on day one: an agent (or human) should be physically unable to end a turn or merge a PR with an unversioned prompt, an unvalidated LLM output path, a live call in CI, or a silent eval regression. The validate chain is the single source of truth; hooks and CI both run it, so local and remote enforcement cannot drift.
recommendations.tooling: Skeletons: packages/llm-port, packages/llm-adapters, packages/eval/{prompts,fixtures,recordings,baselines,scorers}, scripts/gates/{prompt-version-lint,schema-grammar-check,eval-regression,exemplar-disjointness,provenance-schema-check}.mjs; lefthook pre-commit runs the cheap gates; Claude Code hooks: PreToolUse write-guard on prompts/** and adapter-boundary greps, Stop hook that refuses to end the turn until pnpm validate (tsc, biome/eslint, unit, prompt lint, grammar check, offline eval) exits 0
recommendations.enforcement: Stop-gate hook returns a blocking decision until the validate chain is green (the agent cannot declare done); lefthook mirrors the same commands pre-commit/pre-push; GitHub Actions required checks run the identical pnpm validate plus the path-filter gates (prompts→baselines coupling), with branch protection making them mandatory; Renovate keeps llama.cpp image digest, evalite/promptfoo, Zod/ajv versions current via PRs that must pass the same gates

pitfalls: Trusting grammar-constrained output as fully schema-valid: llama.cpp's JSON-schema→GBNF converter silently skips unsupported features (uniqueItems, if/then/else, patternProperties, prefixItems, non-integer numeric bounds) and non-standardly defaults additionalProperties to false — always re-validate with ajv/Zod after generation, and lint schemas against the converter's supported subset in CI.

llama-server response_format sharp edges: passing both grammar and json_schema errors ('Either json_schema or grammar can be specified, but not both'), and there are documented bugs in json_schema handling on /v1/chat/completions in some builds (issues #11847, #11988) — pin an exact server build and keep a smoke test that round-trips a schema-constrained request.

The JSON schema is only used to constrain sampling — it is NOT injected into the prompt; if the prompt doesn't also describe the expected structure, the model fights the grammar and produces degenerate or looping output that is syntactically valid but semantically junk.

Grammar performance gotchas: unbounded repetition and chained optional patterns can make sampling extremely slow; use bounded repetition {0,N} and inspect converted grammars with the Python converter (which surfaces warnings the server swallows) plus llama-gbnf-validator.

Live model calls sneaking into PR CI: tool-level caches (e.g. promptfoo's 14-day disk cache) silently expire and trigger real calls; make hermeticity structural (net-connect disabled in test setup, adapter throws in replay mode) rather than relying on cache hits.

Vacuous-green evals: suites that pass because fixtures cover only easy strata or because a scorer bug returns 1.0 for everything — require per-stratum minimum case counts and seeded positive controls (known-bad outputs that must fail) so an always-passing suite is itself a CI failure.

Confidence theater: model-emitted confidence floats are uncalibrated by default; routing HITL review queues on raw confidence thresholds without a nightly calibration check (bucketed accuracy vs stated confidence) silently mis-prioritizes reviewer attention.

Feedback-loop contamination: accepted corrections used as retrieval exemplars leaking into the eval holdout (train/test contamination) or wrong 'accepted' rows amplifying errors in future extractions — enforce ID-level disjointness between exemplar store and gold set with a CI gate, and eval with/without exemplars so a poisoned store surfaces as a regression.

topic: Meta-sweep: what separates world-class TypeScript codebases and engineering orgs in 2026, with mechanical enforcement mappings for the Tauri/Hono/Postgres agent harness

recommendations.practice: Make AGENTS.md the single canonical agent contract; CLAUDE.md contains only '@AGENTS.md'; ship repo-native agent skills as first-class versioned artifacts
recommendations.rationale: Direct inspection of tldraw/tldraw (July 2026) shows the elite pattern: CLAUDE.md is literally one line ('@AGENTS.md'), AGENTS.md has sections for Core rules / Common commands / Validation workflow / Skills / Code conventions, and the repo ships ~20 in-repo skills (write-unit-tests, spec-driven-tests, shepherd-pr, tldraw-migrate, simplify-trace). AGENTS.md is now stewarded by the Agentic AI Foundation (Linux Foundation), read by 30+ tools, adopted by 60k+ repos. Anthropic's own guidance: keep CLAUDE.md lean and layered per-directory; anything that MUST hold goes in hooks/permissions, not prose.
recommendations.tooling: AGENTS.md spec (agents.md); per-package AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md files; .claude/skills/ directory; path-scoped skills (like this repo's authoring-vertical-slice)
recommendations.enforcement: CI script asserts CLAUDE.md == '@AGENTS.md' (drift check); a 'docs-commands-verify' CI job parses fenced commands in AGENTS.md and asserts each exists in package.json scripts (fails on doc drift); SessionStart hook injects current validate-chain status; lint ignorePatterns exclude skills/** from app lint (tldraw does exactly this)

recommendations.practice: Encode project-specific invariants as custom lint rules with actionable messages, not just preset configs
recommendations.rationale: tldraw maintains 17 custom rules in one ESLint-API-compatible plugin file (works in both oxlint and ESLint 9): no-internal-imports (package boundary), no-export-star (tree-shaking/API surface), no-focused-tests, tsdoc-param-matching, no-exported-arrow-const, and notably 'claude-agents-pairing' — a lint rule about keeping agent config files in sync. World-class repos treat 'we keep telling people X in review' as a lint-rule backlog. For your stack: rules like 'no direct db access outside packages/schema DAL', 'no fetch outside apps/server/src/llm client', 'every Hono route registers an auth middleware', 'no raw SQL string concatenation'.
recommendations.tooling: ESLint 9 flat config custom plugin (plain create(context) rules, no build step needed); eslint-plugin-eslint-comments@^4 require-description for justified disables; Biome stays on formatting + fast correctness (disjoint responsibility)
recommendations.enforcement: Custom rules run at error level in the validate chain; PostToolUse hook runs scoped `eslint --no-warn-ignored <changed-file>` so the agent gets same-session feedback; CI re-runs the identical `pnpm validate` script (single source of truth so hook-green implies CI-green); eslint-comments/require-description makes every disable carry a written justification

recommendations.practice: Hard architecture gates: dependency-cruiser for layer/boundary rules + Knip for dead exports, unused files, and unused dependencies
recommendations.rationale: Generic no-unused-vars can't see cross-module rot. Knip analyzes the full workspace module graph (first-class pnpm monorepo support) and is the 2026 standard for dead-code detection; dependency-cruiser validates declared dependency rules (no cycles, no apps/desktop importing apps/server internals, packages/schema depends on nothing app-level). Effect-TS runs `pnpm circular` as a named CI check — circularity is treated as a build-breaking defect, not a smell. Agents especially benefit: boundaries the linter enforces are boundaries the agent cannot silently erode.
recommendations.tooling: knip@^5 (workspace-aware, knip.json per-workspace config); dependency-cruiser@^16 (.dependency-cruiser.cjs with forbidden rules: no-circular, no-cross-app, layered schema→server→desktop); madge as lightweight alternative for cycles only
recommendations.enforcement: Both run in `pnpm validate` (Stop gate refuses until green) and as required CI checks; dependency-cruiser 'forbidden' rules fail the build with the rule's own explanation string; Knip in --strict mode for packages/*, CI-only (too slow for pre-write hooks) but included in pre-push lefthook

recommendations.practice: Typed-error doctrine: Result<T,E> for expected failures at module boundaries, throw only for invariant violations; enforce the split with scoped lint
recommendations.rationale: 2025–26 consensus: throw for the unexpected, return Result for the expected. Exceptions are invisible in TS signatures, so callers can't see failure modes — fatal for an AI-extraction pipeline with human-in-the-loop QA where partial failure is the normal case. neverthrow gives Result without the full Effect paradigm shift (Effect is powerful but a large dependency and steep for mixed teams; overkill unless you adopt it wholesale). Your importer/eval/LLM-client boundaries should return ok/err; Hono error middleware is the single throw→HTTP mapping point.
recommendations.tooling: neverthrow@^8 (Result, ResultAsync, safeTry); typescript-eslint@^8 no-floating-promises + only-throw-error; a custom 'no-throw-in-services' rule via no-restricted-syntax ThrowStatement scoped to apps/server/src/services/** and packages/importer/** (note: eslint-plugin-neverthrow is stale — prefer typescript-eslint + custom rules)
recommendations.enforcement: Scoped ESLint flat-config block: files ['apps/server/src/services/**','packages/importer/**'] with no-restricted-syntax banning ThrowStatement (error message points at Result doctrine doc); must-use: tsconfig noUnusedLocals + neverthrow's Result being a value forces handling; unit tests assert err() paths for every service (test-name convention checked by grep gate)

recommendations.practice: Hermetic, reproducible builds + hardened GitHub Actions as a single supply-chain layer
recommendations.rationale: Post-Shai-Hulud (2025 npm worm) the bar moved: frozen lockfile installs, SHA-pinned actions, workflow static analysis, and dependency cooldowns are now table stakes at serious orgs. GitHub's own 2026 roadmap (workflow dependency locking, native egress firewalls) confirms direction but isn't GA — so you enforce it yourself. Hermeticity also serves agents: `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` failing loudly on drift is exactly the deterministic feedback an agent harness needs. Fully on-prem deployment makes reproducible artifacts (same inputs → same signed .exe checksum) auditable for the medical school.
recommendations.tooling: pnpm packageManager field + engines + corepack pin Node 22; `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` in CI; zizmor@^1 (GHA static analyzer: unpinned-uses, template-injection, pull_request_target); actionlint; step-security/harden-runner with egress-policy: block on release/signing workflows; Renovate minimumReleaseAge '7 days' cooldown; pnpm overrides + pnpm audit signatures
recommendations.enforcement: lefthook pre-commit runs zizmor+actionlint on .github/** changes; CI job 'workflow-security' runs zizmor --min-severity low as required check; zizmor's unpinned-uses rule mechanically forbids tag-pinned actions (SHA only); Renovate config in-repo enforces cooldown; release workflow asserts rebuild checksum equality before signing the Windows .exe

recommendations.practice: ADRs + spec-driven artifacts with mechanical traceability: guarded paths require an ADR, requirement IDs must resolve to tests
recommendations.rationale: GitHub Spec Kit formalized the 2026 pattern: constitution → spec → plan → tasks as versioned markdown living beside code, with cross-layer traceability (requirement → contract → task → test) as 'a mechanical lookup with entity IDs in frontmatter', not narrative. For a curriculum-mapping platform with compliance stakes (WCAG 2.2 AA, medical-school audit), traceability is a product requirement. ADRs make the 'why' durable for both humans and agents — an agent reading docs/adr/0007-force-rls.md won't undo FORCE RLS to fix a test. Pitfall to avoid: spec theater — specs with no mechanical link to code rot immediately.
recommendations.tooling: MADR 4.x format in docs/adr/; github/spec-kit workflow patterns (/speckit.specify → plan → tasks) adapted as Claude Code skills; Vale + markdownlint-cli2 lint the ADRs/specs themselves (Vale is used by Datadog, GitLab, Microsoft, Elastic); REQ-ID frontmatter convention
recommendations.enforcement: CI 'adr-guard' script: if diff touches guarded globs (packages/schema/migrations/**, anything matching /rls|auth|oidc/) and no docs/adr/** change or 'ADR-\d+' reference in PR body, fail; traceability gate: script extracts REQ-IDs from specs' frontmatter and greps test files for each ID, failing on orphans; Vale + markdownlint run in the same docs CI job; commitlint scope 'adr' reserved

recommendations.practice: Observability-driven development: OpenTelemetry with trace-correlated structured logs from day one, verified by test
recommendations.rationale: OpenTelemetry reached CNCF Graduated status (May 2026) with all three signals stable in JS — it is the baseline, not an add-on. The elite pattern is 'wide events': every log line carries trace_id/span_id so a slow coverage-matrix render (<1s budget!) can be traced from React → Hono → Drizzle query → pgvector. Fully on-prem is fine: OTel collector + self-hosted backend. For agents, structured telemetry doubles as the verification substrate — an agent can query 'did my change add a span / regress p95' instead of guessing.
recommendations.tooling: @opentelemetry/sdk-node + auto-instrumentations-node (Hono, pg, undici); pino with mixin injecting trace context from AsyncLocalStorage; self-hosted collector + Grafana Tempo/Loki (zero external cloud); web-vitals + custom render-timing span in the SPA
recommendations.enforcement: Biome/ESLint no-console at error (pino logger is the only sink); integration test with InMemorySpanExporter asserts every registered Hono route emits a named span with required attributes (fails when a new route ships uninstrumented); perf budget test: Playwright trace asserts coverage-matrix first-render span < 1000ms against seeded 10k-node fixture, run as required CI check

recommendations.practice: Calibrated complexity budgets: cognitive-complexity caps and function-length warnings, NOT blanket file-size caps
recommendations.rationale: Evidence check: elite repos (tldraw, Excalidraw, Effect-TS) do NOT enforce max-lines; they enforce targeted invariants via custom rules and keep files cohesive by convention. Blanket file caps cause agents to split files arbitrarily to satisfy the linter, destroying cohesion. But agent-first repos DO benefit from bounded units: cognitive complexity caps keep any single function within an agent's (and human's) reliable-edit envelope, and ESLint's own docs note complexity default 20 is too lax. The budget should bind at the function level where it correlates with defect rates, with escape hatches that require written justification.
recommendations.tooling: eslint-plugin-sonarjs cognitive-complexity at 15 (error); ESLint core complexity (cyclomatic) 12 warn; max-lines-per-function 120 warn / max-lines 600 warn (warn-only, surfaced in review); eslint-comments/require-description so every override is justified inline
recommendations.enforcement: cognitive-complexity=error blocks the Stop gate and CI; the warn-tier rules feed a PR annotation (eslint --format github) so reviewers/agents see growth without hard-blocking; a ratchet script stores current warn-count in repo and CI fails if count increases (no new debt, no forced rewrites)

recommendations.practice: One canonical validate chain with tiered latency, type-level tests, and session-survivable gate state
recommendations.rationale: Anthropic's long-running-agent guidance: init script + progress artifacts + 'unacceptable to remove or edit tests' + verify-before-complete. Effect-TS's CI names the tiers explicitly: pnpm lint / pnpm circular / pnpm test-types (tstyche) / pnpm docgen — each a separate fast fail. Harness prior art (claude-code-harness 1.7k stars, ECC, sd0x-dev-flow) converged on: script-based hooks not inline one-liners, fail-closed gates, and gate state persisted outside the conversation so it survives context compaction. Type-level tests matter for packages/schema: Drizzle inference and GBNF/JSON-schema types are load-bearing contracts that runtime tests can't see.
recommendations.tooling: tstyche@^4 for type-level tests in packages/schema; madge/depcruise for circular; Vitest projects with fake timers + blocked network (no real llama.cpp in unit tier; recorded fixtures); single `pnpm validate` orchestrating typecheck→biome→eslint→test→knip; gate state in .claude/state/*.json
recommendations.enforcement: Stop hook runs `pnpm validate --affected` and returns block-decision until green, reading/writing persisted gate state (survives compaction); PostToolUse hook runs the sub-second tier (format+scoped lint) per write; lefthook pre-push runs full chain; CI runs the byte-identical `pnpm validate` so local-green ⇒ CI-green; a meta-test executes each AGENTS.md-documented command with --help to catch doc drift

recommendations.practice: Test-suite quality beyond coverage: incremental mutation testing on critical packages + anti-vacuity controls
recommendations.rationale: Coverage says lines ran; mutation score says tests would catch bugs. StrykerJS incremental mode + per-test coverage analysis makes PR-time mutation testing viable (1–5 min on changed code). For this product the critical modules are packages/importer (AI extraction correctness feeding a medical curriculum) and packages/eval — exactly where mutation testing pays. Pair with anti-vacuity discipline this repo already started (rls_verify seeded positive control): every guard must be shown to fail on a known-bad input, or a green gate proves nothing. tldraw's no-focused-tests custom rule is the same instinct: prevent silently-narrowed suites.
recommendations.tooling: @stryker-mutator/core@^9 with --incremental and vitest runner, scoped to packages/importer + packages/eval; mutation-score threshold break: 70 rising via ratchet; ESLint no-focused-tests / vitest.config forbidOnly; seeded negative fixtures for RLS and schema validators
recommendations.enforcement: CI job 'mutation' runs Stryker incremental on changed files in critical packages, failing under threshold; forbidOnly:true in vitest config makes .only a hard CI failure; 'positive control' tests (known-bad row must be rejected by RLS, malformed LLM JSON must fail the GBNF/zod validator) are tagged @control and a CI grep asserts they exist and run — a gate that can't demonstrate failure is itself a failure

pitfalls: CLAUDE.md bloat: stuffing every rule into prose degrades agent performance and none of it is enforced — Anthropic's own guidance is that anything that MUST hold belongs in hooks/permissions/lint, with CLAUDE.md kept lean and layered (tldraw's is one line: '@AGENTS.md')

Copying blanket max-lines/file-size caps from blog posts: verified that elite repos (tldraw, Excalidraw, Effect-TS) don't use them; naive caps make agents shard files arbitrarily to appease the linter — bind budgets at function-level cognitive complexity instead, with ratchets not rewrites

Vacuous gates: scoped lint that misses newly created files, mutation/RLS checks that pass on empty selections, or a Stop gate whose state is lost on context compaction — every gate needs a seeded positive control proving it can fail, and gate state must persist outside the conversation (sd0x-dev-flow's core lesson)

Stale enforcement tooling: eslint-plugin-neverthrow is effectively unmaintained against ESLint 9 flat config — build the throw-ban as a scoped no-restricted-syntax/custom rule via typescript-eslint v8 rather than depending on it

Biome + ESLint overlap churn: running both with overlapping rules produces fix-fights and double diagnostics; assign disjoint duties (Biome = format + fast correctness, ESLint = type-aware + custom invariants) and add a CI check that no rule ID is enabled in both

Spec/ADR theater: specs and ADRs with no mechanical link to code rot within weeks; traceability must be an ID lookup enforced by CI (guarded-path→ADR gate, REQ-ID→test grep), never a review-time aspiration

Supply-chain blind spots specific to agent-heavy repos: unpinned GitHub Actions and zero dependency cooldown were the Shai-Hulud attack surface; also inline one-liner hooks are fragile and unauditable — use script-based hooks under version control (ECC's 'hook reliability overhaul' lesson)

Treating mutation testing as all-or-nothing: full-suite Stryker is 10–100x test cost and gets abandoned; incremental mode scoped to the two critical packages on PR diffs is the adoption path that survives

topic: Desktop crash reporting, client-side diagnostics, and supportability for an on-prem Tauri 2 Windows app (Rust host + React 19/WebView2), with mechanical enforcement in a Claude Code hook harness and GitHub Actions CI

recommendations.practice: Adopt out-of-process native crash capture as a day-one subsystem: tauri-plugin-sentry 0.5.0 (re-exports sentry-rust-minidump), which re-spawns the app's own signed .exe with a crash-reporter argument as a separate watchdog process; the watchdog runs a minidumper Server (minidumper 0.10.1 + crash-handler 0.7.0, EmbarkStudios crash-handling) and writes/uploads the minidump when the app process faults. Init order is load-bearing: sentry::init(...) then tauri_plugin_sentry::minidump::init(&client) BEFORE tauri::Builder::default() — everything above minidump::init executes in BOTH the app and reporter processes, everything below only in the app.
recommendations.rationale: In-process minidump writing from a crashing process is inherently unreliable (the EmbarkStudios crates and Jake Shadle's crash-reporting writeup exist precisely because of this); retrofitting a two-process architecture later is invasive because the reporter fork point must precede all other init (single-instance locks, DB opens, CLI arg parsing — sentry-tauri issue #29 shows a CLI parser crashing the reporter on the injected --crash-reporter-server argument). Hand-rolling minidumper+crash-handler is the fallback if plugin version skew bites, but the plugin gives Rust panics, JS errors, breadcrumb merging, and minidumps in one wiring. Pin the sentry crate to the version the plugin re-exports (0.5.0 pins sentry 0.42; latest sentry is 0.48.4 — mixing breaks re-exported types).
recommendations.tooling: tauri-plugin-sentry = "0.5" (crates.io, Sep 2025), sentry = "0.42" (plugin-pinned; standalone latest 0.48.4, Jul 2026), sentry-rust-minidump 0.16.0 (May 2026) with its `ipc` feature for breadcrumb/tag sync to the reporter, minidumper 0.10.1, crash-handler 0.7.0. Configure the dump directory to the Tauri app_data_dir: %APPDATA%\com.<org>.<app>\crashes so the diagnostics bundle can find the last dump.
recommendations.enforcement: (a) CI job on windows-latest that installs the built signed .exe, launches it with a hidden `--qa-force-crash` test command (behind a build-time cfg flag) that calls std::process::abort(), then asserts a fresh .dmp exists under %APPDATA%\...\crashes AND a crash envelope was received by a local mock DSN (tiny HTTP listener); (b) a repo lint in the Stop-gate validate chain (ripgrep AST check) asserting `minidump::init` appears lexically before `tauri::Builder` in apps/desktop/src-tauri/src/main.rs and that no I/O-side-effect calls (db open, single-instance plugin) precede it; (c) cargo-deny / Renovate rule pinning sentry to the plugin's re-exported version so version skew fails CI.

recommendations.practice: Run self-hosted Sentry (getsentry/self-hosted, CalVer 26.x) as the on-prem ingestion backend — not GlitchTip 6 and not Bugsink — on the same VM/VLAN as the Hono API, sized at the documented floor: 4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM + 16 GB swap, 20+ GB disk (~20 containers incl. Kafka, ClickHouse, Snuba, Relay, symbolicator).
recommendations.rationale: The deciding feature is minidump symbolication: GlitchTip's own SDK docs state 'GlitchTip does not support minidumps at this time' (still true in GlitchTip 6.1, Mar 2026), and Bugsink's minidump code is gated behind FEATURE_MINIDUMPS which its docs label development-only and not security-reviewed (DoS risk). Only Sentry ships symbolicator, which turns a WebView2/Rust .dmp plus uploaded PDBs into a readable stack. If the team later decides minidumps will be symbolicated offline by IT instead (minidump-stackwalk), GlitchTip 6.1 or Bugsink become viable lightweight backends for JS/Rust events — but that forfeits automated native triage. The DSN stays an on-prem origin (e.g. https://errors.medschool.internal), satisfying the zero-external-cloud constraint.
recommendations.tooling: getsentry/self-hosted (docker compose, requires Docker >= 19.03.6, Compose >= 2.32.2); comparison points: GlitchTip 6.1 (Postgres + Valkey/Redis 7 + Celery, port 8000), Bugsink (single container, SQLite/Postgres, ~1.5M events/day on 2 vCPU/4 GB).
recommendations.enforcement: (a) Nightly CI 'ingestion canary': POST a synthetic Sentry envelope and a known-good fixture minidump to the on-prem DSN and assert (via Sentry API) the event appears symbolicated — fails loud before a real crash is lost; (b) infra healthcheck in the harness doctor script asserting the DSN origin resolves inside the .internal zone; (c) config-schema test that rejects any DSN whose host is not on the committed on-prem allowlist.

recommendations.practice: Pin the crash/telemetry origin into the Tauri CSP and make it lint-enforced: tauri.conf.json app.security.csp connect-src must be exactly ['self', 'ipc:', 'http://ipc.localhost', '<on-prem API origin>', '<on-prem DSN origin>'] — no wildcards, no external hosts. The @sentry/browser SDK injected by tauri-plugin-sentry sends via fetch, so the DSN origin must be in connect-src or frontend capture silently dies.
recommendations.rationale: Tauri's strict CSP is the mechanical guarantee the shipped .exe cannot exfiltrate to cloud telemetry even if a dependency tries; simultaneously, a forgotten connect-src entry is the #1 silent failure mode for in-webview error capture (blocked requests never reach the DSN and Sentry SDKs swallow transport errors).
recommendations.tooling: tauri.conf.json app.security.csp (Tauri 2 security config); JSON-schema validation of tauri.conf.json in the validate chain; Vite define for the DSN constant so it is not user-configurable at runtime.
recommendations.enforcement: (a) A schema/lint step in the Stop-gate: a small script (ajv or zod) parses tauri.conf.json, tokenizes connect-src, and fails on any origin not in the committed allowlist (this also catches '*'); (b) a Playwright/WebdriverIO smoke test against the dev build asserting a Sentry test event actually arrives at a local mock DSN (proves CSP admits it); (c) Claude Code pre-write hook refusing edits to tauri.conf.json that touch `security.csp` unless the allowlist file changes in the same diff.

recommendations.practice: Treat symbols as release artifacts: build release with [profile.release] debug = "full" and split-debuginfo = "packed" (MSVC emits app.pdb beside the .exe); exclude the PDB from the NSIS/MSI bundle; per tagged release, upload debug files to the on-prem Sentry with sentry-cli debug-files upload (native PDB preferred over Breakpad .sym — Sentry docs note dump_syms-produced Breakpad symbols drop inline frames) AND archive the PDB + sourcemaps as a GitHub Actions artifact / internal release asset keyed by the tag.
recommendations.rationale: A minidump without the exact build's PDB is unsymbolicatable — debug IDs must match per build, so symbol preservation must happen in the same workflow run that signs the .exe. Shipping the PDB inside the installer leaks source paths and internals to every university workstation; uploading + archiving instead keeps dumps debuggable for the life of the release. The Vite build must likewise upload sourcemaps (sentry-cli sourcemaps inject/upload) for WebView2 JS stacks, with sourcemaps stripped from the bundle.
recommendations.tooling: sentry-cli 2.x (`sentry-cli debug-files upload --include-sources ./target/release`, `sentry-cli debug-files check`, `sentry-cli sourcemaps inject && upload`); mozilla dump_syms only if an offline minidump-stackwalk path is chosen; Cargo [profile.release] debug settings; GitHub Actions upload-artifact with retention matched to app support lifetime.
recommendations.enforcement: Release-workflow gate (required job for the tag to publish): fails if (1) target/release/*.pdb is absent after tauri build, (2) `sentry-cli debug-files check` reports no matching debug ID for the built .exe, (3) the sentry-cli upload step returned zero uploaded/kept files, or (4) the installer payload (7z-listed) contains a .pdb or .map. A companion unit test asserts the Cargo profile keys are present so a 'cleanup' PR cannot silently drop debug info.

recommendations.practice: One structured logging spine on the Rust side: tracing + a panic hook installed before Tauri's builder (sentry's panic integration gives panics-as-events; the hook also logs the panic with location to the file log), bridged into tauri-plugin-log 2.8.0 writing to the appdata log dir with .max_file_size(...) and .rotation_strategy(RotationStrategy::KeepAll) plus a retention cap, and ban ad-hoc output everywhere: println!/eprintln!/dbg! in Rust, console.* in TS.
recommendations.rationale: IT can only attach what exists on disk: rotated, size-capped, structured logs under %APPDATA%\com.<org>.<app>\logs are the raw material for both the diagnostics bundle and crash breadcrumbs. Unstructured prints go nowhere in a windowed .exe (no console) and bypass redaction; console.* in the SPA bypasses breadcrumb capture and leaks into WebView2 devtools on shared machines.
recommendations.tooling: tauri-plugin-log 2.8.0 (Jan 2026; TargetKind::LogDir, max_file_size, RotationStrategy) with the attach-console feature off in release; tracing 0.1 + sentry's tracing/log integration; Rust lint config via [workspace.lints.clippy]: print_stdout = "deny", print_stderr = "deny", dbg_macro = "deny"; Biome 2.4 rule lint/suspicious/noConsole (off by default — must be enabled) with allow: [] (or allow error/warn routed through a wrapper), plus ESLint 9 no-console as belt-and-braces for files Biome skips.
recommendations.enforcement: (a) cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings in the Stop-gate validate chain and CI (workspace lints make the ban un-droppable per-crate); (b) Biome check with noConsole=error in the same chain, and a Claude Code pre-write guard that rejects diffs introducing `console.` outside the designated logger module; (c) a unit test that spawns the app harness and asserts the panic hook fires before tauri::Builder (install a probe hook, panic in a child thread pre-build, assert the structured record); (d) a log-rotation test writing >max_file_size and asserting rotation occurred and total log-dir size stays under the cap.

recommendations.practice: Handle WebView2 process failures explicitly on Windows: in setup, call WebviewWindow::with_webview and via webview2-com register add_ProcessFailed on the CoreWebView2; on ProcessFailedKind RenderProcessExited/Unresponsive call Reload() (Microsoft's documented recovery), on BrowserProcessExited tear down and recreate the webview window; every event is logged as a structured 'webview_process_failed' record with kind, exit code, and process description, and mirrored as a Sentry event. Additionally sweep WebView2's own Crashpad dumps from <UserDataFolder>\EBWebView\Crashpad\reports (Tauri default: %LOCALAPPDATA%\com.<org>.<app>\EBWebView) into the diagnostics story.
recommendations.rationale: A renderer crash is invisible to the Rust crash handler — the app process never faults; without a ProcessFailed handler the user sees a frozen/blank window ('the app closed') and nothing is recorded. Microsoft's process-related-events guidance is explicit that ProcessFailed is the diagnostics-and-recovery channel, and WebView2 writes its own minidumps under the user-data folder's Crashpad directory — a bundle that only looks in your own crashes dir misses every renderer crash.
recommendations.tooling: tauri (2.x) with_webview platform escape hatch; webview2-com crate at the version wry pins (ICoreWebView2ProcessFailedEventHandler, ICoreWebView2ProcessFailedEventArgs; ICoreWebView2Environment11::FailureReportFolderPath for the exact dump path); tauri::WebviewWindow::webview_version() (wraps GetAvailableCoreWebView2BrowserVersionString) recorded at startup.
recommendations.enforcement: (a) windows-latest CI integration test: launch the built app, locate the msedgewebview2.exe renderer child PID (toolhelp snapshot filtered by --type=renderer and user-data-dir), TerminateProcess it, then assert (1) the structured webview_process_failed log record exists, (2) the app window recovered (health-ping IPC round-trip succeeds), (3) a Sentry event was queued to the mock DSN; (b) a source-presence lint in the validate chain asserting the add_ProcessFailed registration exists in the Windows cfg block (prevents silent deletion); (c) diagnostics-bundle test asserting EBWebView\Crashpad\reports is included in the sweep globs.

recommendations.practice: Frontend capture wired to the same on-prem DSN with trace correlation: @sentry/react 10.64.0 using React 19 root error hooks — createRoot(container, { onUncaughtError: Sentry.reactErrorHandler(), onCaughtError: Sentry.reactErrorHandler(), onRecoverableError: Sentry.reactErrorHandler() }) — plus a top-level Sentry.ErrorBoundary for UX fallback; browserTracingIntegration with tracePropagationTargets limited to the on-prem API origin so every fetch to Hono carries sentry-trace/baggage (W3C-compatible), and the Hono OTel middleware records the same trace_id and echoes its reqId, which the client attaches as a Sentry tag. A desktop error event then links to the exact failing API span in the server's OTel backend.
recommendations.rationale: React 19 moved error interception to root options (onUncaughtError/onCaughtError/onRecoverableError); Sentry.reactErrorHandler (available since v8.6, current v10 line) is the supported adapter, and window.onerror/onunhandledrejection are covered by the SDK's global handlers integration. Without propagation-target-scoped trace headers, a desktop 'save failed' ticket and the Hono 500 that caused it are two unlinkable events; with it, one click goes from the React error to the Postgres/RLS span that failed.
recommendations.tooling: @sentry/react 10.64.0 (bundles @sentry/browser; injected by tauri-plugin-sentry — use init_with_no_injection(&client) to merge custom options like tracePropagationTargets and beforeSend), Hono OTel middleware + pino reqId already in the corpus; tag convention: reqId + trace_id on both sides.
recommendations.enforcement: (a) ESLint 9 flat-config custom rule (or shared eslint-plugin in packages/) that errors if react-dom/client createRoot is called without all three error-hook options — main.tsx cannot regress; (b) Vitest unit test rendering a throwing component under the real root options against a mocked transport, asserting the event carries reqId/trace_id tags; (c) Playwright E2E in CI: force a 500 from a Hono test route, assert the captured Sentry event's trace_id equals the traceparent recorded by the server's OTel exporter (fixture in-memory exporter); (d) the CSP lint from the connect-src recommendation guarantees the DSN transport isn't silently blocked.

recommendations.practice: Ship a 'Collect diagnostics bundle' command from day one: a Tauri command (also reachable from a Help menu) that zips — with a schema-validated manifest.json — the last N rotated logs, the most recent minidump(s) from both the app crashes dir and EBWebView\Crashpad\reports, app version + git SHA + build channel, WebView2 runtime version (webview_version()), OS build, a config fingerprint (hash of effective config, never raw values), and pending unsent crash envelopes. Every text file passes through a redaction pass (regex set: Bearer/JWT patterns eyJ..., Entra ID tokens, Set-Cookie, DSN secrets, email-like PII beyond the user's own UPN) before zipping; auth tokens are never logged in the first place (logger serializer denylist).
recommendations.rationale: On a university network with no cloud telemetry, the bundle is the support channel: IT attaches one zip to a ticket instead of 'the app closed'. Making the manifest schema-validated turns supportability into a testable contract, and building redaction into the bundle (not just the logger) is defense-in-depth for a medical-school environment where machines are shared and FERPA/GDPR-adjacent data is nearby.
recommendations.tooling: Hand-rolled Tauri command in apps/desktop/src-tauri (zip crate + serde manifest); zod/JSON-Schema definition of the manifest shared in packages/schema; the same redaction regex set reused by the existing hygiene credential-pattern checks in the harness.
recommendations.enforcement: (a) Unit test that seeds logs with known fake secrets (fake JWT, fake Entra access token, Authorization header, cookie) plus a canary minidump, invokes the bundle command, unzips, and asserts zero regex hits and manifest validates against the schema — this test is in the Stop-gate validate chain; (b) a golden-file test pinning the manifest schema version so fields can only be added consciously; (c) CI windows-latest job runs the bundle command against the built .exe and asserts the zip contains logs + dump + versions (proves the paths are right on real Windows, not just in dev).

recommendations.practice: Codify an internal-enterprise privacy/consent posture: crash and diagnostic telemetry to the on-prem backend is legitimate-interest IT operations data (no consent dialog needed for an institution-managed app on institution machines), but enforce data minimization mechanically — send_default_pii: false in both Rust ClientOptions and the browser init, beforeSend/before_send hooks that strip IP, drop breadcrumb bodies for auth routes, and set user context to an opaque directory ID (Entra oid) never UPN/display name; server-side data-scrubbing rules on in the self-hosted Sentry project; a visible 'Diagnostics' page in-app documenting exactly what is collected, with an admin-policy (not per-user) kill switch; diagnostics-bundle creation is user-initiated (implicit consent by action).
recommendations.rationale: The privacy risk here is not cloud exfiltration (CSP forecloses it) but over-collection inside the institution: crash payloads that embed auth tokens, patient-adjacent curriculum data in URLs, or user identities create internal audit exposure. Pinning pii flags and scrubbing in code, plus documenting the posture in-app, matches enterprise expectations and survives review by university counsel far better than an unenforced policy doc.
recommendations.tooling: sentry ClientOptions { send_default_pii: false, before_send } (Rust 0.42/0.48 line); @sentry/react init { sendDefaultPii: false, beforeSend, beforeBreadcrumb }; self-hosted Sentry per-project Data Scrubbers; app settings backed by admin-deployed policy file.
recommendations.enforcement: (a) Unit tests asserting send_default_pii is false and that beforeSend on a fixture event removes ip_address, request cookies/headers, and rewrites user to { id: oid } — placed in both apps/desktop and the web layer; (b) ripgrep CI gate (and Claude Code pre-write guard) banning `sendDefaultPii: true` / `send_default_pii: true` and banning `user.email|username` assignment in Sentry scope code; (c) the diagnostics-bundle redaction test above doubles as the PII regression gate; (d) docs lint: the in-app Diagnostics page content is generated from the same manifest schema, so collected-fields drift fails the golden-file test.

recommendations.practice: Make crash delivery resilient to on-prem network unavailability: because the Sentry Rust SDK has no persistent offline queue, keep every written minidump on disk in %APPDATA%\...\crashes until the backend acks, and on each app start run a 'crash sweeper' that (1) detects dumps not yet marked uploaded, (2) re-submits them (as attachments on a synthesized event, or via Sentry's raw minidump endpoint /api/<project>/minidump/), (3) prunes acked dumps beyond a count/size cap, and (4) tags the next session with last_run_crashed=true (dirty-shutdown sentinel file) so even un-symbolicated 'silent' kills (power loss, AV kill) are counted.
recommendations.rationale: The out-of-process reporter uploads at crash time — exactly when a VM reboot, VPN drop, or backend maintenance window can eat the report. VDI and lab machines at a university are frequently offline from internal services; without persist-and-resend plus a dirty-shutdown sentinel, the crash rate is systematically undercounted and IT loses the only artifact (the .dmp) that made the ticket debuggable.
recommendations.tooling: Hand-rolled sweeper in the Tauri setup hook (std fs + sentry 0.42/0.48 attachment API, or reqwest POST to the DSN minidump endpoint with the on-prem CA in the trust store); sentinel file pattern (create on start, remove on graceful exit) — same technique Crashpad/browsers use for crash-loop detection.
recommendations.enforcement: (a) Integration test: place a fixture .dmp in the crashes dir, start the app with the mock DSN blackholed (refused connections), assert the dump survives and is re-sent successfully on the next start when the mock DSN is up; (b) unit test for the sentinel: simulated non-graceful exit leaves the file, next start logs/records session status 'crashed'; (c) size-cap test asserting the crashes dir never exceeds the configured budget (prevents filling lab-machine roaming profiles); all three run in the standard CI matrix, the first also on windows-latest.

pitfalls: Choosing GlitchTip because it is lighter to operate: GlitchTip's own SDK docs state it does not support minidumps, and that is still true in GlitchTip 6.1 (Mar 2026) — native Rust/WebView2 crashes would arrive as unsymbolicatable blobs or not at all; only self-hosted Sentry runs symbolicator on-prem.

Bugsink's minidump code exists but is gated behind FEATURE_MINIDUMPS, which its own settings docs describe as development-only and not security-reviewed (opens the install to DoS) — do not treat it as a supported native-crash backend.

Any side-effectful code placed before sentry_rust_minidump::init / tauri_plugin_sentry::minidump::init runs in BOTH the app and the re-spawned crash-reporter process: single-instance plugins, port binds, migrations, and strict CLI parsers all break the reporter (timfish/sentry-tauri #29: clap rejected the injected --crash-reporter-server argument and killed crash reporting silently).

Version skew between tauri-plugin-sentry 0.5.0 (pins sentry 0.42 and re-exports sentry-rust-minidump) and the standalone sentry crate (0.48.4 as of Jul 2026): adding your own newer sentry dependency alongside the plugin produces incompatible re-exported types or two competing clients — pin to the plugin's version and upgrade them together.

On MSVC targets a default release profile emits limited/no usable PDB and a 'cleanup' that deletes [profile.release] debug settings silently makes every future dump unsymbolicatable — while conversely shipping the .pdb inside the installer leaks source paths to every workstation; both directions need CI gates, not review vigilance.

WebView2 renderer crashes never fault the Rust process and its Crashpad dumps land under %LOCALAPPDATA%\<app>\EBWebView\Crashpad\reports, not your crashes dir — without an add_ProcessFailed handler plus a bundle sweep of that folder, every renderer crash is an invisible blank-window ticket.

The Sentry Rust SDK has no persistent offline queue: a crash uploaded only at crash time is lost whenever the on-prem backend or network is briefly down (VDI, VPN, maintenance windows) — persist dumps and re-send on next launch or crash counts will be systematically wrong.

Biome's lint/suspicious/noConsole is NOT enabled by default (it must be switched on per-project), and it does nothing for Rust — println!/eprintln!/dbg! need clippy restriction lints (print_stdout/print_stderr/dbg_macro) at the [workspace.lints] level or crates can opt out one by one.
