topic: Operational data lifecycle for self-hosted Postgres 16 + pgvector behind a Hono API with Intune-deployed Tauri clients: pgBackRest backup/PITR with verified restores, expand-contract destructive-migration doctrine, and desktop-server version-skew management (verified July 2026)

recommendations.practice: Scaffold a pgBackRest stanza (pgbackrest.conf template) for the single-node on-prem VM: weekly full + daily incremental + continuous WAL archiving, AES-256 encrypted repo, count-based retention. Template keys: repo1-path on separate volume, repo1-cipher-type=aes-256-cbc, repo1-cipher-pass=<from on-prem secret file, mode 0600, never in git>, repo1-retention-full=4, repo1-retention-full-type=count, repo1-bundle=y, repo1-block=y, compress-type=zst, start-fast=y, archive-async=y, process-max=2; postgresql.conf gets archive_mode=on, archive_command='pgbackrest --stanza=curriculum archive-push %p'. Scaffold systemd units pgbackrest-full.timer (Sun 02:00) and pgbackrest-incr.timer (daily 02:00).
recommendations.rationale: pgBackRest 2.58.0 (released 2026-01-19) is the current release and the de-facto standard for self-hosted Postgres PITR; WAL archiving is what turns 'nightly backup' (24h RPO) into minutes-level RPO. aes-256-cbc is the only built-in repo encryption type, satisfying at-rest encryption for medical-school data without external KMS (none exists on-prem). Retention must be explicit or the repo grows unbounded on a fixed-size VM.
recommendations.tooling: pgBackRest 2.58.0 (verify: 2.55.0+ required for per-backup verify --set); Postgres 16 (EOL Nov 2028); zstd compression built in; systemd timers (no cron drift, journald logging).
recommendations.enforcement: Stack smoke-test CI job runs `pgbackrest check --stanza=curriculum` inside the compose stack (fails if archive_command cannot push a WAL segment); harness hygiene PreToolUse hook + gitleaks-style CI scan deny any commit containing repo1-cipher-pass= with a literal value; a config-lint script asserts the shipped pgbackrest.conf parses and contains cipher-type, retention-full, and bundle keys (drift check against template); nightly systemd service runs `pgbackrest verify --stanza=curriculum` with OnFailure= alert unit.

recommendations.practice: Scaffold a weekly automated restore drill (scripts/restore-drill.sh + systemd timer): `pgbackrest restore` the latest backup into a scratch data directory, start a throwaway Postgres 16 container on it, replay WAL to latest, then run a smoke-query suite: (1) exact row counts of curriculum-critical tables vs a committed manifest, (2) `SELECT count(*) FROM pg_policies` and `relforcerowsecurity` true for every RLS table, (3) `SELECT extversion FROM pg_extension WHERE extname='vector'` equals pinned version, (4) canary pgvector query `ORDER BY embedding <=> :canary LIMIT 5` must return a pinned ID list (catches HNSW corruption that no checksum sees), (5) pg_amcheck for heap+btree. The drill writes restore-metrics.json {rto_seconds, wal_replay_lag_seconds, backup_age_hours} — these ARE the documented RTO/RPO numbers.
recommendations.rationale: pgBackRest's built-in `verify` command (per-backup via --set since 2.55.0) checks repo file checksums and WAL consistency but does NOT prove a restore boots into a queryable cluster with intact RLS and vector indexes — only a real restore does. The vitabaks/pgbackrest_auto pattern (restore + pg_dump sequential read for physical validation + amcheck for logical) validates the approach, but a purpose-built ~200-line script is preferable to vendoring a large bash tool: the drill must know this schema's RLS tables and canary vectors. 'We had backups but never restored one' is the canonical failure for exactly this kind of years-curated data.
recommendations.tooling: pgBackRest 2.58.0 restore + verify; pgvector/pgvector:0.8.5-pg16 image for the scratch instance; pg_amcheck (contrib, PG16: heap + btree only — it cannot check HNSW/IVFFlat, hence the canary query); vitabaks/pgbackrest_auto (MIT) as the reference pattern; systemd timer + OnFailure= unit posting to self-hosted Alertmanager/ntfy/Healthchecks instance (on-prem constraint: no external SaaS paging).
recommendations.enforcement: systemd timer weekly with OnFailure= alert (page-on-red, not report-on-green); a scheduled GitHub Actions workflow on the on-prem self-hosted runner also invokes the drill and FAILS the workflow if restore-metrics.json is missing, older than 8 days, rto_seconds > target, or backup_age_hours > RPO budget — stale-metrics-fail-loud prevents the silent-skip failure mode; smoke suite exits nonzero on any assertion, including a seeded positive control (a query that MUST fail if RLS were dropped) so a vacuously green drill is impossible.

recommendations.practice: Ship pg_dump logical backups as the secondary format and smoke-test dump/restore in CI: nightly `pg_dump -Fc --no-owner` retained 14 days alongside pgBackRest (physical backups cannot cross major versions; the custom-format dump is the pg16→pg17+ upgrade and vendor-exit format). CI job (on migration or schema-package changes): start pgvector/pgvector:0.8.5-pg16, apply all Drizzle migrations + seed fixture graph with embeddings, pg_dump -Fc, pg_restore into a second fresh container, run the same smoke-query suite, and record HNSW rebuild time separately.
recommendations.rationale: pg_restore recreates pgvector indexes from scratch — HNSW rebuild is the dominant cost of logical restore (needs maintenance_work_mem sized to the index and shm for parallel builds), so logical-path RTO must be measured, not assumed equal to physical RTO. Testing the dump/restore round-trip in CI catches dump-breaking schema constructs (e.g., objects owned by the migrator role that --no-owner must neutralize under the two-role model) the day they are introduced, not at upgrade time.
recommendations.tooling: pg_dump/pg_restore 16.x client tools; pgvector/pgvector:0.8.5-pg16 official image (tag pattern <ext-version>-pg<major>; pgvector 0.8.5 released 2026-07-08, and 0.8.3/0.8.4 fixed HNSW corruption/vacuum bugs — pin ≥0.8.4); Docker with --shm-size ≥ maintenance_work_mem for parallel HNSW builds.
recommendations.enforcement: Required PR check `dump-restore-smoke` triggered by paths packages/schema/** and drizzle/**; the job diffs `pg_dump --schema-only` output of source vs restored container (normalized) and fails on drift; asserts smoke suite green; uploads timing as a job artifact so RTO regressions are visible in PR history.

recommendations.practice: Encode expand-contract (parallel change) as the only path for renames/drops/type changes, detected mechanically by Squawk's data-loss rules plus an ADR gate: enable ban-drop-column, ban-drop-table, ban-drop-database, ban-drop-not-null, changing-column-type, adding-not-nullable-field (Squawk 2.59.0 — note it DOES cover destructive DDL, not just locking, so use its Postgres-grammar parser instead of fragile regex). A CI script runs squawk on only the NEW migration files in the PR; if any data-loss rule fires, the gate requires a matching docs/adr/destructive/<migration_filename>.md containing required headings (What data is lost / Expand phase migration ref / Verified backfill complete query / Rollback story) — else the PR fails.
recommendations.rationale: Drizzle-kit happily generates `ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN` on a rename (its interactive rename prompt does not exist in CI/agent context), which is instant, unlogged data loss against a live curriculum graph. Squawk gives parser-accurate detection; the ADR file requirement converts 'destructive change' from a lint warning someone suppresses into an artifact a human reviewer and the agent harness can both check. Contract-phase drops become legitimate only with the paper trail proving the expand phase shipped and the backfill completed.
recommendations.tooling: Squawk 2.59.0 (squawk-cli on npm/PyPI; supports per-file rule disable comments for the allowlist path) with .squawk.toml pinning the rule set; drizzle-kit pinned exactly (1.0.0-rc line as of May 2026 — pin the exact rc or stay on last 0.31.x until 1.0 stable; migration-file format churn mid-rc is a real risk); sbdchd/squawk-action for PR annotation.
recommendations.enforcement: Required PR check `migration-safety`: (1) squawk lint on changed drizzle/*.sql with locking rules as errors, (2) node script mapping fired data-loss rules → required ADR path, failing if absent; Claude Code PreToolUse hook denies Write/Edit that introduces DROP TABLE/DROP COLUMN/ALTER ... TYPE/SET NOT NULL into drizzle/**/*.sql unless the matching ADR file already exists in the working tree (guides the agent to write the ADR first); forward-only rule already in corpus stays.

recommendations.practice: Scaffold a batched-backfill runner (packages/schema/backfills/) that runs OUTSIDE migration transactions: template script does keyset-paginated UPDATE batches (e.g. 5k rows by PK) with per-batch commit, a backfill_progress checkpoint table (backfill_name, last_key, rows_done, updated_at) for idempotent resume, SET lock_timeout='2s' and statement_timeout='30s' per batch, and runs as the app/API role (RLS-exempt path via a dedicated backfill role if needed) — never as part of `drizzle-kit migrate`.
recommendations.rationale: A backfill inside a migration transaction holds row locks for the whole table, blows past lock_timeout doctrine, bloats WAL in one burst (pressuring pgBackRest archiving), and cannot resume after a failure at row 8M of 10M. Checkpointed batches make backfills killable, resumable, and observable — a prerequisite for the expand-contract contract phase ('drop only after backfill_progress shows complete').
recommendations.tooling: Node 22 script using the existing Drizzle client; postgres advisory lock (pg_advisory_lock on backfill name hash) so two runners can't race; template includes a vitest test that kills the runner mid-batch and asserts resume-from-checkpoint.
recommendations.enforcement: CI `migration-safety` check additionally parses new drizzle/*.sql with the squawk parser and FAILS on any DML statement (UPDATE/DELETE/INSERT ... SELECT) inside a migration file — DML belongs in backfills/; scaffolded backfill template ships with the kill-and-resume test wired into the standard validate chain so an agent-authored backfill without checkpointing fails tests, not review.

recommendations.practice: Add a pre-deploy 'migration dry-run against last night's restored backup' CI stage: the release workflow (on-prem runner) reuses the restore-drill scratch cluster (or restores the latest pgBackRest backup fresh), then executes all pending Drizzle migrations against real production data with lock_timeout='5s'/statement_timeout='5min', capturing per-migration wall time and any lock-timeout aborts; gate fails on error or budget overrun.
recommendations.rationale: Squawk reasons about statements; only running the migration against production-shaped data catches the real killers: a 40-minute index build on the 10k-node graph's edge table, a CHECK constraint the live data violates, an ALTER that rewrites a table that fit in dev but not on the VM's disk. This also makes the restore drill load-bearing twice — the backup gets exercised every release, not just weekly.
recommendations.tooling: pgBackRest 2.58.0 restore --delta to scratch path; drizzle-kit migrate (pinned) pointed at the scratch cluster via MIGRATION_DATABASE_URL; GitHub Actions self-hosted runner on-prem (the backup repo never leaves the network).
recommendations.enforcement: release.yml job `migrate-dry-run` with `needs: [restore-scratch]` and required before `deploy-server`; job fails on nonzero drizzle exit, on any 'canceling statement due to lock timeout' in logs (grep gate), or total time > budget; branch protection makes the release workflow the only path to the deploy environment (GitHub environment protection rule on `production`).

recommendations.practice: Scaffold a version-handshake contract: server exposes GET /api/meta returning {serverVersion, apiVersion, schemaVersion: <latest applied drizzle migration id>, minClientVersion}; a global Hono middleware (registered before auth, covering all /api routes) reads the x-client-version header the Tauri client injects (single-sourced from the workspace root version → tauri.conf.json → build-time constant) and rejects semver < MIN_CLIENT_VERSION with HTTP 426 Upgrade Required + application/problem+json body {type:'.../client-update-required', code:'CLIENT_UPDATE_REQUIRED', minClientVersion, clientVersion}. The SPA's single fetch wrapper pattern-matches the typed 426 and renders a full-screen 'Update required — your IT-managed install will update automatically; contact IT if urgent' state (keyboard-reachable, WCAG AA).
recommendations.rationale: Intune-deployed .exe fleets WILL lag the server by days (check-in cadence + ring deadlines), so the failure mode of an uncoordinated deploy is every installed client breaking with undefined behavior. A typed handshake converts skew from 'random 500s/misparsed JSON in a medical-school demo' into one deliberate, rendered screen. Missing header = oldest-possible client = reject, so pre-handshake builds are also safe.
recommendations.tooling: Hono 4.x middleware + hc typed client; semver compare via a 20-line vendored function (no dep needed); problem+json per RFC 9457; Tauri 2 app version from tauri.conf.json fed by pnpm workspace root version (syncpack or a version-sync script keeps them identical).
recommendations.enforcement: Route-coverage vitest: introspect the Hono route table and assert every route except /api/meta and /healthz sits behind the skew middleware (a new route added without it fails the test, so protection is structural, not conventional); SPA vitest renders the 426 path from a fixture; CI version-sync check fails if package.json version ≠ tauri.conf.json version ≠ the constant the client sends.

recommendations.practice: Commit openapi.json (generated from @hono/zod-openapi route definitions) and gate PRs with oasdiff: required check `api-contract` runs (1) regenerate-and-diff — regenerated spec must equal the committed one (fails on drift, so the committed spec is always truthful), then (2) oasdiff/oasdiff-action/breaking@v0 with base: 'origin/main:openapi.json', revision: 'HEAD:openapi.json', fail-on: ERR — breaking API changes become unmergeable rather than discoverable in production. A companion script enforces the coupling rule: if the PR carries an oasdiff ignore-file entry (deliberate break), it must ALSO bump MIN_CLIENT_VERSION in the same diff, or the check fails.
recommendations.rationale: Hono RPC's hc<AppType> gives compile-time safety only inside one commit — it says nothing about the previous release's clients. oasdiff is the mechanical breaking-change oracle (current action v0.1.5, 2026-07-05, running oasdiff v1.22.0; @v0 is the maintained moving tag). Tying allowlisted breaks to a forced minClientVersion bump makes the version handshake and the API contract provably consistent: you cannot break old clients without simultaneously locking them out cleanly.
recommendations.tooling: @hono/zod-openapi (spec from the same Zod schemas Drizzle-adjacent validators use); oasdiff/oasdiff-action/breaking@v0 (successor of Tufin/oasdiff-action; inputs base/revision/fail-on; optional github-token for PR review comments); node script for the bump-coupling rule.
recommendations.enforcement: Both steps in one required status check under branch protection; the regenerate-diff step also runs in the Claude Code Stop-gate validate chain so the agent cannot end a turn with a stale committed spec.

recommendations.practice: N-1 compatibility test lane: every release archives a contract artifact — openapi.json, the emitted hc<AppType> declaration (.d.ts), and recorded request/response fixtures for the critical flows (auth handshake, graph query, coverage-matrix data, extraction-QA submit). tests/skew/ is a separate pnpm project that (a) typechecks the PREVIOUS release's .d.ts client calls against the new server types and (b) boots the new server (with migrated scratch DB) and replays the previous release's recorded fixtures, asserting 2xx + response-shape parity (or a clean 426 if minClientVersion was deliberately bumped).
recommendations.rationale: oasdiff proves the spec didn't break; the skew lane proves the running server actually still serves last release's traffic — catching breaks that live below the spec (serializer changes, enum widening the old client can't parse, RLS/authz changes that 403 old flows). N-1 is exactly the guaranteed skew window of an Intune ring rollout.
recommendations.tooling: pnpm workspace project tests/skew; contract artifacts stored in-repo under contracts/vN/ at release time (on-prem: no external registry needed; a Verdaccio instance is an alternative if artifacts get large); vitest + undici for fixture replay; the release workflow's tag job writes contracts/v<next>/ automatically.
recommendations.enforcement: Required branch-protection check `skew-n1` on PRs touching apps/server/**; release.yml refuses to tag if contracts/ for the current version is missing (script gate); fixture-recording script is scaffolded so 'no fixtures for new endpoint' fails a coverage assertion (every route in openapi.json must appear in at least one fixture or an explicit exempt list).

recommendations.practice: Encode deploy ordering as a release-workflow DAG, not a runbook: release.yml jobs with hard `needs:` chaining — 1) backup-checkpoint (pgbackrest backup --type=incr + verify --set on it), 2) migrate-dry-run (against restored backup), 3) migrate-prod (expand-phase only), 4) deploy-server (requires skew-n1 green: new server must serve N-1 clients), 5) package-client (.msi/.exe signed, wrapped .intunewin via IntuneWinAppUtil, assignment created/handed to IT with ring groups: pilot → faculty → all, staggered required-deadlines). Contract-phase migrations are a SEPARATE later release gated by fleet telemetry: the server logs min observed x-client-version, and a CI gate blocks any migration whose ADR marks it 'contract' until the recorded fleet-minimum ≥ the version that stopped using the dropped column.
recommendations.rationale: The safe order (backup → migrate expand → server → clients → later contract) only survives if the pipeline physically cannot run it out of order. Intune has no percentage rollout for Win32 apps — staged rollout is ring groups with staggered deadlines (Deployment Plans now formalize this), and device check-in latency means the old client population persists for days; the telemetry gate turns 'wait long enough' into a measured precondition.
recommendations.tooling: GitHub Actions on-prem runner; environment protection rules on `production`; IntuneWinAppUtil (Win32 Content Prep Tool) + Microsoft Graph deviceAppManagement API for assignment automation (or artifact handoff if IT owns the tenant); server-side min-version gauge exported to the on-prem Prometheus and snapshotted to a JSON the CI gate reads.
recommendations.enforcement: Job DAG via needs: (ordering is unforgeable within the workflow); environment protection = deploys only via this workflow; `contract-migration-gate` script: for each pending migration with ADR phase:contract, compare ADR's requiredFleetMin against the telemetry snapshot, exit 1 if unmet — wired both as a required check and into the Stop-gate so the agent cannot 'helpfully' ship the drop early.

pitfalls: Treating `pgbackrest check` or `pgbackrest verify` as a restore test: verify (per-backup --set since 2.55.0) validates repo checksums and WAL consistency but never boots a cluster — only the full restore drill proves RLS policies, roles, and vector indexes come back; both are needed, they are not substitutes.

Encrypted repo with the passphrase stored next to it (or only in the live VM's config): losing repo1-cipher-pass loses every backup, and a passphrase that burns down with the server is equivalent to no backup — the drill must periodically run from the offline escrow copy of the passphrase to prove the escrow works.

Assuming corruption checks cover pgvector: pg_amcheck on PG16 validates heap and btree only — HNSW/IVFFlat corruption (real bugs fixed as recently as pgvector 0.8.3/0.8.4, June 2026) sails through checksums and amcheck; only a canary similarity query with pinned expected top-k IDs catches it, and the extension must be pinned ≥0.8.4.

Quoting one RTO number: logical restore RTO is dominated by HNSW index rebuild (maintenance_work_mem + shm-dependent), so pg_restore RTO can be a large multiple of pgBackRest physical-restore RTO — the drill must measure and report both paths or the documented number is fiction.

Believing 'Squawk covers locking, not data loss' (the premise is outdated): Squawk 2.x ships ban-drop-column/ban-drop-table/ban-drop-database/ban-drop-not-null/changing-column-type — the real gap is that a per-file linter cannot know whether the expand phase shipped and the backfill finished, which is why the ADR/allowlist layer (not another regex parser) is the missing piece.

drizzle-kit renames generate silent DROP+ADD (data loss) because its interactive rename prompt never fires in CI/agent runs; additionally drizzle-kit is mid-1.0.0-rc churn (rc.3, May 2026) — an unpinned version can change migration file layout and break the destructive-DDL gate's file discovery.

A version handshake enforced by convention: if the skew middleware is mounted per-route (or someone adds a router above it), new endpoints silently bypass it — without the route-table coverage test the handshake decays into documentation; also clients that predate the handshake send no header at all and must be rejected, not defaulted to 'allowed'.

Declaring the fleet upgraded when Intune reports 'assignment succeeded': Win32 required installs are eventually-consistent (device check-in cadence, ring-staggered deadlines, offline laptops), so contract-phase migrations gated on deployment status instead of observed x-client-version telemetry will drop columns that live clients still read.

topic: Governance of the public agent-harness template repo: license policy (npm + crates + model weights), third-party notice generation, template-instantiation CI, and testing the enforcement layer (Claude Code hooks) itself

recommendations.practice: License the template repo Apache-2.0 with an explicit 0BSD (or MIT-0) carve-out for everything under template/** (the PyScaffold pattern: tool code Apache-2.0, emitted scaffold 0BSD), stated in LICENSE, README, and per-file SPDX headers, so generated projects carry the consumer's license with zero attribution obligation and never falsely inherit the harness's copyright.
recommendations.rationale: Apache-2.0's explicit patent grant protects the university and contributors on the tool side; but GitHub's 'Use this template' copies LICENSE verbatim into consumer repos, so without a public-domain-equivalent carve-out every scaffolded medical-school project would wrongly ship the harness's license. PyScaffold has run this exact split (MIT tool + 0BSD templates) for years and it is the recognized answer to 'who owns scaffold output'. The sibling repo already chose Apache-2.0, so this stays consistent.
recommendations.tooling: SPDX-License-Identifier headers (Apache-2.0 for installer/scripts/tests, 0BSD for template/**); REUSE spec 3.3 with REUSE.toml annotations for un-headerable files (JSON, lockfiles, dotless workflow templates); fsfe/reuse-tool (reuse >= 5.x, current docs 6.2.0) and fsfe/reuse-action.
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: fsfe/reuse-action runs `reuse lint` as a required check — any new file missing an SPDX header or REUSE.toml entry fails the PR; the installer's post-scaffold step is unit-tested (node:test) to assert it writes the consumer-chosen LICENSE and strips harness SPDX headers from emitted files, and the instantiation-CI job greps generated output for 'Apache License' / harness copyright strings and fails on a match.

recommendations.practice: Add an npm-side dependency-license gate mirroring the planned cargo-deny [licenses] allowlist: `pnpm licenses list --json --prod` piped to a small Node policy script (or license-compliance --production --allow) with a permissive allowlist (MIT;Apache-2.0;ISC;BSD-2-Clause;BSD-3-Clause;0BSD;BlueOak-1.0.0;CC0-1.0;Unlicense) and hard denial of GPL/AGPL/LGPL/SSPL for the shipped university-internal .exe.
recommendations.rationale: The corpus only gated crate licenses; npm deps ship inside the signed Windows .exe too. Classic davglass/license-checker walks node_modules directly and cannot handle pnpm's symlinked store, silently under-reporting — the gate must be pnpm-native. `pnpm licenses list` (pnpm 10/11) supports --json, --prod, and --filter for per-workspace-package scoping in the monorepo; license-compliance exits 1 on violation with production-only mode, making it CI-composable. A copyleft denylist is the right posture for a distributed (even internally) binary.
recommendations.tooling: pnpm 11 `licenses list --json --prod --filter ./apps/desktop...` piped to scripts/license-policy.mjs; alternatively license-compliance (npm) with --allow list; cargo-deny (EmbarkStudios) [licenses] allow = [...] in deny.toml for crates.
recommendations.enforcement: Both checks wired as `pnpm licenses:check` inside the template's validate chain, so the Stop gate itself refuses to end a turn on a license violation; a required CI job runs the same script plus `cargo deny check licenses`; policy script exits non-zero on any package outside the allowlist or with UNKNOWN license (unknown = fail, with an explicit reviewed-exceptions map in the script, not a warning).

recommendations.practice: Generate THIRD-PARTY-NOTICES for the shipped .exe automatically from both ecosystems — @quantco/pnpm-licenses generate-disclaimer for npm (fed by `pnpm licenses list --json --prod --filter` for the desktop app's graph) and cargo-about generate (about.toml accepted list + about.hbs) for crates — concatenated into one NOTICES file bundled as a Tauri resource and attached as a release asset.
recommendations.rationale: MIT/BSD/Apache all require preserving copyright/license text in distributed binaries; a university shipping an internal .exe still distributes. Quantco/pnpm-licenses is the pnpm-correct disclaimer generator (list + generate-disclaimer, --prod, --json-input, --output-file, programmatic API with resolveLicensesBestEffort); cargo-about is the Embark-maintained Rust counterpart with its own accepted-licenses config that cross-checks the cargo-deny policy.
recommendations.tooling: @quantco/pnpm-licenses (npx @quantco/pnpm-licenses generate-disclaimer --json-input --output-file), cargo-about >= 0.6 with about.toml `accepted = [...]`, a scripts/gen-notices.mjs concatenator, tauri.conf.json bundle.resources entry for the NOTICES file.
recommendations.enforcement: release.yml gate before code-signing: regenerate NOTICES in CI and fail on `git diff --exit-code` against the committed file (stale-notice detection), fail if either generator exits non-zero or output is below a sanity byte-floor, assert the file is present inside the bundled app resources, then upload as a release asset; the job is a required dependency of the signing/publish step.

recommendations.practice: Treat model weights as licensed dependencies: the eval/provenance config schema gains required fields `model_license` (identifier, e.g. Apache-2.0, llama3.3-community, qwen-license) and `model_license_url` alongside the existing GGUF sha256, and the docs state the obligations matrix — Llama-community licenses require shipping a copy of the agreement, a 'Built with Llama' display, a Notice file, and Llama-prefixed derivative names even for university-internal 'make available' deployment; Apache-2.0 weights (Qwen3 family, Mistral 7B/NeMo/Small, Ministral 3 / Mistral 3 family) carry only NOTICE preservation; GGUF quantization is a derivative work, so the original weight license follows the .gguf file.
recommendations.rationale: The university deploys llama.cpp-served GGUFs to staff/students, which plausibly meets Llama's 'distribute or make available' trigger — obligations attach even without external redistribution. License varies within model families (Qwen2.5 3B/72B were NOT Apache-2.0; Ministral 8B was Mistral Research License), so the gate must key on the exact model card, not the family. Recording license next to the hash makes provenance auditable and lets the harness block unlicensed weights mechanically.
recommendations.tooling: JSON Schema (Ajv or zod in packages/eval) validating eval/provenance config; llama.com/llama3_3/license as the canonical Llama-community text; help.mistral.ai license matrix; per-model HuggingFace LICENSE files.
recommendations.enforcement: Schema validation of the eval config runs inside the validate chain (Stop gate blocks until green) and in CI; the PreToolUse write-guard denies edits that add a `.gguf` path or hash without a sibling model_license field (simple structural check in the guard script); for llama-* identifiers, a CI assertion that the repo/app bundle contains the license copy and 'Built with Llama' notice string.

recommendations.practice: Template-instantiation CI ('selftest') as a required status check: on every PR, scaffold a fresh project with the installer into a temp dir on a matrix — ubuntu-latest runs the FULL `pnpm validate` chain plus `cargo deny`/clippy gates against the generated output; windows-latest runs `pnpm tauri build --debug` to prove the signed-exe path compiles — and a step greps the generated tree for leftover `{{`/`}}` placeholder tokens and dotless-workflow rename failures (asserts .github/workflows/ exists and template/github/ residue does not), failing on any hit.
recommendations.rationale: The only proof a template works is instantiating it: the sibling repo's selftest.yml already frames this as 'this matrix IS the release acceptance test'. Windows must be in the matrix because the deliverable is a Windows .exe and Tauri's Rust host has platform-specific build failures ubuntu never sees. Placeholder-scan is needed because GitHub has no native token substitution for template repos — the installer does it, so CI must prove it completed.
recommendations.tooling: GitHub Actions matrix (ubuntu-latest + windows-latest), actions pinned by commit SHA, step-security/harden-runner with egress-policy, the repo's own `npx installer` CLI, pnpm/action-setup + rust-toolchain, `git grep -nE '\{\{[A-Z_]+\}\}'` scan step.
recommendations.enforcement: Repository ruleset marks selftest (all matrix legs) as required status checks for merge to main and for v* tags; release workflow depends on the same jobs so a tag cannot publish an installer whose output does not reach green.

recommendations.practice: For the downstream-update story, keep the bespoke `npx create-*` / `npx <repo> update` CLI with a hashed file manifest (three-way: pristine-at-install vs current vs new-upstream), rather than migrating to copier or cruft — but adopt copier's discipline: record template version + answers in a committed .harness-manifest.json so update can diff and report conflicts instead of clobbering.
recommendations.rationale: copier has the best built-in update mechanism (answers file, diff-replay of template evolution) and cruft bolts the same onto cookiecutter via .cruft.json, but both impose Python/Jinja tooling on a Node/Rust team and fight the dotless-template + hook-manifest design already shipped in the sibling repo (`npx github:BhodiSea/next-supabase-agent-harness update`). A bespoke CLI with recorded template commit + per-file hashes reproduces the essential copier property — consumers can pull harness improvements after divergence with conflict detection — without a second toolchain.
recommendations.tooling: Node 22 installer CLI (installer/cli.mjs pattern), .harness-manifest.json {templateCommit, files: {path: sha256}}, `update` command doing pristine/current/upstream comparison; copier >= 9 and cruft cited as the compared alternatives.
recommendations.enforcement: Installer unit tests (node --test tests/installer) assert: fresh install writes a complete manifest; update on an unmodified project applies cleanly; update on a locally-modified guarded file reports a conflict and exits non-zero instead of overwriting; instantiation CI runs `update` against the previous released tag's output to prove the upgrade path stays green.

recommendations.practice: Unit-test every hook script as a black-box process contract: node:test + spawnSync feeding JSON on stdin to each .mjs hook, asserting the documented Claude Code exit-code contract — Stop gate exits 2 (with stderr reason) while the validate chain is red and 0 when green; PreToolUse write-guard exits 2 / emits permissionDecision:'deny' for protected paths and allows others; Bash-guard denies denylisted commands; malformed or truncated JSON stdin exits non-zero (fail-loud), never 0.
recommendations.rationale: The hooks ARE the product: per the Claude Code hooks reference, exit 2 is the only blocking code (PreToolUse: blocks the tool; Stop: forces Claude to continue), so a Stop hook that crashes with exit 0 — a swallowed exception, an unhandled async rejection — silently disables every guarantee and nothing downstream notices. The sibling repo's tests/hooks/hook-contract.test.mjs already proves this pattern (spawnSync of the hook with fixture stdin, exit-code assertions); the fail-loud-on-malformed-JSON case is the one most templates omit and the one that catches exit-0-on-error regressions.
recommendations.tooling: node:test (built into Node 22), child_process.spawnSync with `input:` fixtures, process.exitCode + process.on('uncaughtException', ...) fail-loud handlers inside every hook; hook sources typechecked via tsc --noEmit --checkJs (or Biome lint) since they are .mjs; shellcheck via shellcheck-action for any residual .sh (lefthook snippets).
recommendations.enforcement: `node --test tests/hooks/*.test.mjs` is a required CI job (hook-contracts in selftest.yml) and also runs in the local validate chain, so the Stop gate cannot go green while its own tests fail; typecheck/shellcheck of hook sources are separate required steps; CODEOWNERS forces maintainer review on template/base/.claude/hooks/**.

recommendations.practice: Deliberate-violation canary (anti-vacuity for the harness): a CI job scaffolds a project, snapshots it green, then injects a known lint error, a type error, and a protected-path write attempt into a scratch copy and asserts (a) the validate chain exits non-zero, (b) the Stop-gate hook — invoked with real hook-JSON stdin against that state — exits exactly 2, and (c) after reverting the injection both return to 0/green in the same job.
recommendations.rationale: A test suite that only checks the happy path cannot detect the catastrophic failure mode (gate exits 0 on error). The canary is the harness-level analogue of the repo's existing rls_verify 'seeded positive control (no vacuous green)' fix: proving red-goes-red AND green-goes-green in one job means any regression that makes the gate permanently green (or permanently red) fails CI immediately. This directly answers the gap: the enforcement layer is otherwise unverified code.
recommendations.tooling: A selftest.yml `canary` job: cp -r of the instantiated project to $RUNNER_TEMP, sed/append of a known-bad token (e.g. `const x: string = 1` and a Biome violation), invocation of stop-validate-gate.mjs with a fixture Stop-event JSON, bash assertions on $? (expect 2, then expect 0 after revert).
recommendations.enforcement: Required status check; the job itself asserts both directions so it cannot pass vacuously; a step also asserts the injected files were actually modified (grep for the token) before trusting the red result — guarding the guard test against silent no-op injection.

recommendations.practice: Version-stamp the hook scripts: every hook carries a HARNESS_HOOK_VERSION constant (and the installer records per-file sha256 in .harness-manifest.json); a `doctor` command in instantiated projects compares local hook hashes/versions against the pinned template commit and reports 'stale hook — upstream fix available' distinctly from 'locally modified hook'.
recommendations.rationale: When a Stop-gate bug is fixed upstream (e.g. the exit-0-on-error class), every previously instantiated project keeps the broken enforcement forever unless it can detect drift. Hash-manifest + version constants make hook provenance mechanically checkable, and distinguishing intentional local edits from staleness prevents the update path from either clobbering customizations or missing security-relevant fixes.
recommendations.tooling: installer/lib manifest writer, `npx <repo> doctor` and `update` subcommands, GitHub Releases tags (v*) as the upstream version source, optional Renovate rule (renovate.json already present) pinning the harness dependency so updates surface as PRs.
recommendations.enforcement: Template-repo CI asserts every hook file contains a parseable HARNESS_HOOK_VERSION and that it was bumped when the file changed (diff-aware check script); in instantiated projects, the validate chain runs `doctor --quiet` which warns on stale and exits non-zero if a hook hash matches a version listed in the template's published known-bad list (a security-advisory-driven denylist file fetched at install/update time, checked offline thereafter).

recommendations.practice: Public-repo hygiene as gates, not badges: SECURITY.md with a private disclosure channel (GitHub Security Advisories 'Report a vulnerability', no public issues), ossf/scorecard-action v2 on cron + push with publish_results: true, id-token: write, SARIF upload to code scanning, and the README badge; GitHub Repository Rules (not classic branch protection — Scorecard can read Rules with the default GITHUB_TOKEN) requiring the selftest/hook-contract/canary/reuse/license checks; CODEOWNERS pinning template/base/.claude/hooks/**, .github/workflows/**, and installer/** to maintainers; all actions pinned by full commit SHA behind step-security/harden-runner.
recommendations.rationale: A template repo transitively configures every consumer's security posture, so its own must be demonstrably hardened — Scorecard checks (pinned dependencies, token permissions, branch protection, security policy) are exactly the properties the harness preaches, and publishing results makes regressions visible. Repository Rules over classic protection is the Scorecard team's own recommendation for readability without an admin PAT. CODEOWNERS on the guarded paths means the enforcement layer cannot change without human review even if an agent opens the PR.
recommendations.tooling: ossf/scorecard-action v2 (SHA-pinned), github/codeql-action/upload-sarif, step-security/harden-runner (already in selftest.yml at v2.20.0), GitHub Repository Rulesets, CODEOWNERS, permissions: contents: read as workflow default.
recommendations.enforcement: scorecard.yml scheduled + push workflow (exists; verify publish_results and badge wiring); ruleset marks scorecard-relevant checks required and blocks force-push/deletion on main and release/*; a hygiene CI script (scripts/hygiene.mjs pattern) fails if any workflow step uses a mutable tag instead of a SHA, if SECURITY.md loses its advisory link, or if CODEOWNERS stops covering the guarded globs.

pitfalls: Classic license-checker (davglass) and forks that walk node_modules directly cannot traverse pnpm's symlinked virtual store — they silently under-report dependencies, so an npm license gate built on them passes while unvetted licenses ship in the .exe; only pnpm-native tools (`pnpm licenses list`, @quantco/pnpm-licenses, pnpm-license-checker) are trustworthy here.

`pnpm licenses list` in a workspace aggregates ALL projects' dependencies by default; per-artifact notices for the desktop .exe must use --filter to scope to apps/desktop's production graph, or the NOTICES file both over-attributes (server-only deps) and masks gaps.

The Stop-gate's catastrophic failure mode is exiting 0 on internal error: a swallowed exception or unhandled async rejection in the hook yields exit 0, Claude Code treats it as 'allow stop', and every quality guarantee silently evaporates — hooks must install fail-loud uncaughtException/unhandledRejection handlers that set a non-zero exit, and tests must include the malformed-stdin and thrown-exception cases, not just happy paths.

GitHub 'Use this template' copies LICENSE verbatim and offers no placeholder substitution — without a 0BSD/MIT-0 output carve-out plus an installer step that writes the consumer's license, every scaffolded university project falsely ships the harness's Apache-2.0 copyright, and no existing gate would notice.

Model licenses vary WITHIN families: Qwen2.5 was Apache-2.0 except the 3B and 72B variants (Qwen license, >100M-MAU clause), Ministral 8B was Mistral Research License while Mistral 7B/NeMo/Small were Apache-2.0 — gating on family reputation instead of the exact model card is how a non-commercial-only weight ends up in production; and GGUF quantization is a derivative work, so re-quantizing never launders the license.

Llama-community obligations trigger on 'distribute or make available', which internal university deployment to students/staff plausibly satisfies — the agreement copy, Notice file, and 'Built with Llama' display are owed even though nothing leaves the org; treating 'internal' as 'exempt' is the common legal miss.

A canary test that only asserts red-goes-red is itself vacuous (a gate that always fails passes it), and one that only asserts green-goes-green misses the exit-0-on-error class — the canary job must assert both directions in one run and additionally verify the fault was actually injected (grep for the token) before trusting the red result.

Dotless workflow templates (template/**/github/workflows stored without the leading dot to keep the template repo's own CI and GitHub App workflow-permission restrictions from firing) create a new failure mode: if the installer's rename step regresses, instantiated projects silently have NO CI at all — instantiation CI must positively assert .github/workflows/*.yml exists and runs in the generated project, not merely that no {{tokens}} remain.

topic: Enterprise Windows environment resilience for a Tauri 2 desktop app: TLS-inspecting proxies and private CAs, EDR/AV interference, VDI/Citrix and roaming profiles, long paths, offline/degraded-network UX, and clock-skew-tolerant auth

recommendations.practice: Pin every Rust-side HTTP path to OS-trust-store certificate verification. tauri-plugin-updater v2 (reqwest 0.13) already defaults to rustls-platform-verifier — pin that floor. tauri-plugin-http v2 still pins reqwest 0.12 where its default `rustls-tls` feature = webpki (Mozilla) roots that reject the enterprise private CA; if used, declare it with `default-features = false, features = ["rustls-tls-native-roots", ...]` or `native-tls` (SChannel). Any first-party reqwest client must use rustls-platform-verifier 0.6.x (`ClientConfig` via `with_platform_verifier()`, the approach used by 1Password/Signal/rustup) or reqwest 0.13 defaults.
recommendations.rationale: Zscaler/Palo Alto TLS inspection re-signs every HTTPS response with a private root CA distributed only via the Windows machine store. webpki-roots-only clients fail 100% of handshakes on day one; the failure is silent-looking ('connection error') and does not reproduce on developer machines.
recommendations.tooling: rustls-platform-verifier 0.6.2; reqwest 0.13.x (changelog: 'rustls roots features removed, rustls-platform-verifier is used by default'); tauri-plugin-updater 2.x (reqwest 0.13); tauri-plugin-http 2.x (reqwest 0.12 — needs explicit rustls-tls-native-roots/native-tls); cargo-deny >= 0.17
recommendations.enforcement: CI gate: `cargo deny check bans` with `[bans] deny = [{ name = "webpki-roots" }]` plus a `cargo tree -i webpki-roots --locked` step that must report nothing in apps/desktop; Rust unit test asserting the shared client builder is constructed from `rustls_platform_verifier` config; Claude Code PreToolUse hook on `**/Cargo.toml` rejecting writes containing `rustls-tls-webpki-roots` or bare `webpki-roots`

recommendations.practice: Route all interactive traffic (Hono API, llama.cpp SSE) through WebView2's Chromium network stack via fetch from the SPA; confine Rust-side HTTP to the updater. WebView2 resolves the system proxy (WinINET, including PAC files) and can traverse NTLM/Kerberos-authenticated proxies with SSO, and verifies certs against the Windows store; reqwest supports only env-var and static registry proxies — no PAC evaluation, no SSPI auth — so Rust-side API calls will fail on exactly the networks WebView2 handles fine. Keep `WindowConfig.additionalBrowserArgs` (`--proxy-server=...`) documented as the IT escape hatch.
recommendations.rationale: PAC + authenticated proxies are the norm on university/medical-school fleets. Making the browser stack the single network path means proxy/PAC/auth/trust are all handled by code Microsoft ships and IT already trusts, instead of reimplementing WinHTTP semantics in Rust.
recommendations.tooling: WebView2 Evergreen runtime; Tauri 2 WindowConfig.additionalBrowserArgs; @tauri-apps/plugin-http explicitly NOT installed; reqwest proxy limitations (env + Windows registry static only)
recommendations.enforcement: ESLint 9 flat-config `no-restricted-imports` banning `@tauri-apps/plugin-http` in apps/desktop/src; dependency-cruiser rule forbidding any module outside src-tauri/updater from importing HTTP client crates (mirrored in Rust by cargo-deny banning `tauri-plugin-http` from the workspace); the mitmproxy CI lane (next item) proves the webview path actually works through an intercepting proxy

recommendations.practice: Add a TLS-interception smoke lane on windows-latest: install mitmproxy, `certutil -addstore Root mitmproxy-ca-cert.cer` into LocalMachine Root, set WinINET proxy (registry ProxyEnable/ProxyServer) and HTTPS_PROXY, then drive the built .exe with tauri-driver/WebDriver against a stubbed /readyz + one authenticated API call, asserting success through the intercepting proxy and zero ERR_CERT_* / handshake failures for both the webview path and the updater's Rust path.
recommendations.rationale: This is the only test that exercises the exact production failure mode (re-signed certificates + forced proxy) end-to-end against the real binary; unit-level TLS assertions cannot catch a plugin that silently ships the wrong root store, and the WebView2 'measures' doc lists ERR_CERT_* under TLS-inspection misalignment as a top enterprise breakage.
recommendations.tooling: mitmproxy 12.x; tauri-driver 2.x + Microsoft Edge WebDriver (msedgedriver matching the runner's WebView2); GitHub Actions windows-latest; certutil
recommendations.enforcement: Required PR status check `proxy-interception-smoke` in the hardened CI workflow; the job fails on any certificate or proxy error string in app logs; included in the release workflow so an artifact cannot ship without passing

recommendations.practice: Make the installer work with zero internet egress and enterprise install conventions: NSIS `installMode: perMachine`, `webviewInstallMode: { "type": "offlineInstaller" }` (Tauri's default `downloadBootstrapper` downloads WebView2 from Microsoft's CDN at install time and fails on egress-filtered networks; offlineInstaller embeds ~127 MB), and sign the .exe + installer with Azure Artifact Signing (formerly Trusted Signing) with an RFC 3161 timestamp — the 3-day certs stay valid indefinitely once timestamped, and SmartScreen reputation keys on the durable publisher identity.
recommendations.rationale: On an on-prem, proxied fleet the default bootstrapper is a guaranteed day-one installer failure that presents as a corrupt package; per-machine install avoids per-user AppData installer copies that FSLogix/VDI multiplies; consistent signed identity is what AppLocker/WDAC publisher rules and SmartScreen reputation attach to.
recommendations.tooling: Tauri 2 bundler (NSIS); azure/trusted-signing-action (or `signtool sign` via dlib) + `signtool verify /pa /all`; tauri.conf.json bundle.windows.webviewInstallMode
recommendations.enforcement: Vitest config test validating tauri.conf.json against a repo-owned ajv JSON schema that hard-requires perMachine + offlineInstaller (fails the fast lane); release CI gate running `signtool verify /pa /all` and failing if unsigned or untimestamped; PreToolUse hook rejecting edits that set webviewInstallMode back to downloadBootstrapper

recommendations.practice: Gate every release artifact through Defender and ship an IT pre-clearance kit: run `MpCmdRun.exe -SignatureUpdate` then `MpCmdRun.exe -Scan -ScanType 3 -File <artifact> -DisableRemediation` (custom scan; MUST use -DisableRemediation because plain return 0 also means 'malware found and remediated' — i.e., Defender may have quarantined your artifact and still exited 0), failing the workflow on return code 2. Version a docs-as-code IT checklist: WDSI file submission ('Software developer – false positive') for each blocked build, AppLocker/WDAC publisher rules for the app exe AND msedgewebview2.exe, scoped (never global) exclusions, preserve WebView2 runtime/UDF ACLs and LowIL, no DLL injection into WebView2 processes, allow WebView2 child processes (renderer/GPU/network/Crashpad) and Evergreen updates. Publish a SHA-256 manifest per release for Defender for Endpoint allow-indicators.
recommendations.rationale: Microsoft's WebView2 'measures' doc (updated 2026-02) enumerates exactly these EDR failure modes — blocked child processes, tightened ACLs, injected DLLs — as the causes of blank screens, freezes, and sign-in loops; and newly-hashed binaries trip SmartScreen until reputation accrues, so pre-clearance is an IT workflow, not a code fix.
recommendations.tooling: MpCmdRun.exe (Defender antimalware platform, doc dated 2026-06-09) on windows-latest; Microsoft Security Intelligence submission portal (microsoft.com/en-us/wdsi/filesubmission); AppLocker publisher rules; docs/it-onboarding.md in-repo
recommendations.enforcement: Release workflow step fails on MpCmdRun nonzero exit; a release-PR lint (danger/custom script) requiring docs/it-onboarding.md and the SHA-256 manifest to be updated whenever the version in tauri.conf.json bumps; checksum manifest generated and attached by CI, never by hand

recommendations.practice: Data-directory discipline for VDI/Citrix/FSLogix: place ALL mutable heavy state — SQLite, caches, logs, and the WebView2 user data folder — under `app_local_data_dir()` (FOLDERID_LocalAppData\<identifier>, where Tauri already defaults the EBWebView UDF) and only small preference files in `app_data_dir()` (FOLDERID_RoamingAppData, which roams/redirects). Add a startup self-check: resolve both dirs, reject UNC (`\\` prefix) and `GetDriveTypeW == DRIVE_REMOTE`, probe write+lock, and refuse SQLite WAL mode on remote filesystems (WAL's shared-memory file misbehaves on SMB) — degrade to a documented relocation or actionable error, never a crash. Set WEBVIEW2_USER_DATA_FOLDER only if IT requires relocating the UDF, and always to a local disk.
recommendations.rationale: Microsoft explicitly warns that a WebView2 UDF on a network drive causes 'slowdowns, crashes, or loss of data'; folder redirection puts Roaming AppData on a UNC share on real fleets; FSLogix containers look local but bloat with EBWebView cache; SQLite over SMB corrupts silently and presents as random app bugs.
recommendations.tooling: Tauri 2 PathResolver (app_data_dir/app_local_data_dir/app_cache_dir/app_log_dir); windows crate GetDriveTypeW; rusqlite `PRAGMA journal_mode`; WEBVIEW2_USER_DATA_FOLDER env var; sample FSLogix redirections.xml excluding EBWebView cache shipped in docs
recommendations.enforcement: Rust unit tests on the self-check module; Windows CI integration test launching the binary with APPDATA=\\localhost\C$\redirect-test (simulated folder redirection) asserting the documented relocation log line and exit-0 degraded behavior; clippy `disallowed-methods` banning `std::env::var("APPDATA")` and direct `dirs`/`known-folders` calls outside the single paths module

recommendations.practice: Long-path hardening: embed a Windows application manifest via `tauri_build::WindowsAttributes::app_manifest` in build.rs containing `<longPathAware>true</longPathAware>` (SMI WindowsSettings namespace), keep the bundle identifier short (path budget for `AppData\Local\<id>\EBWebView\...` trees), and document in the IT checklist that the manifest is inert unless the fleet GPO sets HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem LongPathsEnabled=1. Rust std already auto-converts absolute paths to \\?\ verbatim form for std::fs, so the manifest chiefly protects Win32 calls made by dependencies and child tooling.
recommendations.rationale: Redirected/roaming profiles and FSLogix prepend long UNC prefixes, and WebView2 cache trees nest deeply; MAX_PATH (260) overflows then surface as spurious ERROR_PATH_NOT_FOUND far from the cause. App-side manifest + short identifier is the only part the vendor controls.
recommendations.tooling: tauri-build 2.x WindowsAttributes::app_manifest; mt.exe or a PowerShell resource parser in CI to extract the embedded manifest; tauri.conf.json identifier lint
recommendations.enforcement: CI step on windows-latest extracts the built .exe's embedded manifest and greps for `longPathAware>true` (fail otherwise); Windows integration test creating a >280-char nested path under app-local data and round-tripping a file through the app's storage layer; config test asserting tauri.conf.json identifier length <= 30 chars

recommendations.practice: Treat offline/degraded network as a first-class, tested UX contract: connectivity state derived from an exponential-backoff-with-jitter probe of the API's /readyz (never navigator.onLine alone — validate content-type and zod shape so proxy-injected block pages/interstitials count as DOWN); persistent connection-state indicator using role="status" aria-live="polite" (WCAG 2.2 AA 4.1.3); explicit per-mutation queue-or-block policy — human-in-the-loop QA mutations BLOCK with an actionable retry surface (no silent queues that reorder curriculum-mapping decisions), read paths serve cached data with a staleness banner.
recommendations.rationale: The on-prem API and llama.cpp endpoint WILL be unreachable sometimes (maintenance windows, VLAN moves, proxy outages); a frozen spinner or hung UI is a support ticket and an accessibility failure, while navigator.onLine reports true behind captive proxies that answer every request with an HTML interstitial.
recommendations.tooling: TanStack Query v5 (onlineManager, retry with backoff, staleTime); zod response validation; shadcn/ui status component; Playwright >= 1.53 network APIs (context.setOffline, route.abort, route.fulfill with delay)
recommendations.enforcement: Playwright `degraded-network` project in the fast E2E lane on every PR: `context.setOffline(true)` and `page.route('**/api/**', r => r.abort('connectionrefused'))` plus slow-response fulfill; asserts the documented degraded state (banner `toBeVisible({ timeout: 3000 })`, Tab still moves focus, no wedged spinner) and recovery after reconnect; required status check wired into the Stop-gate validate chain

recommendations.practice: Define interrupted-LLM-stream semantics: llama.cpp's OpenAI-compatible streaming is fetch-POST SSE with NO EventSource auto-reconnect, so make extraction/generation jobs idempotent and resumable server-side (job id + monotonic chunk cursor, Last-Event-ID-style), have the client detect stream death (heartbeat/idle timeout), surface a WCAG-compliant 'generation interrupted — resume' state, and cap resume attempts with backoff. Server replays from the cursor exactly once; duplicate submissions of the same job id are no-ops.
recommendations.rationale: TLS-inspecting proxies and EDR network filters routinely kill long-lived streaming connections mid-response; without resumable jobs the AI extraction pipeline silently loses partial output or double-bills GPU time, and the QA reviewer sees a hung stream.
recommendations.tooling: Hono streamSSE (hono/streaming) on Node 22; AbortController + idle-timeout watchdog in the SPA; job table in Postgres keyed by idempotency key
recommendations.enforcement: Playwright test that aborts the stream route after N chunks (route.abort mid-flight) and asserts the UI reaches 'interrupted, resume available' within budget, then resumes to completion; server vitest asserting replay-with-cursor returns the remaining tokens exactly once and duplicate job submission is idempotent; both in the PR-blocking lane

recommendations.practice: Clock-skew-tolerant Entra ID token validation: a single `verifyEntraToken` wrapper around jose `jwtVerify` with `clockTolerance: 300` (aligns with the Kerberos/AD default 5-minute max skew that managed fleets already tolerate), explicit issuer/audience pinning, and the `currentDate` option injected for deterministic tests; map skew-shaped failures (nbf in future / exp just passed) to a distinct, user-actionable 'check this machine's clock' error instead of a generic 401.
recommendations.rationale: Managed and VDI machines drift — non-persistent VDI images boot with stale clocks and w32time may take minutes to converge — so freshly-minted Entra tokens can carry nbf values seconds in the 'future' relative to the API server; jose applies clockTolerance to nbf/exp (and iat with maxTokenAge), turning a class of mystery auth failures into non-events.
recommendations.tooling: jose v6 JWTVerifyOptions (clockTolerance: number-seconds or '5 minutes'; currentDate: Date); vitest fake timers for backoff-side tests
recommendations.enforcement: Vitest cases feeding tokens with nbf/exp shifted ±4 min (must pass) and ±6 min (must fail) via `currentDate`; ESLint `no-restricted-imports` allowing `jose` only inside apps/server/src/auth/** so every caller goes through the wrapper; part of the standard PR lint+test gate

pitfalls: Split-brain TLS defaults inside one Tauri app: tauri-plugin-updater v2 (reqwest 0.13) verifies via rustls-platform-verifier and works behind the proxy, while tauri-plugin-http v2 (reqwest 0.12, default feature `rustls-tls` = webpki roots) rejects the enterprise CA — 'the updater works, so TLS is fine' is a false conclusion.

reqwest cannot evaluate PAC files or authenticate to NTLM/Kerberos proxies (env-var + static registry proxy only), so Rust-side calls fail or bypass the proxy on exactly the fleets where WebView2 traffic sails through — the bug only reproduces on the customer network, never in CI without a proxy lane.

Tauri's default webviewInstallMode `downloadBootstrapper` fetches WebView2 from Microsoft's CDN during install; on an egress-filtered university network the signed installer fails in a way that looks like a corrupted or malicious package.

A Defender CI gate without -DisableRemediation is unsound: MpCmdRun returns 0 both for 'no malware' and 'malware found and successfully remediated' — Defender can quarantine your release artifact mid-pipeline while the workflow stays green.

SmartScreen reputation is partly per-file-hash, and every release build is a new hash: even a properly Artifact Signing-signed .exe shows 'Windows protected your PC' on early downloads; the durable fix on managed fleets is AppLocker/WDAC publisher rules and WDSI pre-submission, not waiting for reputation.

Putting the WebView2 user data folder or SQLite on redirected/UNC AppData: Microsoft explicitly warns of slowdowns, crashes, and data loss, and SQLite WAL's shared-memory file misbehaves on SMB — corruption presents as unrelated intermittent app bugs, and Roaming (app_data_dir) is precisely what folder redirection moves to a share.

The longPathAware manifest is inert unless the fleet enables HKLM ...\FileSystem\LongPathsEnabled=1 — an app-side-only fix silently does nothing on default-configured machines, so a short bundle identifier and shallow data trees do more than the manifest.

navigator.onLine and generic fetch success are meaningless behind intercepting proxies: captive/blocking proxies answer with HTTP 200 HTML interstitials, so a connectivity check that doesn't validate response shape reports UP while every real API call fails to parse.
